blocks.text: Imagine you bought a powerful computer but it had no operating system at all — just bare hardware: a CPU, some RAM, a disk, a keyboard, and a screen. If you wanted to run a program, you would have to load it into memory at the right address, tell the CPU where to start, manually read each key press from the keyboard controller, and write each pixel to the screen yourself. Worse, if you wanted to run two programs at once, you would have to manually decide which one uses the CPU at each microsecond and make sure they do not overwrite each other's memory. This is clearly impossible to do by hand. An Operating System (OS) is the software that sits between your programs and the hardware and does all of this for you. It is the permanent manager of the machine: it decides who runs, who waits, who gets memory, and who is allowed to touch which device. Every app you run — a browser, a game, your code — is its customer.

Think of a computer as a busy restaurant kitchen. The hardware (CPU, RAM, disk, devices) is the equipment: stoves, counters, the fridge. The programs are chefs who all want to cook at the same time. Without a manager, chefs would fight over the single stove, grab each other's ingredients, and produce chaos. The OS is the head chef / kitchen manager. It schedules who uses the stove and for how long, hands out counter space (memory), keeps one chef from stealing another's ingredients (protection), and takes orders to the equipment on everyone's behalf. The individual chefs never touch the gas line directly — they ask the manager. The four jobs of an OS Almost everything an OS does falls into four buckets. Remembering these four gives you a frame for the entire subject: Job What it manages Real-world parallel Process management Which program runs on the CPU and when Manager assigning chefs to the stove Memory management Who gets which part of RAM Allocating counter space File / storage management How data is stored and found on disk The pantry and its labelling system Device / I/O management Talking to keyboard, disk, network, screen Operating the shared equipment
The OS does P-M-F-D: Processes, Memory, Files, Devices. Mnemonic: “Please Manage Files Daily.” If an interviewer asks “what does an OS do?”, name these four and you will never blank.

Almost every OS interview opens with one of: “What is an operating system and why do we need it?” or “What problems would you face without an OS?” The strong answer is not a textbook definition — it is the story above: bare hardware is unusable, the OS is a resource manager and an abstraction layer that lets many programs share one machine safely. Mention the two roles explicitly: (1) resource manager (arbitrates CPU, memory, devices) and (2) abstraction layer (gives programs a clean API instead of raw hardware).

qas.q: What are the two primary purposes of an operating system?
qas.a: (1) Resource management — fairly and efficiently arbitrating the CPU, memory, and I/O devices among competing programs. (2) Abstraction — hiding messy hardware details behind clean interfaces (files, processes, sockets) so programmers do not deal with raw disk sectors or interrupt controllers.

qas.q: Is the OS always running?
qas.a: Where does it live? The core part (the kernel) is loaded into a protected region of RAM at boot and stays resident the entire time the machine is on. It is not 'a program that runs to completion'; it is event-driven — it springs into action on system calls, interrupts, and traps, and otherwise lets user programs run.

qas.q: Give one example of the OS acting as an abstraction.
qas.a: When you call open("file.txt"), you never specify a disk track, sector, or cylinder. The file abstraction hides the physical layout. Similarly a 'process' hides the fact that the CPU is being time-shared among many programs.

blocks.text: “The OS is just the desktop / the GUI.” No. The graphical desktop (Explorer, GNOME) is an application that runs on top of the OS. The real OS is the kernel plus its services. A server with no screen still runs a full OS.

chapters.title: OS Architecture, Kernel, Modes & System Calls

blocks.text: The kernel: the core that always runs

The kernel is the central, trusted part of the OS that has complete control over the hardware. It manages processes, memory, and devices, and it is the only code allowed to execute the most privileged CPU instructions (like directly talking to a disk controller or changing the memory map). Everything else — your browser, editor, even much of the OS's own utilities — runs outside the kernel as ordinary programs that must ask the kernel for anything dangerous.
User programs can only reach the hardware by passing a request through the kernel.
User mode vs kernel mode
Modern CPUs have (at least) two privilege levels, controlled by a single bit in a special register:
User mode Kernel mode (supervisor mode) Who runs here Normal applications The kernel
Privileges
Restricted — cannot touch hardware directly Full — every instruction allowed If it breaks
Only that one program crashes
Whole system can crash (kernel panic / BSOD) How you enter the other By making a system call / trap Returns to user mode when done

User mode vs kernel mode is like a bank. Customers (user programs) stand on the public side and can fill out forms but can never walk into the vault. To move money they hand a request slip (a system call) to a teller. The teller (kernel) is the only one allowed behind the counter and into the vault (hardware). This separation is exactly why one crashing app does not take down your whole machine. System calls: the doorway into the kernel A system call is the controlled way a user program asks the kernel to do something privileged — open a file, allocate memory, create a process, send data over the network. The program puts the request details in registers and executes a special trap instruction (e.g. syscall on x86-64). This switches the CPU into kernel mode, jumps to a fixed, trusted entry point, the kernel validates and performs the work, then switches back to user mode and returns the result.

When your C++ code does std::cout << "hi", the library eventually invokes the write() system call. Your program does not write to the screen — it asks the kernel to. Common syscall families: process (fork, exec, exit, wait), file (open, read, write, close), memory (mmap, brk), communication (pipe, socket), info (getpid, time). Kernel architectures: monolithic vs microkernel Monolithic kernel Microkernel Idea All OS services run inside the kernel Only the bare minimum in the kernel; rest are user-space servers Speed Fast (no extra boundary crossings) Slower (lots of message passing) Stability A driver bug can crash everything A failed server can be restarted; kernel survives Examples Linux, traditional Unix MINIX, QNX, (macOS/Windows are hybrids)

Monolithic = one big block (mono = one), fast but fragile. Micro = tiny kernel, everything else outside, safer but chattier. Trade-off in one line: monolithic trades safety for speed; microkernel trades speed for safety.

qas.q: What is the difference between user mode and kernel mode, and why have two modes?
qas.a: User mode is restricted; kernel mode is fully privileged. Two modes exist for protection: a buggy or malicious program in user mode cannot directly corrupt memory it does not own or issue dangerous hardware instructions — it must go through the kernel, which validates every request.

qas.q: What exactly happens during a system call?
qas.a: The program sets up arguments and executes a trap instruction. The CPU switches to kernel mode, jumps to a fixed handler, the kernel validates arguments and performs the privileged operation, then switches back to user mode and returns a value/error code. It is a controlled, one-way doorway — you cannot jump to arbitrary kernel code.

qas.q: Is a system call the same as a function call?
qas.a: No. A normal function call stays in user mode and is cheap. A system call crosses the user/kernel boundary, involves a mode switch (saving/restoring state, validation), and is significantly more expensive — which is why we batch I/O and avoid syscalls in tight loops.

qas.q: Difference between a trap and an interrupt?
qas.a: A trap is synchronous and software-generated (a system call or an exception like divide-by-zero) — caused by the running instruction. An interrupt is asynchronous and hardware-generated (a key press, disk completion, timer) — it can arrive between any two instructions.

qas.q: Is Linux a microkernel or monolithic?
qas.a: Monolithic (though modular — it can load/unload kernel modules at runtime). All core services and most drivers run in kernel space, which is why Linux is fast but a bad driver can panic the kernel.

blocks.text: The mode/syscall topic is a favourite because it connects to security and performance. Strong follow-ups to be ready for: “Why are system calls expensive?” (mode switch + cache/TLB effects), and “How would you reduce syscall overhead?” (buffering, batching, e.g. reading 64 KB at a time instead of byte by byte, or using mechanisms like epoll/io_uring). Tying OS concepts to real performance wins signals seniority.

“fork() and printf() are system calls because they are functions I call in C.” Partly wrong. printf is a library function that buffers and eventually calls the write syscall. fork is a thin wrapper over the actual kernel call. The C function and the underlying syscall are not the same layer.

chapters.title: Processes, States, the PCB & Context Switching

blocks.text: Program vs process

A program is a passive thing — a file on disk containing instructions (a.out, chrome.exe). A process is a program in execution — an active entity with its own memory, its current position in the code, open files, and CPU register values. The same program can become many processes: open three browser windows and you may have three processes from one program.

A program is a recipe written in a book. A process is the act of actually cooking that recipe right now — with real ingredients on the counter, a specific step you are on, and a particular pan in your hand. One recipe (program) can be cooked by several cooks simultaneously, each at a different step: those are separate processes. The memory layout of a process high addresses +--------------------+ | STACK | function calls, local variables (grows DOWN) | | | | v | | | | ^ | | | | | HEAP | dynamic memory: new / malloc (grows UP) +--------------------+ | BSS + DATA | global / static variables +--------------------+ | TEXT | the actual machine code (read-only) +--------------------+ low addresses Every process gets this private virtual layout. Stack and heap grow toward each other. Process states A process is not always running on the CPU — there is only so much CPU to go around. It moves through a small set of states: admitted dispatched (scheduler picks it) [ NEW ] ---------------> [ READY ] -------------------> [ RUNNING ] ^ ^ | | | | interrupt / time slice | | | +--------------------------+ | exit I/O or event done | v | [ TERMINATED ] | needs I/O / waits [ WAITING ] <-------------------------+ (a.k.a. BLOCKED) The five classic process states and the transitions between them. State Meaning New Being created; not yet admitted to the ready queue Ready Loaded, willing to run, just waiting for the CPU Running Currently executing on the CPU (only one per core at a time) Waiting / Blocked Cannot proceed until some event (e.g. disk read) completes Terminated Finished execution; about to be cleaned up

“Ready and Waiting are the same.” They are opposites in intent. A Ready process wants only the CPU — give it a core and it runs instantly. A Waiting process is stuck on something else (I/O, a lock, a signal) — even with a free CPU it cannot run yet. Confusing these is the most common process-state error in interviews. The Process Control Block (PCB) How does the OS remember everything about a paused process so it can resume it later? It stores a record per process called the Process Control Block (PCB). The PCB is the process's identity card kept by the kernel. PCB field What it holds Process ID (PID) Unique number identifying the process Process state New / ready / running / waiting / terminated Program counter Address of the next instruction to execute CPU registers Saved register values when the process is not running Memory info Page tables / base and limit registers Scheduling info Priority, pointers to scheduling queues I/O & file info List of open files, allocated devices Accounting CPU time used, time limits, owner

The PCB is like a hospital patient chart. When a doctor (CPU) is pulled away to another patient (process), the chart records the patient's current status, medications, and exactly where treatment stopped. Any doctor can later pick up the chart and continue precisely where things left off. Without the chart, you could never safely switch between patients. Context switching A context switch is the act of saving the state of the currently running process into its PCB and loading the saved state of another process so it can run. This is what makes one CPU appear to run many programs 'at the same time' — it switches between them fast enough that humans perceive simultaneity. Process A running Kernel Process B running ----------------- -------- ----------------- | timer interrupt | | |----------------------->| | | save A's registers | | | into PCB(A) | (this gap = pure | | | overhead, no useful | | load registers from | work is done here) | | PCB(B) | | | |---------------------->| | | | B runs During the switch the CPU does NO useful work — context switching is pure overhead. A context switch is not free. While switching, the CPU does no useful application work, and the new process starts with cold CPU caches and TLB, so it runs slower for a while. This overhead is exactly why we do not set the time slice in round-robin scheduling too small.

Context switch = Save A, Load B. Remember it as a relay race baton pass: the runner must hand off the baton cleanly (save state) before the next runner can go. The hand-off itself costs time even though no distance is covered.

qas.q: Difference between a program and a process?
qas.a: A program is passive code on disk; a process is an active instance of that program in execution, with its own memory (text/data/heap/stack), program counter, registers, and OS resources. One program can spawn many processes.

qas.q: What is stored in a PCB?
qas.a: PID, process state, program counter, CPU registers, memory-management info (page tables), scheduling info (priority), accounting info, and I/O status / open files. It is everything needed to pause and later resume the process exactly.

qas.q: What happens during a context switch and why is it overhead?
qas.a: The kernel saves the current process's registers/PC into its PCB and loads another process's saved state. During the switch no application work is done, and afterwards caches/TLB are cold. It is pure overhead, so frequent switching hurts throughput.

qas.q: What causes a context switch?
qas.a: A timer interrupt (time slice expired), the running process blocking on I/O or a lock, a higher-priority process becoming ready (preemption), or the process voluntarily yielding/exiting.

qas.q: What is an orphan process and a zombie process?
qas.a: A zombie has finished execution but its parent has not yet called wait() to read its exit status, so its PCB entry lingers. An orphan is a process whose parent terminated first; it gets re-parented to init/systemd, which reaps it. Zombies waste a PID slot; orphans are handled cleanly.

qas.q: What does fork() return?
qas.a: It is called once but returns twice: it returns the child's PID to the parent and 0 to the child (and -1 on failure). Both processes then continue from the same point with separate copies of memory (copy-on-write in practice).

blocks.text: Expect the chain: process states → what triggers each transition → context switch → its cost. A classic deep-dive is “walk me through what happens from the moment a timer interrupt fires until a new process is running.” Be able to narrate: interrupt → switch to kernel mode → save current PCB → scheduler picks next → load its PCB → return to user mode. Zombie/orphan and the fork() 'returns twice' question are extremely common at product companies.

A thread is the smallest unit of execution — a single sequence of instructions. A process can contain several threads that all run inside the same process and share its memory (the same heap, the same global variables, the same open files) but each has its own stack, program counter, and registers. Threads let one program do several things at once: a browser can render a page on one thread while downloading on another.

A process is a company office with shared resources: one filing cabinet (heap), one address (memory space), shared printers (files). The threads are the employees inside that office. They all share the filing cabinet and printers, but each has their own desk and notepad (stack and registers). Because they share the cabinet, they can cooperate quickly — but they can also clash if two grab the same file at once (this is the synchronization problem in the next section). PROCESS (shared: CODE, DATA, HEAP, open files) +----------------------------------------------------+ | Thread 1 Thread 2 Thread 3 | | +--------+ +--------+ +--------+ | | | stack | | stack | | stack | <- private per thread | | regs | | regs | | regs | | | | PC | | PC | | PC | | | +--------+ +--------+ +--------+ | | =========== shared heap / globals / files ======= | +----------------------------------------------------+ Threads share almost everything except their own stack, registers, and program counter. Process vs thread Aspect Process Thread Memory Own separate address space Shares the process's address space Creation cost Heavy (new memory space) Light (just a stack + registers) Communication Slow (IPC needed) Fast (shared memory directly) Crash impact One crash does not kill others A crash can take down the whole process Context switch Expensive (memory map changes) Cheaper (same address space) User-level vs kernel-level threads User-level threads Kernel-level threads Managed by A library in user space The OS kernel Kernel aware? No — kernel sees one process Yes — kernel schedules each thread Speed of switching Very fast (no kernel involvement) Slower (needs kernel) Big drawback If one thread blocks on a syscall, ALL block More overhead per thread True parallelism on multicore No (one kernel thread) Yes Real systems combine these via multithreading models: Many-to-One (many user threads onto one kernel thread — fast but no parallelism, one block stalls all), One-to-One (each user thread maps to a kernel thread — true parallelism, used by Linux and Windows), and Many-to-Many (multiplex many user threads onto a smaller pool of kernel threads — flexible).
Same heap, own stack — that one phrase answers half of all thread questions. For the blocking drawback: user threads = “one blocks, all block” because the kernel only sees one schedulable entity.

“More threads always means faster.” False. Beyond the number of CPU cores, extra threads add context-switch overhead and lock contention without adding parallelism. For CPU-bound work the sweet spot is roughly the core count; threads help most for I/O-bound work, where threads spend time waiting anyway.

qas.q: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
qas.a: A process has its own address space; threads of a process share that address space (code, data, heap, files) but each has its own stack, registers, and PC. Threads are cheaper to create and switch, and communicate via shared memory; processes are isolated and need IPC.

qas.q: Why are threads called 'lightweight'?
qas.a: Because creating a thread does not allocate a new address space — it reuses the process's. Only a stack and register set are created, and context switching between threads of the same process skips the memory-map change, so it is cheaper.

qas.q: What is the main disadvantage of user-level threads?
qas.a: The kernel is unaware of them and schedules the process as one unit. If a single thread makes a blocking system call, the whole process (all its threads) blocks, and the threads cannot run in parallel on multiple cores.

qas.q: Difference between concurrency and parallelism?
qas.a: Concurrency is dealing with many tasks by interleaving them (can happen on one core). Parallelism is literally executing tasks at the same instant on multiple cores. Concurrency is about structure; parallelism is about simultaneous execution.

qas.q: Can two threads of the same process run on two different cores?
qas.a: Yes, if they are kernel-level (one-to-one model) — that is true parallelism. Pure user-level threads cannot, because the kernel sees only one schedulable thread.

blocks.text: The process vs thread table is one of the single most-asked things in all of placement interviews — memorise it cold. Common extensions: “When would you choose multiple processes over multiple threads?” (when you need isolation/fault-tolerance, e.g. Chrome uses a process per tab so one crash does not kill the browser), and concurrency vs parallelism, which interviewers love because many candidates use the words interchangeably.

When several processes are Ready at once but there is only one CPU, the OS must choose who runs next. The part of the kernel that decides is the scheduler, and the rule it uses is a scheduling algorithm. The goals usually conflict: we want high CPU use, high throughput, low waiting time, and fairness — improving one can hurt another.

CPU scheduling is exactly a queue at a single bank counter. Many customers (processes) arrive; one teller (CPU) serves one at a time. How you order the queue is the scheduling algorithm: first-come-first-served? shortest job first? give everyone a fixed 2-minute slot then rotate (round robin)? VIPs first (priority)? Each policy trades off speed against fairness. The key metrics (learn these definitions exactly) Term Definition Formula Arrival Time (AT) When the process enters the ready queue given Burst Time (BT) CPU time the process needs given Completion Time (CT) When the process finishes from Gantt chart Turnaround Time (TAT) Total time from arrival to completion CT − AT Waiting Time (WT) Time spent waiting in the ready queue TAT − BT Response Time (RT) Arrival until first time it gets the CPU first start − AT

Two formulas unlock every numerical: TAT = CT − AT and WT = TAT − BT. Mnemonic: “Turnaround = Completion minus Arrival; Waiting = Turnaround minus Burst.” Average WT is the most commonly asked single number — compute each WT, then average. Preemptive vs non-preemptive Non-preemptive: once a process gets the CPU it keeps it until it finishes or blocks (FCFS, SJF). Preemptive: the OS can forcibly take the CPU away — because a time slice ended or a higher-priority/shorter job arrived (Round Robin, SRTF, preemptive priority). Preemption improves responsiveness but adds context-switch overhead. 1. First-Come First-Served (FCFS) Whoever arrives first runs first, to completion. Simple and fair in order, but suffers the convoy effect: one long job at the front makes everyone behind it wait a long time (like one huge trolley at the front of a checkout queue).

Processes (all arrive at t=0): P1 BT=24, P2 BT=3, P3 BT=3. Gantt: | P1 | P2 | P3 | 0 24 27 30 CT: P1=24, P2=27, P3=30. TAT = CT−AT → 24, 27, 30. WT = TAT−BT → 0, 24, 27. Average WT = (0+24+27)/3 = 17. Notice P2 and P3 are tiny yet wait ages behind P1 — that is the convoy effect. If P1 ran last, average WT would drop dramatically. 2. Shortest Job First (SJF) — non-preemptive Pick the ready process with the smallest burst time. Provably gives the minimum average waiting time — but it needs to know burst times in advance (which we usually don't) and can starve long jobs if short ones keep arriving.

All arrive t=0: P1 BT=6, P2 BT=8, P3 BT=7, P4 BT=3. Order by burst: P4, P1, P3, P2. Gantt: | P4 | P1 | P3 | P2 | 0 3 9 16 24 CT: P4=3, P1=9, P3=16, P2=24. WT = TAT−BT → P4=0, P1=3, P3=9, P2=16. Average WT = (0+3+9+16)/4 = 7. Compare with FCFS order (P1,P2,P3,P4) which would give average WT = 10.25. SJF wins. 3. Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) — preemptive SJF The preemptive version of SJF: at every arrival, if a newly arrived process has a shorter remaining burst than the current one, preempt and switch. Best average waiting time of all, but most context switches and worst starvation risk.

P1 AT=0 BT=8, P2 AT=1 BT=4, P3 AT=2 BT=9, P4 AT=3 BT=5. Gantt: |P1|P2 |P4 |P1 |P3 | 0 1 5 10 17 26 At t=1, P2 (rem 4) is shorter than P1 (rem 7) → preempt P1. At t=5 P2 done; remaining: P1=7, P3=9, P4=5 → run P4. Then P1, then P3. CT: P2=5, P4=10, P1=17, P3=26. TAT = CT−AT → P1=17, P2=4, P3=24, P4=7. WT = TAT−BT → P1=9, P2=0, P3=15, P4=2. Average WT = (9+0+15+2)/4 = 6.5. 4. Round Robin (RR) Each process gets a fixed time quantum (slice). When it expires, the process is preempted and moved to the back of the ready queue. Fair and great for responsiveness/time-sharing. The quantum size is critical: Quantum too small Quantum too large Excellent responsiveness BUT too many context switches → high overhead Low overhead BUT degrades into FCFS, poor responsiveness

P1 BT=4, P2 BT=5, P3 BT=2, all AT=0, quantum=2. Gantt: |P1|P2|P3|P1|P2|P2| 0 2 4 6 8 10 11 (P1:2)(P2:2)(P3:2done)(P1:2done)(P2:2)(P2:1done) CT: P3=6, P1=8, P2=11. TAT → P1=8, P2=11, P3=6. WT = TAT−BT → P1=4, P2=6, P3=4. Average WT = (4+6+4)/3 = 4.67. 5. Priority Scheduling Each process has a priority number; the CPU goes to the highest priority (commonly the smallest number = highest priority). Can be preemptive or not. Main danger: starvation — low-priority processes may never run. Fix: aging — gradually raise the priority of waiting processes so they eventually run.

Starvation + Aging always come as a pair. If asked 'how to fix starvation in priority scheduling?', the one-word answer is aging: the longer you wait, the higher your priority climbs. 6. Multilevel Queue (MLQ) The ready queue is split into several separate queues by process type — e.g. a high-priority queue for interactive/system processes and a low-priority queue for batch processes. Each queue can have its own algorithm, and a process stays in its assigned queue permanently. Simple, but inflexible and can starve lower queues. 7. Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) The smart, adaptive version. A process can move between queues based on behaviour. A new process starts in the top (highest-priority, smallest quantum) queue; if it uses up its whole quantum (CPU-bound) it is demoted to a lower queue with a bigger quantum; if it gives up the CPU early (I/O-bound, interactive) it stays high. Aging promotes long-waiting processes back up. This automatically favours short and interactive jobs without knowing burst times in advance — which is why real OS schedulers are based on this idea.

MLFQ is like a hospital triage that re-evaluates patients. You start in the fast lane; if you turn out to need long treatment (CPU-bound), you are moved to a slower ward so quick patients keep flowing. If you have been waiting too long anywhere, you get bumped up (aging). The system learns each patient's behaviour instead of being told in advance. Comparison of scheduling algorithms Algorithm Preemptive? Avg waiting Starvation? Best for FCFS No Can be high (convoy) No Simplicity / batch SJF No Minimum (non-preempt.) Yes (long jobs) Known short jobs SRTF Yes Lowest overall Yes Theoretical optimum Round Robin Yes Moderate, fair No Time-sharing / interactive Priority Either Depends Yes (fix: aging) Important jobs first MLFQ Yes Good, adaptive No (with aging) General-purpose OS

“SJF and FCFS give the same answer when bursts are equal.” Be careful with arrival times. Most exam mistakes come from ignoring AT: a process cannot be scheduled before it arrives, and in preemptive algorithms you must re-evaluate the queue at every arrival. Always draw the Gantt chart on a timeline, marking each arrival.

qas.q: Which scheduling algorithm gives the minimum average waiting time?
qas.a: SJF (non-preemptive) gives the provably minimum average waiting time among non-preemptive policies; SRTF (its preemptive form) gives the minimum overall. The catch is both need future burst times and can starve long jobs.

qas.q: What is the convoy effect?
qas.a: In FCFS, one long CPU-bound process at the front forces many short processes behind it to wait, inflating average waiting time — like one huge trolley holding up a checkout queue.

qas.q: How do you choose the time quantum in Round Robin?
qas.a: Balance overhead against responsiveness. Too small → excessive context switches; too large → behaves like FCFS. A common rule of thumb is a quantum large enough that ~80% of CPU bursts finish within it.

qas.q: What is starvation and how is it solved?
qas.a: Starvation is when a process waits indefinitely because higher-priority/shorter jobs keep being chosen. The standard fix is aging: increase a waiting process's priority over time so it eventually runs.

qas.q: Difference between waiting time and response time?
qas.a: Waiting time is the total time spent in the ready queue (TAT − BT). Response time is only the time from arrival until the process first gets the CPU. Interactive systems optimise response time; batch systems optimise turnaround/waiting time.

qas.q: Why is MLFQ used in real operating systems?
qas.a: It adapts to behaviour without knowing burst times: it favours short and I/O-bound (interactive) jobs by keeping them in high queues, demotes CPU hogs, and uses aging to prevent starvation — approximating SJF's benefits in practice.

blocks.text: Scheduling is the most numerically-tested OS topic. Practice computing average waiting and turnaround time for FCFS, SJF, SRTF and RR until you can draw the Gantt chart in under a minute. Interviewers also love the conceptual pair “SJF is optimal — so why don't real systems use it?” (can't know future bursts; starvation) leading into “so what do they use?” (MLFQ). Always verbalise the convoy effect, starvation, and aging by name.

Processes are isolated by design — each has its own memory, so by default they cannot see each other's data. But often they need to cooperate (a producer feeding a consumer, a shell piping one command into another). IPC is the set of mechanisms the OS provides so processes can exchange data or signals safely.

Two people in separate sealed rooms (isolated processes) need to communicate. Options: slide notes under the door one after another (a pipe); use a shared whiteboard visible through a window both can write on (shared memory); drop letters into each other's mailboxes (message queues); or talk over a phone line that can even reach another building (sockets). Mechanism How it works Notes Pipe One-way byte stream between related processes (parent/child) Used by the shell's | operator Named pipe (FIFO) Like a pipe but has a name in the filesystem Unrelated processes can use it Shared memory A region of RAM mapped into multiple processes Fastest IPC; needs synchronization Message queue Kernel-managed queue of discrete messages Structured, decoupled senders/receivers Socket Endpoint for communication, even across machines Basis of all networking

Shared memory is the fastest IPC because data is not copied through the kernel — processes read/write the same physical pages directly. The trade-off: you must add synchronization (a mutex/semaphore) to stop them clobbering each other. “Fastest but you must lock it yourself.”
The race condition — the root problem

When two threads/processes access shared data and at least one writes, the final result can depend on the exact timing of their execution. This unpredictable outcome is a race condition. The danger is that operations we think are 'one step' actually compile into several machine steps that can be interrupted halfway.

Two threads both run count = count + 1 on a shared count = 5. That line is really three steps: read count → add 1 → write back. Thread A Thread B count read count(5) 5 read count(5) 5 add -> 6 5 add -> 6 5 write count = 6 6 write count = 6 6 <- should be 7! Both read 5 before either wrote, so one increment is lost. Expected 7, got 6. This is a lost-update race condition. The Critical Section problem A critical section is the part of code that accesses shared data and must not be run by more than one thread at a time. The goal of synchronization is to enforce three requirements: Requirement Meaning Mutual Exclusion At most one process in the critical section at a time Progress If no one is inside, a process that wants in must be allowed in (no needless blocking) Bounded Waiting A process cannot be made to wait forever; there is a limit on how many others go first

The three critical-section rules are M-P-B: Mutual exclusion, Progress, Bounded waiting. Mnemonic: “My Personal Bubble.” Only one person in my bubble (ME), the door isn't pointlessly locked (progress), and you won't wait forever to enter (bounded waiting). Mutex (lock) A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) is the simplest tool: a lock you acquire before the critical section and release after. Only the holder can release it — it has an owner. If another thread tries to acquire a held mutex, it waits. Think of it as a single key to a toilet: whoever holds the key is inside; everyone else queues; you must be the one who took the key to give it back. Semaphore A semaphore is a counter with two atomic operations: wait (P / down) decrements it (and blocks if it would go below zero) and signal (V / up) increments it (waking a waiter). A binary semaphore (count 0/1) acts like a lock; a counting semaphore allows up to N processes through — useful for managing N identical resources.

A counting semaphore is the tray of keys at a hotel pool with, say, 5 keys. Each guest takes a key to enter (wait/down, count drops); when the tray is empty the 6th guest waits. Leaving the pool returns a key (signal/up, count rises), letting a waiting guest in. A binary semaphore is just a tray with one key. Mutex Semaphore Purpose Locking (one owner) Signaling + counting resources Values Locked / unlocked Any non-negative integer count Ownership Only the locker can unlock Any process can signal Use when Protect one shared resource Manage N resources or coordinate order

“Mutex and binary semaphore are identical.” Behaviourally similar, but the key difference is ownership: a mutex must be released by the same thread that acquired it, while a semaphore can be signalled by any thread. Use a mutex for locking; use a semaphore for signaling between threads (e.g., 'data is ready'). Monitor Semaphores are powerful but error-prone — forget one signal and you deadlock; misplace a wait and you get a race. A monitor is a higher-level construct (built into languages like Java's synchronized) that bundles the shared data and the procedures that operate on it, and guarantees that only one thread is active inside the monitor at a time automatically. It uses condition variables (wait/signal) to let threads sleep until a condition holds. Monitors move the locking burden from the programmer to the compiler/runtime. Classic synchronization problems Producer–Consumer (Bounded Buffer) A producer adds items to a fixed-size buffer; a consumer removes them. The producer must wait if the buffer is full; the consumer must wait if it is empty; and they must not corrupt the buffer simultaneously. Classic solution uses three semaphores: empty (counts free slots), full (counts filled slots), and a mutex for the buffer itself. PRODUCER [ buffer of N slots ] CONSUMER wait(empty) --put--> [ x | x | | | ] --take--> wait(full) wait(mutex) (full=2, empty=3) wait(mutex) ...add item... ...remove... signal(mutex) signal(mutex) signal(full) signal(empty) empty + full semaphores prevent overflow/underflow; mutex protects the buffer. Readers–Writers Many readers may read shared data simultaneously (reading does not change anything), but a writer needs exclusive access. The challenge is allowing concurrent reads while ensuring a writer gets the data alone — and avoiding starving writers (or readers, depending on the variant). Dining Philosophers Five philosophers sit around a table with five forks, one between each pair. A philosopher needs both neighbouring forks to eat. If all five grab their left fork at once, everyone holds one fork and waits forever for the right — a deadlock. It is the canonical illustration of deadlock and of solutions like: allow at most four to sit, pick up both forks atomically, or make one philosopher left-handed (break the symmetry / circular wait). P0 f4 f0 P4 P1 f3 f1 f2 P3 P2 Each Pi needs fork[i] and fork[(i+1)%5]. All grab left first -> each holds one, waits for the other -> DEADLOCK. Dining philosophers: the classic circular-wait deadlock.

The three classic problems map to three lessons: Producer-Consumer = coordinating full/empty (counting semaphores), Readers-Writers = shared vs exclusive access, Dining Philosophers = deadlock from circular wait. If you can state what each one teaches, you can reason about any synchronization question.

qas.q: What is a race condition?
qas.a: When the correctness of a result depends on the timing/interleaving of multiple threads accessing shared data, where at least one writes. The fix is to make the critical section mutually exclusive (lock it).

qas.q: Difference between a mutex and a semaphore?
qas.a: A mutex is a lock with ownership — only the acquiring thread can release it; it protects one resource. A semaphore is a signaling counter that any thread can increment; a binary semaphore mimics a lock, a counting semaphore manages N resources.

qas.q: What are the three requirements a critical-section solution must satisfy?
qas.a: Mutual exclusion (one at a time), progress (no unnecessary blocking when the section is free), and bounded waiting (no indefinite postponement).

qas.q: What is a monitor and why is it preferred over raw semaphores?
qas.a: A monitor is a high-level construct that encapsulates shared data with the procedures accessing it and automatically guarantees mutual exclusion, using condition variables for waiting. It is less error-prone because the programmer does not manually pair wait/signal calls.

qas.q: In producer–consumer, why do you need both a mutex and the empty/full semaphores?
qas.a: The empty/full counting semaphores handle the buffer-full and buffer-empty conditions (blocking the producer/consumer appropriately). The mutex separately guarantees that only one of them modifies the buffer's internal pointers at a time. They solve different problems.

qas.q: What is busy waiting / spinlock, and when is it acceptable?
qas.a: Busy waiting is repeatedly checking a condition in a loop instead of sleeping, wasting CPU. A spinlock is a lock based on busy waiting. It is acceptable only when the wait is expected to be extremely short and on multiprocessors, where sleeping/waking would cost more than spinning.

blocks.text: Synchronization questions test whether you understand why things break, not just definitions. Be ready to: trace a lost-update race step by step (as in the example above), state the mutex vs semaphore ownership distinction crisply, and explain at least one solution to dining philosophers by linking it to breaking a deadlock condition. The producer–consumer 'why both mutex and semaphores' question is a common filter for strong candidates.

A deadlock is a situation where a set of processes are all stuck forever, each waiting for a resource that another process in the set is holding. No one can proceed, and no one will give up what they hold. The system makes zero progress on those processes.

Picture a narrow one-lane bridge with cars entering from both ends. Each car advances onto the bridge (holds part of it) and waits for the other side to clear — but neither will reverse. Both are stuck forever. That is a deadlock: everyone holds something and waits for something held by another, in a cycle. The four necessary (Coffman) conditions A deadlock can occur only if all four of these hold at once. Break any one and deadlock becomes impossible — this is the basis of every prevention strategy: Condition Meaning 1. Mutual Exclusion At least one resource is held in non-shareable mode (only one user at a time) 2. Hold and Wait A process holds at least one resource while waiting to acquire more 3. No Preemption A resource cannot be forcibly taken; it is released only voluntarily 4. Circular Wait A closed chain of processes exists, each waiting for a resource the next one holds

Four conditions = “Mutual Hold No Circle” → Mutual exclusion, Hold and wait, No preemption, Circular wait. All four must be true together — so deadlock prevention = make sure at least one is always false. Strategies for handling deadlocks Strategy Idea Cost / drawback Prevention Design so one of the four conditions can never hold Low resource utilization, restrictive Avoidance Allow conditions, but only grant requests that keep the system in a 'safe state' (Banker's) Needs max needs known in advance; overhead Detection + Recovery Let deadlocks happen, detect via wait-for graph, then recover Recovery is disruptive (kill/rollback) Ignore (Ostrich) Pretend it cannot happen; reboot if it does Used by most general OSes (rare in practice) Prevention — attack each condition  Mutual exclusion: make resources shareable where possible (e.g. read-only files). Often unavoidable.  Hold and wait: require a process to request all resources at once up front, or hold none while requesting. Wastes resources.  No preemption: if a process holding resources requests one that is unavailable, force it to release everything and try later.  Circular wait: impose a global ordering on resources and require processes to request them in increasing order. This is the most practical prevention method. Deadlock Avoidance — Banker's Algorithm The Banker's Algorithm treats resource allocation like a cautious banker lending money: it only grants a request if, after granting it, there is still some order in which all processes can finish (a safe sequence). If no such order exists, the state is 'unsafe' and the request is denied even if resources are free. Key matrices: Max (maximum a process may ever need), Allocation (currently held), Need = Max − Allocation, and Available (free resources).

5 processes (P0–P4), 3 resource types A,B,C with total instances A=10, B=5, C=7. Process Alloc (A B C) Max (A B C) Need = Max−Alloc P0 0 1 0 7 5 3 7 4 3 P1 2 0 0 3 2 2 1 2 2 P2 3 0 2 9 0 2 6 0 0 P3 2 1 1 2 2 2 0 1 1 P4 0 0 2 4 3 3 4 3 1 Total allocated = (7 2 5). Available = Total − Allocated = (10 5 7) − (7 2 5) = (3 3 2). Find a safe sequence (a process can run if its Need ≤ Available; then it frees its Allocation): Available = (3 3 2) P1 Need(1 2 2) <= (3 3 2)? YES -> run, free (2 0 0) -> Avail = (5 3 2) P3 Need(0 1 1) <= (5 3 2)? YES -> run, free (2 1 1) -> Avail = (7 4 3) P4 Need(4 3 1) <= (7 4 3)? YES -> run, free (0 0 2) -> Avail = (7 4 5) P0 Need(7 4 3) <= (7 4 5)? YES -> run, free (0 1 0) -> Avail = (7 5 5) P2 Need(6 0 0) <= (7 5 5)? YES -> run, free (3 0 2) -> Avail = (10 5 7) Safe sequence: P1 → P3 → P4 → P0 → P2. The state is safe, so the system can grant requests as long as it stays safe. (Other valid sequences may also exist.) Detection & Recovery If we neither prevent nor avoid, we detect: build a wait-for graph (an edge P→Q means P is waiting for a resource held by Q). A cycle in this graph means deadlock (for single-instance resources). Recovery options: kill processes one by one until the cycle breaks, or preempt resources and roll back a process to a safe checkpoint. Both lose work, so detection runs periodically rather than constantly.

“A safe state means no deadlock can ever happen; an unsafe state means deadlock.” Subtle: a safe state guarantees no deadlock. But an unsafe state is not the same as a deadlock — it merely means deadlock might occur depending on future requests. Banker's refuses to enter unsafe states as a precaution.

“Deadlock and starvation are the same.” No. In a deadlock, the involved processes are blocked forever and make zero progress. In starvation, a process is indefinitely delayed because others keep being preferred — but the system as a whole is still making progress, and the starved process could eventually run.

qas.q: What are the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
qas.a: Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait. All four must hold simultaneously, so removing any one prevents deadlock.

qas.q: Difference between deadlock prevention and avoidance?
qas.a: Prevention structurally ensures one of the four conditions can never hold (e.g. resource ordering to kill circular wait). Avoidance allows all conditions but uses runtime knowledge of maximum needs to only grant requests that keep the system in a safe state (Banker's Algorithm).

qas.q: What is a safe state?
qas.a: A state in which there exists at least one sequence of all processes such that each can obtain its maximum needed resources, run to completion, and release them — guaranteeing no deadlock.

qas.q: How do you detect deadlock?
qas.a: For single-instance resources, build a wait-for graph and look for a cycle. For multi-instance resources, use a detection algorithm similar to the Banker's safety check.

qas.q: Which deadlock condition is easiest to attack in practice, and how?
qas.a: Circular wait — impose a total ordering on resource types and require every process to request resources only in increasing order. This is simple and widely used (e.g. lock ordering conventions in code).

qas.q: Why do most general-purpose OSes 'ignore' deadlocks?
qas.a: Because deadlocks are rare in practice and the overhead of full prevention/avoidance/detection is high. They use the 'ostrich algorithm' — accept the rare risk and let the user reboot/kill if it happens (a cost-benefit decision).

blocks.text: Deadlock is a guaranteed interview topic. Memorise the four conditions and pair each with how prevention attacks it. Be ready to compute a safe sequence with the Banker's Algorithm on a small matrix — practice the Need = Max − Alloc step. The two high-value conceptual distinctions are deadlock vs starvation and safe vs unsafe vs deadlocked state. Mentioning real-world lock ordering as the circular-wait fix shows practical maturity.

RAM is limited and shared by many processes. Memory management answers: how do we give each process the memory it needs, keep processes from reading/corrupting each other, and run programs that are larger than physical RAM? The central idea is virtual memory: every process believes it has its own large, private, contiguous memory, while the OS quietly maps those virtual addresses onto whatever physical RAM (and disk) is actually available.

Virtual addresses are like seat numbers on a movie ticket. Your ticket says “Seat A5,” a clean simple label (virtual address). The cinema's seating chart (page table) maps “A5” to an actual physical chair somewhere in the hall. You never need to know the real location — and the cinema can rearrange physical chairs without reprinting your ticket. The OS does the same mapping for memory. Paging Paging splits a process's virtual memory into fixed-size blocks called pages, and physical RAM into equal-size blocks called frames (a typical size is 4 KB). Any page can go into any free frame — they need not be contiguous. A per-process page table records which frame holds each page. This eliminates external fragmentation entirely (any free frame fits any page). Virtual address space Page Table Physical RAM (frames) +---------+ page -> frame +---------+ frame 0 | page 0 | ----------------+ 0 -> 3 ----+ | page 2 | frame 1 | page 1 | --------------+ | 1 -> 6 | | | frame 2 | page 2 | ------------+ | | 2 -> 1 ----|---->| page 0 | frame 3 | page 3 | ----------+ | | | 3 -> 0 | | | ... +---------+ | | | | +---->| page 1 | frame 6 +-|-|-|--> scattered, not contiguous Pages map to scattered frames via the page table — no external fragmentation. A virtual address is split into a page number (which page) and an offset (how far into the page). The page number indexes the page table to get the frame number; the offset is added unchanged. Internal fragmentation can still occur (the last page may be partly empty), but it is bounded by the page size. Segmentation Segmentation divides memory by logical units of variable size that match the program's structure: a code segment, a data segment, a stack segment, etc. Each segment has a base and a limit. It matches how programmers think, and protection can be per-segment — but variable sizes cause external fragmentation. Paging Segmentation Block size Fixed (e.g. 4 KB pages) Variable (logical units) Divided by The OS, mechanically The program's logical structure Fragmentation Internal (small, bounded) External Address page number + offset segment number + offset Visible to programmer? No Yes (code/data/stack segments)

Paging = fixed size → internal fragmentation. Segmentation = variable size → external fragmentation. One-liner: “Paging is Physical/fixed; Segmentation is Structural/variable.” Modern x86 uses paging on top of (mostly flat) segmentation. Virtual memory & demand paging Virtual memory lets a process run even if it does not fully fit in RAM, by keeping only the needed pages in RAM and the rest on disk (the swap area). Demand paging means a page is loaded into RAM only when it is actually accessed — not preloaded. When a process touches a page that is not in RAM, the hardware raises a page fault. CPU accesses page X | v Is X in RAM? --YES--> use it (fast) | NO (PAGE FAULT - a trap to the OS) | v OS finds X on disk -> picks a free frame (or evicts one via page-replacement) -> loads X into RAM -> updates page table -> restarts the instruction A page fault is not an error — it is the normal mechanism of demand paging. Thrashing If too many processes run with too little RAM, the system spends almost all its time swapping pages in and out of disk rather than doing useful work. CPU utilization collapses. This is thrashing. It happens when the combined 'working sets' (the pages each process actively needs) exceed physical memory. Fixes: reduce the degree of multiprogramming (suspend some processes), or add RAM; the working-set model and page-fault-frequency schemes detect and prevent it.

Thrashing is like trying to study from 20 textbooks on a tiny desk that fits only 2. Every time you need a different book you must put one away and fetch another from the shelf. You spend all your time shuffling books and almost none actually reading. The fix is fewer books at once (less multiprogramming) or a bigger desk (more RAM). The TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) Every memory access needs a virtual→physical translation via the page table — but the page table is itself in RAM, so a naive design needs two memory accesses per access (one for the table, one for the data). The TLB is a small, very fast hardware cache that stores recent page→frame translations. A TLB hit skips the page-table lookup entirely. Because programs exhibit locality, hit rates are typically very high (often >95%).

Suppose TLB access = 10 ns, RAM access = 100 ns, TLB hit ratio = 90%. Hit: 10 (TLB) + 100 (data) = 110 ns. Miss: 10 (TLB) + 100 (page table) + 100 (data) = 210 ns. Effective access time = 0.9×110 + 0.1×210 = 99 + 21 = 120 ns. The TLB turns a worst case of 210 ns into an average of 120 ns.

On a page fault, if no frame is free, the OS must evict an existing page to make room. The page-replacement algorithm decides which victim to evict. A good choice minimises future page faults. We measure quality by counting faults for a given reference string and number of frames.
FIFO (First-In First-Out)
Evict the page that has been in memory longest. Simple, but can evict a heavily-used page just because it arrived early. Suffers Belady's anomaly: more frames can sometimes cause more faults.

Reference string: 7 0 1 2 0 3 0 4 2 3, with 3 frames. Ref: 7 0 1 2 0 3 0 4 2 3 7 7 7 2 2 2 0 0 0 3 0 0 0 0 3 3 3 2 2 1 1 1 1 1 4 4 4 F F F F . F F F F F (F = fault, . = hit) FIFO faults = 9 (only the '0' at position 5 is a hit). LRU (Least Recently Used) Evict the page that has not been used for the longest time — a good approximation of optimal based on the assumption that recently-used pages will be used again (locality). Needs hardware support (timestamps or a stack) and never suffers Belady's anomaly.

Same string 7 0 1 2 0 3 0 4 2 3, 3 frames. Ref: 7 0 1 2 0 3 0 4 2 3 7 7 7 2 2 2 2 4 4 4 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 3 1 1 1 3 3 3 2 2 F F F F . F . F F F LRU faults = 8. Better than FIFO here because it keeps the frequently used '0'. Optimal (OPT / MIN) Evict the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future. This gives the theoretical minimum number of faults — but it requires knowing the future, so it is impossible to implement; it is used only as a benchmark to judge real algorithms.

Same string, 3 frames. Ref: 7 0 1 2 0 3 0 4 2 3 7 7 7 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 0 0 0 0 0 0 4 4 3 1 1 1 3 3 3 3 3 F F F F . F . F . F Optimal faults = 7 — the lower bound. So for this string: OPT(7) ≤ LRU(8) ≤ FIFO(9), exactly the expected ordering. Clock (Second-Chance) A practical approximation of LRU. Pages sit in a circular list, each with a reference bit. A 'hand' sweeps around: if the bit is 1, give a second chance (clear the bit to 0, move on); if it is 0, evict that page. It captures most of LRU's benefit with far less hardware cost, which is why real systems use variants of it.

“More frames always means fewer page faults.” True for LRU and Optimal (they are 'stack algorithms'), but false for FIFO — this is Belady's anomaly, where increasing frames can increase faults. A classic interview gotcha; name the anomaly and say it occurs with FIFO.

Fault ordering you can rely on for the same input: Optimal ≤ LRU ≤ FIFO (usually). Remember LRU looks at the past, Optimal looks at the future, and Optimal is only a yardstick because the future is unknown.

qas.q: Difference between paging and segmentation?
qas.a: Paging uses fixed-size blocks decided by the OS and causes only (bounded) internal fragmentation; segmentation uses variable-size logical units matching program structure and causes external fragmentation. Paging is invisible to the programmer; segments are visible.

qas.q: What is a page fault?
qas.a: Is it an error? A page fault is a trap raised when a process accesses a page not currently in RAM. It is not an error in demand paging — it is the normal trigger for the OS to load the page from disk and resume the instruction.

qas.q: What is thrashing and how do you fix it?
qas.a: Thrashing is when the system spends most of its time swapping pages instead of executing, because the combined working sets exceed RAM. Fix by reducing the degree of multiprogramming (suspend processes), using the working-set model, or adding RAM.

qas.q: What is the purpose of the TLB?
qas.a: It is a fast hardware cache of recent virtual-to-physical translations, avoiding a slow page-table lookup in RAM on every memory access. High hit rates (due to locality) make address translation nearly free.

qas.q: What is Belady's anomaly?
qas.a: A counterintuitive situation where increasing the number of frames increases the number of page faults. It can occur with FIFO replacement but not with stack algorithms like LRU or Optimal.

qas.q: Why can't we implement the Optimal page-replacement algorithm?
qas.a: It requires knowing the future reference string to evict the page used farthest in the future. Since the OS cannot see the future, Optimal is only used as a benchmark to evaluate real algorithms like LRU.

qas.q: What is the difference between internal and external fragmentation?
qas.a: Internal fragmentation is wasted space inside an allocated block (e.g. the unused part of the last page). External fragmentation is free memory split into small non-contiguous pieces that cannot satisfy a large request despite enough total free space.

blocks.text: Memory is heavily tested both conceptually and numerically. You must be able to compute page faults for FIFO, LRU and Optimal given a reference string and frame count, and to calculate effective access time with a TLB hit ratio. The conceptual must-knows: paging vs segmentation, internal vs external fragmentation, thrashing/working set, and Belady's anomaly. Connecting the TLB and page faults to real performance (locality, caches) earns extra credit.

A disk is just a huge array of numbered blocks. A file system is the OS layer that turns that raw array into something humans use: named files, folders, permissions, and reliable storage. It answers: where are a file's blocks, how do we find a file by name, and how do we not lose data if power is cut mid-write?
Directory structures

Directories map human-readable names to files. They evolved through: single-level (one big list, name clashes), two-level (one directory per user), tree (the familiar nested folders), and acyclic-graph (allows sharing a file/folder under multiple names via links, without cycles).
Inodes

In Unix/Linux file systems, every file is described by an inode (index node) — a structure holding the file's metadata and the pointers to its data blocks, but not its name. Metadata includes size, owner, permissions, timestamps, link count, and block pointers. The name lives in the directory, which maps a name to an inode number. This separation is why a single file can have multiple names (hard links). Directory entry Inode (number 1247) Data blocks

+----------------+         +--------------------+        +----------+
  | name: report   | ----->  | size, owner, perms |  --->  | block 12 |
  | inode: 1247    |         | timestamps         |  --->  | block 87 |
  +----------------+         | link count         |  --->  | block 33 |
                             | -> direct pointers |        +----------+
                             | -> indirect ptr    | -> (points to a block of pointers)
                             +--------------------+
 The directory holds the NAME; the inode holds metadata + block pointers (not the name).

The inode holds everything about a file EXCEPT its name. The name lives in the directory. That single fact explains hard links (many names → one inode) and why renaming/moving within a filesystem is cheap (just edit a directory entry). File allocation methods Method How blocks are tracked Trade-off Contiguous File occupies consecutive blocks; store start + length Fast sequential + random access; external fragmentation, hard to grow Linked Each block points to the next No external fragmentation; slow random access, pointer overhead Indexed An index block lists all the file's block addresses Fast random access; index block overhead (basis of inodes) Journaling If power is lost while the OS is half-way through updating a file (data written but metadata not, say), the file system can be left inconsistent. A journaling file system (ext4, NTFS) first writes the intended changes to a small log (the journal); only after the log entry is safely on disk does it apply the real change, then clears the log entry. After a crash, the OS replays the journal to restore consistency quickly — no slow full-disk scan needed.

Journaling is like writing your plan in a notebook before doing each step of a recipe. If the kitchen loses power mid-step, you reopen the notebook, see exactly which step was in progress, and either finish or redo just that step — instead of throwing out the whole dish and starting over.

qas.q: What is an inode and what does it store?
qas.a: An inode is the metadata structure for a file in Unix-like systems: size, owner, permissions, timestamps, link count, and pointers to the file's data blocks. It does NOT store the file's name — the name is in the directory, which maps names to inode numbers.

qas.q: Difference between a hard link and a soft (symbolic) link?
qas.a: A hard link is another directory entry pointing to the same inode — both names are equal and the file's data survives until all hard links are removed. A soft link is a separate file containing a path to the target; it breaks if the target is deleted and can cross filesystems.

qas.q: What problem does journaling solve?
qas.a: Filesystem inconsistency after a crash or power loss during an update. By logging intended changes before applying them, the OS can replay or roll back the journal on reboot, restoring consistency without a lengthy full check.

qas.q: Compare contiguous, linked, and indexed allocation.
qas.a: Contiguous: fast access but external fragmentation and hard to grow. Linked: no fragmentation but poor random access. Indexed: an index block enables fast random access at the cost of index overhead — it is the basis of the inode design.

blocks.text: File systems are tested more lightly than scheduling/memory but two questions recur: “what is an inode / hard vs soft link” and “what is journaling and why”. Knowing that the name lives in the directory (not the inode) is the detail that separates a memorised answer from an understood one.
A full Linux/command-line book is Book 5 of this series; here is the OS-relevant core that interviewers expect every candidate to know.
File system hierarchy

Path Purpose / Root of everything /bin, /usr/bin Essential user commands / binaries /etc System-wide configuration files /home Users' home directories /var Variable data: logs, mail, spool /tmp Temporary files (cleared on reboot) /proc Virtual filesystem exposing kernel/process info /dev Device files
Permissions

Each file has permissions for user / group / others, each with read(4) / write(2) / execute(1). They are commonly written as a 3-digit octal number: chmod 755 file means rwx for the owner (7=4+2+1), r-x for group (5=4+1), r-x for others. 644 = owner read/write, everyone else read-only.

Permission digits: r=4, w=2, x=1, added per category. 755 = “owner can do everything, others can read+run”; 644 = “owner read/write, others read.” These two cover most real cases. Commands interviewers expect Command What it does ls, cd, pwd, mkdir, rm, cp, mv Navigate and manage files/directories cat, less, head, tail View file contents (tail -f follows a live log) grep Search text by pattern (grep -r searches recursively) find Search the filesystem by name, size, time, type sed Stream editor: find-and-replace / transform text awk Field-based text processing (columns, sums) chmod / chown Change permissions / ownership ps, top, htop View running processes and resource usage kill, kill -9 Send a signal to a process (TERM, then KILL) netstat / ss Show network connections and listening ports curl / wget Make HTTP requests / download files ssh Securely log in to a remote machine df / du Disk free space / directory disk usage Signals A signal is a software interrupt sent to a process. Common ones: SIGINT (Ctrl+C) politely interrupt, SIGTERM ask to terminate (can be caught/cleaned up), SIGKILL (kill -9) force kill (cannot be caught or ignored), SIGSTOP pause. Best practice: try SIGTERM first so the program can clean up; use SIGKILL only if it refuses to die. Pipes and redirection

cat access.log | grep "404" | wc -l — count 404 errors: cat streams the file, grep keeps only 404 lines, wc -l counts them. The | connects one command's output to the next's input. command > out.txt redirects output to a file (overwrite); >> appends; 2> redirects errors.

qas.q: What is the difference between kill and kill -9?
qas.a: Plain kill sends SIGTERM, a polite request to terminate that the process can catch to clean up. kill -9 sends SIGKILL, which the kernel enforces immediately — it cannot be caught or ignored, so no cleanup happens. Prefer SIGTERM first.

qas.q: How do you find which process is using a port?
qas.a: Use ss -ltnp (or netstat -tulpn), or lsof -i :PORT. These list listening sockets and the owning process IDs.

qas.q: What does chmod 755 mean?
qas.a: Owner gets read+write+execute (7), group and others get read+execute (5). It is the typical permission for executable scripts and directories.

qas.q: What is the difference between a process shown in 'ps' as a zombie vs running?
qas.a: A running/sleeping process is active or waiting; a zombie (state Z) has finished but its parent has not reaped its exit status, so only its PCB entry remains until the parent calls wait().

qas.q: How would you search all .log files under /var for the word ERROR?
qas.a: grep -r "ERROR" /var/*.log, or combine find and grep: find /var -name '*.log' -exec grep ERROR {} +. This shows you can compose tools with pipes/exec.

blocks.text: For SDE roles, Linux questions are practical: navigating logs, finding a process on a port, understanding permissions, and the SIGTERM-vs-SIGKILL distinction. Be ready to compose commands with pipes (the log-counting example) — interviewers care that you can chain grep/awk/sed/find, not that you memorised flags. CHAPTER 1B Interview Question Bank Curated, high-yield OS questions most likely to be asked in SDE rounds This bank collects the questions that recur most across product and service companies. Each section above already embedded its own questions; below are additional rapid-fire and cross-cutting ones. Drill them by covering the answer and saying it aloud. Quality over count: master these well rather than skimming hundreds of near-duplicates. Processes, Threads & Scheduling

qas.q: Why does a context switch hurt performance beyond the save/restore cost?
qas.a: After switching, the new process runs with cold CPU caches and a cold TLB, so its first instructions are slower until those refill. The direct cost is saving/loading state; the indirect cost is lost cache/TLB locality.

qas.q: Can a process have zero threads?
qas.a: No. A process always has at least one thread of execution (the main thread). Threads are what actually run; the process is the container of resources.

qas.q: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling?
qas.a: Preemptive scheduling can forcibly take the CPU from a running process (on a timer tick or a higher-priority arrival); non-preemptive lets it run until it finishes or blocks. Preemption improves responsiveness but adds context-switch overhead.

qas.q: What is a daemon process?
qas.a: A long-running background process not attached to a terminal, usually started at boot, providing a service (e.g. sshd, cron). Names often end in 'd'.

qas.q: What is the difference between multiprogramming, multitasking, and multiprocessing?
qas.a: Multiprogramming keeps several jobs in memory to keep the CPU busy. Multitasking (time-sharing) rapidly switches so users feel concurrency. Multiprocessing uses multiple physical CPUs/cores for true parallel execution.

qas.q: What is CPU-bound vs I/O-bound?
qas.a: A CPU-bound process spends most time computing; an I/O-bound process spends most time waiting on I/O. Schedulers favour I/O-bound processes for responsiveness; thread counts above core count help I/O-bound but not CPU-bound work. Synchronization & Deadlock

qas.q: What is priority inversion?
qas.a: A high-priority task is blocked waiting for a lock held by a low-priority task, which itself is preempted by medium-priority tasks — so the high-priority task waits on lower ones. Fixed by priority inheritance (the lock holder temporarily inherits the waiter's high priority).

qas.q: Is it possible to have a deadlock with a single resource and single process?
qas.a: No. Deadlock requires circular wait among at least two processes (each holding what the other needs). One process cannot deadlock on a single resource by itself.

qas.q: What is a livelock?
qas.a: Processes are not blocked but keep changing state in response to each other without making progress — like two people repeatedly stepping aside in a corridor. Unlike deadlock, they are active, yet nothing useful happens.

qas.q: Why is busy waiting bad and when is it acceptable?
qas.a: It wastes CPU cycles spinning instead of sleeping. Acceptable only when the expected wait is shorter than the cost of sleeping/waking and on multiprocessors (spinlocks in kernels).

qas.q: Can mutual exclusion alone cause a deadlock?
qas.a: No single condition causes deadlock; all four Coffman conditions must hold together. Mutual exclusion is necessary but not sufficient. Memory & Virtual Memory

qas.q: What is the difference between logical (virtual) and physical address?
qas.a: A logical address is generated by the CPU/program; a physical address is the actual RAM location. The MMU translates logical to physical at runtime using the page table; the program never sees physical addresses.

qas.q: What is demand paging vs prepaging?
qas.a: Demand paging loads a page only when it is first accessed (lazy). Prepaging proactively loads pages expected to be needed soon, reducing fault count if the guess is right, wasting I/O if wrong.

qas.q: What is the working set of a process?
qas.a: The set of pages a process is actively using in a recent time window. If the sum of all working sets exceeds RAM, thrashing occurs. The working-set model keeps a process's needed pages resident.

qas.q: What is copy-on-write (COW)?
qas.a: After fork(), parent and child share the same physical pages marked read-only; a page is physically copied only when one of them writes to it. This makes fork() fast and memory-efficient since most pages are never modified.

qas.q: Where is the page table stored, and what speeds it up?
qas.a: In main memory (per process), pointed to by a register (CR3 on x86). The TLB caches recent translations to avoid a memory access for the table on every reference.

qas.q: What is a segmentation fault?
qas.a: A fault raised when a process accesses memory it is not allowed to (e.g. dereferencing a null/dangling pointer or writing read-only memory). The MMU detects the invalid access and the OS sends SIGSEGV. Files, Boot & Misc

qas.q: Walk through what happens when a computer boots.
qas.a: Firmware (BIOS/UEFI) runs power-on self-test, then loads the bootloader (e.g. GRUB) from disk. The bootloader loads the kernel into memory; the kernel initialises hardware, mounts the root filesystem, and starts the first user process (init/systemd), which starts all other services.

qas.q: What is the difference between a library function and a system call again?
qas.a: A library function runs in user space and may or may not invoke a system call; a system call crosses into kernel mode for a privileged operation. printf (library) buffers and eventually calls write (syscall).

qas.q: What is virtualization / a virtual machine, briefly?
qas.a: Software (a hypervisor) presents virtual hardware so multiple guest OSes can run on one physical machine, each isolated. Type-1 hypervisors run on bare metal; type-2 run atop a host OS.

qas.q: What is the difference between a process and a program one more time, in one line?
qas.a: A program is passive code on disk; a process is that program actively executing with its own memory and state.

qas.q: What is spooling?
qas.a: Simultaneous Peripheral Operation On-Line: buffering data for a slow device (like a printer) in a queue on disk so the CPU/processes are not held up waiting for it.
