What thrashing is and how to fix it? End of Book 1 — Operating Systems. Next in the series: DBMS, Computer Networks, OOP, and Linux + Software Engineering. The most effective way to use this book is to read a section, shut it, and explain the idea aloud — then test yourself on the orange and teal boxes. Reading is recognition; speaking is recall, and interviews test recall.

blocks.text: Imagine storing all your data in plain files — a students.txt, an orders.txt. Two programs writing to the same file at once can corrupt it. A power cut mid-write can leave a half-written, inconsistent record. Finding 'all orders above Rs.500' means scanning the entire file line by line. Changing one program's idea of the file's layout breaks every other program reading it. This is the file-processing approach, and it does not scale. A Database Management System (DBMS) is software that manages data so that many programs and users can store, query, update, and rely on it safely and efficiently, without each program having to reinvent storage, concurrency control, and crash recovery from scratch.

A pile of loose paper files in a cupboard is the file-processing approach: anyone can grab a folder, scribble on it, and put it back wherever — duplicate folders appear, two people edit the same folder at once and one person's edit vanishes, and there is no single rulebook on what a 'valid' folder looks like. A DBMS is a professional records office with one librarian controlling all access: requests go through a desk (the DBMS), which enforces rules on what counts as valid data, lets multiple people query at once without conflicts, and keeps a logbook so that even if the lights go out mid-update, the records can be restored consistently. Problem with raw files How a DBMS solves it Data redundancy & inconsistency Centralised, normalized storage — one source of truth Difficult concurrent access Concurrency control (locking / MVCC) for safe simultaneous use No atomicity on crash Transactions + logging/recovery (ACID) Hard to enforce rules Constraints: primary key, foreign key, NOT NULL, CHECK Application tightly coupled to file layout Data independence via schemas/views Ad-hoc querying is painful A declarative query language (SQL) The three-schema architecture To let the physical storage change without breaking applications, DBMSs separate concerns into three levels: +-------------------------------------------------------+ | EXTERNAL / VIEW LEVEL (what each user/app sees) | | e.g. a "StudentView" showing only name + course | +---------------------------|-----------------------------+ | view mapping +---------------------------v-----------------------------+ | CONCEPTUAL / LOGICAL LEVEL (the overall schema) | | tables, columns, relationships, constraints | +---------------------------|-----------------------------+ | logical/physical mapping +---------------------------v-----------------------------+ | INTERNAL / PHYSICAL LEVEL (how it's actually stored) | | file organisation, indexes, block layout on disk | +-------------------------------------------------------+ Changes at one level (e.g. adding an index) need not ripple up to the level above. Data independence is the payoff of this separation. Physical data independence means you can change storage/indexing (internal level) without touching the logical schema or applications. Logical data independence means you can change the logical schema (e.g. add a column) without breaking every view/application that does not use that column.
Three levels, top to bottom: View → Logical → Physical. Mnemonic: “VLP — Very Layered Protection.” Each layer shields the one above from changes below.

qas.q: Why use a DBMS instead of flat files?
qas.a: Flat files have redundancy, no concurrency control, no crash recovery, and no enforced rules. A DBMS centralises data, enforces constraints, supports concurrent transactions safely, and recovers cleanly from crashes — all via a declarative query interface.

qas.q: What is data independence and why does it matter?
qas.a: The ability to change one schema level without forcing changes in the levels above it. Physical independence lets you re-index or reorganise storage without touching applications; logical independence lets the conceptual schema evolve without breaking every view built on it. It is what makes long-lived systems maintainable.

qas.q: What is the difference between DDL, DML, DCL and TCL?
qas.a: DDL (CREATE/ALTER/DROP) defines schema. DML (SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) manipulates data. DCL (GRANT/REVOKE) controls access/permissions. TCL (COMMIT/ROLLBACK/SAVEPOINT) controls transactions.

blocks.text: Interviewers rarely linger on “what is a DBMS” for long, but they use it as a warm-up to judge communication. Answer in under 60 seconds: name the file-processing problems, then say a DBMS centralises data and adds constraints, concurrency control, and crash recovery via SQL. Then they move to ER modeling or normalization — be ready to pivot immediately.

Before building tables, we design conceptually using an Entity-Relationship (ER) model — a diagram-friendly way to capture what real-world things matter (entities), what describes them (attributes), and how they relate (relationships), before worrying about SQL syntax. Term Meaning

Entity A real-world object/concept worth storing Student, Course, Employee Attribute A property describing an entity name, rollNo, dob Entity Set A collection of similar entities All Student rows Relationship An association between entities Student ENROLLS_IN Course Key Attribute Uniquely identifies an entity instance rollNo Types of attributes  Simple — cannot be divided further (age).  Composite — can be split into parts (name → firstName + lastName).  Single-valued — one value (dob); Multi-valued — several possible (phoneNumbers).  Derived — computed from another attribute (age derived from dob); not stored directly. Cardinality (relationship ratios) Cardinality describes how many instances of one entity can relate to instances of another: Type Meaning

One-to-One (1:1) Each entity on either side relates to at most one on the other Person ↔ Passport One-to-Many (1:N) One entity relates to many on the other side Department → Employees Many-to-Many (M:N) Either side can relate to many on the other Students ↔ Courses (enrolment) ONE-TO-MANY: MANY-TO-MANY: [Department] 1 ----< N [Employee] [Student] N >----< M [Course] \ / (via a junction table: Enrollment(studentId, courseId)) M:N relationships need a junction/bridge table when mapped to the relational model.

Cardinality is exactly like describing relationships between people and phone numbers. Old-school landline = 1:1 (one house, one number). A person owning many SIM cards = 1:N from person to numbers. Group WhatsApp chats where many people are in many groups = M:N — and notice you need a separate 'membership' list (junction table) to record who is in which group, because neither side alone can store it. Strong vs weak entities A strong entity has its own key attribute and exists independently (Student). A weak entity has no key of its own and depends on a strong entity for identification — e.g. Dependent (a person's family member for insurance) only makes sense tied to an Employee; its 'partial key' (name) plus the employee's key together identify it. Shown in ER diagrams with a double-bordered box.

“ER diagrams and the relational schema are the same thing.” No — ER modeling is a conceptual design step; converting it into actual tables (especially handling M:N relationships and weak entities) is a separate mapping process. Interviewers sometimes ask you to do this mapping live, so practice turning an ER diagram into CREATE TABLE statements.

qas.q: How do you map a many-to-many relationship into relational tables?
qas.a: Create a separate junction (bridge) table containing the primary keys of both entities as foreign keys, together forming its composite primary key. E.g. Enrollment(studentId, courseId) for Student↔Course, with FKs to both.

qas.q: What is a weak entity and how is it represented in tables?
qas.a: An entity without its own full key, existing only in relation to a strong (owner) entity. Its table includes the owner entity's primary key as part of its own primary key (a composite of the owner's key + the weak entity's partial key).

qas.q: Difference between a derived and a stored attribute?
qas.a: A stored attribute holds an actual value in the table (dob). A derived attribute is computed on the fly from other attributes (age from dob) and is typically not stored, to avoid it going stale.

blocks.text: Keys let us uniquely identify rows — this single idea underlies primary keys, foreign keys, normalization, and indexing. Get comfortable with this vocabulary; it recurs throughout the rest of the book. Key type Meaning
Super Key
Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies a row (may have extra, redundant attributes)
Candidate Key
A minimal super key — remove any attribute and it stops being unique
Primary Key
The candidate key chosen to be THE official identifier; cannot be NULL Alternate Key A candidate key not chosen as primary
Composite Key
A primary/candidate key made of more than one attribute together
Foreign Key
An attribute in one table referencing the primary key of another table

Super → Candidate → Primary is a funnel: Super keys are any unique combo (possibly bloated), Candidate keys are the minimal ones among those, and Primary is the one we crown from the candidates. “All candidates are super keys; not all super keys are candidates.” Common constraints Constraint Enforces PRIMARY KEY Uniqueness + NOT NULL, one per table FOREIGN KEY Referential integrity — value must exist in the referenced table UNIQUE No duplicate values (NULLs usually allowed, unlike primary key) NOT NULL Column cannot be empty CHECK A custom boolean condition (e.g. age >= 18) DEFAULT A value used automatically when none is supplied

CREATE TABLE Employee ( empId INT PRIMARY KEY, deptId INT REFERENCES Department(deptId), email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL, age INT CHECK (age >= 18) ); deptId is a foreign key referencing Department's primary key — the DB will reject any insert with a deptId that does not already exist there. This is referential integrity.

“A table can have multiple primary keys.” No — a table has exactly one primary key (which may be composite, spanning multiple columns), but it can have many other UNIQUE constraints (alternate keys). Saying “composite primary key” is correct; saying “multiple primary keys” is not.

qas.q: What is the difference between a primary key and a unique key?
qas.a: Both enforce uniqueness, but a primary key additionally disallows NULL and a table can have only one; a table can have multiple UNIQUE constraints, and (in most DBs) UNIQUE columns can contain a NULL.

qas.q: What is referential integrity?
qas.a: The guarantee that a foreign key value either matches an existing primary key value in the referenced table or is NULL — preventing 'dangling' references to non-existent rows.

qas.q: What happens if you try to delete a row that is referenced by a foreign key?
qas.a: By default it is rejected (RESTRICT). Depending on the FK's ON DELETE clause it could instead CASCADE (delete dependents too), SET NULL, or SET DEFAULT — the schema designer chooses the behaviour.

qas.q: Can a foreign key reference a non-primary-key column?
qas.a: It must reference a column with a UNIQUE constraint (often the primary key, but an alternate unique key also works) — it cannot reference an arbitrary non-unique column, since the reference must resolve to at most one row.

blocks.text: Keys are foundational — interviewers test them indirectly through normalization and schema-design questions. Be fluent enough to design a small schema live: name the entities, pick primary keys, add foreign keys for relationships, and state which constraints you'd add and why.

Normalization is the process of organising tables to reduce redundancy and prevent data-inconsistency bugs called anomalies. It works by progressively splitting a 'messy' table into smaller, cleaner ones connected by foreign keys, guided by rules called normal forms. The three anomalies normalization prevents Anomaly What goes wrong
Insertion anomaly
Cannot insert a fact (e.g. a new course) without also having unrelated data (a student already enrolled in it)
Update anomaly
The same fact is stored in multiple rows; updating one place but not others leaves inconsistent copies
Deletion anomaly
Deleting one fact accidentally erases an unrelated fact stored only alongside it

Unnormalized table StudentCourse(rollNo, studentName, courseId, courseName, instructor): rollNo studentName courseId courseName instructor 101 Asha C1 DBMS Mr. Rao 101 Asha C2 OS Ms. Iyer 102 Ravi C1 DBMS Mr. Rao Update anomaly: if 'Mr. Rao' is renamed, you must update every row mentioning C1 or risk inconsistency. Deletion anomaly: if Ravi drops C1 and that row is deleted, you lose the fact that 'C1 = DBMS, taught by Mr. Rao' entirely if no one else is enrolled. Insertion anomaly: you cannot add a new course with no students yet, because the table requires a rollNo/studentName for every row. Functional Dependency (FD) — the foundation A functional dependency X → Y means: given the value of X, the value of Y is uniquely determined. Example: rollNo → studentName (each roll number determines exactly one name). Normal forms are defined entirely in terms of which FDs are 'allowed' to exist in a well-designed table. 1NF — First Normal Form A table is in 1NF if every cell holds a single atomic value (no lists, no comma-separated values, no repeating groups) and rows are uniquely identifiable.

Violates 1NF: Student(rollNo, name, phones) where phones = "9876543210, 9123456780" — a list crammed into one cell. Fix: split into a separate row per phone number, e.g. StudentPhone(rollNo, phone) with one row per number — now every cell is atomic. 2NF — Second Normal Form A table is in 2NF if it is in 1NF and every non-key attribute depends on the whole primary key, not just part of it. This only matters when the primary key is composite — the violation is called a partial dependency.

Table Enrollment(studentId, courseId, studentName, marks) with composite key (studentId, courseId). studentName depends only on studentId (part of the key), not on the whole (studentId, courseId) pair — a partial dependency, violating 2NF. Fix — decompose: Student(studentId, studentName) and Enrollment(studentId, courseId, marks). Now studentName lives once per student, not once per enrollment row. 3NF — Third Normal Form A table is in 3NF if it is in 2NF and no non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute. This violation is called a transitive dependency: key → A → B, where B should really depend only on the key.

Table Employee(empId, deptId, deptName). Here empId → deptId → deptName: deptName depends on deptId, a non-key attribute, not directly on empId. That is a transitive dependency. Fix — decompose: Employee(empId, deptId) and Department(deptId, deptName). Now deptName is stored once per department, not duplicated across every employee in that department. BCNF — Boyce-Codd Normal Form A stricter version of 3NF: for every non-trivial functional dependency X → Y, X must be a super key (not just 'part of a candidate key' as 3NF tolerates in rare cases). BCNF removes a subtle class of anomalies that 3NF can still allow when there are multiple overlapping candidate keys.

Table Lecture(course, instructor, room), where each instructor always teaches in one fixed room (instructor → room), but a room can host several courses. (course, instructor) is a candidate key. This is in 3NF, but instructor → room violates BCNF because instructor alone is not a super key. Fix — decompose: Teaches(course, instructor) and InstructorRoom(instructor, room). Now instructor → room holds in a table where instructor genuinely is the key. 1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups | v remove PARTIAL dependency (non-key attr depends on PART of composite key) 2NF | v remove TRANSITIVE dependency (non-key attr depends on another non-key attr) 3NF | v every determinant (LHS of an FD) must be a super key BCNF Each normal form fixes one specific kind of bad dependency left over from the previous. Normal Form Removes Rule of thumb 1NF Non-atomic values One value per cell 2NF Partial dependency Whole key, not just part of it, determines every attribute 3NF Transitive dependency Non-key attributes depend only on the key, nothing else BCNF Anomalies from overlapping candidate keys Every determinant must be a super key

1NF→2NF→3NF→BCNF removes, in order: “Atomic, Partial, Transitive, Key” — Atomicity (1NF), Partial dependency (2NF), Transitive dependency (3NF), every determinant is a Key (BCNF). Mnemonic sentence: “A Pirate's Treasure is Key.”

“More normalization is always better.” Normalization reduces redundancy and anomalies but increases the number of joins needed to answer a query, which costs performance. Real systems often denormalize deliberately (e.g. for read-heavy analytics/reporting tables) trading some redundancy for query speed. A senior answer mentions this trade-off unprompted.

qas.q: What is the difference between 2NF and 3NF?
qas.a: 2NF removes partial dependencies — non-key attributes must depend on the whole composite key, not part of it. 3NF additionally removes transitive dependencies — non-key attributes must depend only on the key, not on another non-key attribute.

qas.q: What is BCNF and how does it differ from 3NF?
qas.a: BCNF requires that for every functional dependency X → Y, X must be a super key. 3NF has a small exception allowing X to be part of a candidate key in certain cases; BCNF removes that exception, eliminating anomalies that can survive in 3NF when candidate keys overlap.

qas.q: Why would you ever denormalize a database?
qas.a: To trade some redundancy/anomaly risk for fewer joins and faster reads — common in reporting/analytics tables, caching layers, or read-heavy systems where write consistency matters less than query speed.

qas.q: What is a functional dependency, in your own words?
qas.a: X → Y means knowing the value of X tells you the value of Y uniquely — Y is fully determined by X. All normal forms are defined in terms of which FDs are permitted to exist.

qas.q: Give an example of a transitive dependency.
qas.a: Employee(empId, deptId, deptName): empId → deptId → deptName. deptName is transitively dependent on empId through deptId, so it should be moved to a separate Department table.

blocks.text: Normalization is the single highest-yield DBMS topic for interviews — expect to be given an unnormalized table and asked to normalize it live, identifying each FD and which normal form is violated. Practice the exact pattern used in the examples above: state the violating dependency by name (partial/transitive), then show the decomposition. Also rehearse defending denormalization as a deliberate trade-off, not a mistake.

SQL is declarative: you state what data you want, not how to fetch it — the DBMS's query optimizer decides the execution plan. This section focuses on the parts interviewers actually probe: joins, subqueries, grouping, and set operations, using one running example schema. RUNNING EXAMPLE SCHEMA (used throughout this section) Student(studentId, name, deptId) Department(deptId, deptName) Enrollment(studentId, courseId, marks) Course(courseId, courseName) Command categories Category Commands Purpose DDL CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE Define/modify schema structure DML SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE Manipulate the actual data DCL GRANT, REVOKE Control access permissions TCL COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT Control transaction boundaries

“DELETE and TRUNCATE are the same.” DELETE removes rows one at a time, can have a WHERE clause, is logged, and can be rolled back. TRUNCATE deallocates the whole table's data at once, cannot be filtered, and in many DBs cannot be rolled back (or resets auto-increment counters) — it is DDL-like, not DML. Joins A join combines rows from two tables based on a related column — almost always a foreign-key-to-primary-key relationship. Student Enrollment INNER JOIN result (only matches) +----+------+ +-----+-------+ +----+------+--------+ | id | name | | sid | course| | id | name | course | | 1 | Asha | | 1 | C1 | ---> | 1 | Asha | C1 | | 2 | Ravi | | 1 | C2 | | 1 | Asha | C2 | | 3 | Zoya | | 9 | C3 | (Ravi, Zoya: no match -> dropped) +----+------+ +-----+-------+ (sid 9: no matching student -> dropped) INNER JOIN keeps only rows with a match on both sides. Join type Returns INNER JOIN Only rows with a match in both tables LEFT (OUTER) JOIN All rows from the left table, matched data from the right (NULL if no match) RIGHT (OUTER) JOIN All rows from the right table, matched data from the left (NULL if no match) FULL OUTER JOIN All rows from both sides; NULLs where there is no match on either side CROSS JOIN Every row of the left paired with every row of the right (Cartesian product) SELF JOIN A table joined with itself (e.g. employee–manager hierarchy)

Find every student with the courses they are enrolled in, including students enrolled in nothing: SELECT s.name, e.courseId FROM Student s LEFT JOIN Enrollment e ON s.studentId = e.studentId; Zoya (no enrollment) would still appear, with courseId shown as NULL — that is exactly what LEFT JOIN guarantees: every row from the left table is kept. Aggregation & GROUP BY Aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) compute over a set of rows. GROUP BY buckets rows before aggregating; HAVING filters after grouping, while WHERE filters before grouping.

Departments with more than 50 students: SELECT deptId, COUNT(*) FROM Student GROUP BY deptId HAVING COUNT(*) > 50; WHERE could not do this filter — COUNT(*) does not exist until rows are grouped, so the filter on the aggregate must be HAVING.

“WHERE and HAVING are interchangeable.” WHERE filters individual rows before grouping and cannot reference aggregate functions. HAVING filters groups after aggregation and can reference COUNT/SUM/etc. Using WHERE COUNT(*) > 5 is a syntax error in standard SQL. Subqueries A subquery is a query nested inside another. Scalar subqueries return one value; row/table subqueries return multiple rows used with IN/EXISTS; correlated subqueries reference a column from the outer query and re-run once per outer row (more expensive).

Students scoring above their department's average — a correlated subquery: SELECT s.name FROM Student s WHERE s.marks > (SELECT AVG(marks) FROM Student s2 WHERE s2.deptId = s.deptId); The inner query depends on s.deptId from the outer row, so it conceptually re-runs once per outer student — this is what makes it 'correlated' rather than independent. EXISTS IN Returns true/false based on whether the subquery returns any rows Checks if a value matches any value in a list/subquery result Often faster when subquery result is large (can short-circuit) Simpler to read for small, static lists NULL-safe in most cases Can behave unexpectedly with NULLs in the subquery result Set operations Operation Meaning UNION Combine results of two queries, removing duplicates UNION ALL Combine results, keeping duplicates (faster — no dedup step) INTERSECT Only rows that appear in both result sets EXCEPT / MINUS Rows in the first result set but not the second

UNION ALL is faster than UNION because plain UNION must sort/hash to remove duplicates; UNION ALL skips that step entirely. Default to UNION ALL whenever you know duplicates cannot occur or do not matter. Order of logical query execution (important!) SQL is written FROM...WHERE...GROUP BY...HAVING...SELECT...ORDER BY but is conceptually executed in roughly this order: FROM/JOIN -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> HAVING -> SELECT -> ORDER BY -> LIMIT (build rows) (filter rows) (bucket) (filter groups) (pick cols) (sort) (cap rows) This is why WHERE can't see aggregates (not computed yet) but HAVING can (computed by then).

qas.q: Difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN?
qas.a: INNER JOIN keeps only rows with a match in both tables. LEFT JOIN keeps every row from the left table regardless of a match, filling unmatched right-side columns with NULL — useful for 'show me everything, even with no related data.'

qas.q: What is a correlated subquery and why is it potentially slow?
qas.a: A subquery that references a column from the outer query, so conceptually it re-executes once per outer row, rather than once overall — making it costlier than an independent subquery, especially on large tables without good indexing.

qas.q: Why can't you filter on an aggregate using WHERE?
qas.a: Because WHERE filters rows before GROUP BY runs, and aggregates like COUNT()/SUM() only exist after grouping. HAVING runs after aggregation, so it can filter on aggregate values.

qas.q: Difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
qas.a: UNION removes duplicate rows from the combined result (requires an implicit sort/dedup); UNION ALL keeps all rows including duplicates and is faster since it skips deduplication.

qas.q: What is a self join and when would you use one?
qas.a: Joining a table to itself, typically to relate rows within the same table — e.g. an Employee table with a managerId column, joined to itself to list each employee next to their manager's name.

qas.q: Write a query to find the second-highest salary.
qas.a: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM Employee WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM Employee); — or use LIMIT/OFFSET with ORDER BY, or window functions like DENSE_RANK() for ties. Interviewers want you to handle duplicate top salaries correctly.

blocks.text: SQL questions are usually live coding, not multiple choice — practice writing joins, GROUP BY/HAVING, and the 'Nth highest value' family of questions until you can type them without hesitation. Always say your query out loud as you write it; interviewers are grading your reasoning, not just the final syntax. Know window functions (RANK, DENSE_RANK, ROW_NUMBER) — they come up often for 'Nth highest' and 'top-N per group' problems.

Without an index, finding a row means a full table scan — checking every row. An index is an auxiliary data structure that lets the DBMS jump almost directly to the rows matching a condition, similar to how a book's index lets you skip straight to a page instead of reading cover to cover.

A textbook's index at the back maps topics to page numbers. Looking up 'mitochondria' in the index takes seconds; flipping through every page would take forever. A database index does the same job for column values — it maps a value (or range of values) to the physical location of matching rows. Clustered vs non-clustered index Clustered index Non-clustered index Data order The table's actual rows are physically sorted by this key A separate structure holding pointers to rows; table order unaffected How many per table At most one (data can only be sorted one way) Many allowed Lookup speed Very fast — the index IS the data Fast, but needs one extra hop to fetch the actual row Typical default Primary key (in many DBs) Any column you explicitly index (e.g. email) CLUSTERED (on id): NON-CLUSTERED (on email): +----+-------+--------+ +-----------------+ points to -> row location | id | name | email | | email (sorted) | in the table +----+-------+--------+ +-----------------+ (one extra hop) | 1 | Asha | a@x... | <-- physical row order a@x... -------> id=1 | 2 | Ravi | r@x... | matches the index r@x... -------> id=2 | 3 | Zoya | z@x... | z@x... -------> id=3 A clustered index dictates physical row order; a non-clustered index is a side lookup.

Clustered = the data IS the index. Think of a phone book sorted by surname — the book itself is in that order. Non-clustered = a sticky-note index pointing into an unsorted pile — one extra step to find the actual page. B-Tree vs B+Tree Most relational databases implement indexes using a B+Tree — a balanced tree structure where every leaf is at the same depth, giving predictable, fast lookups (O(log n)) even for huge tables. B-Tree B+Tree Data (and keys) can live in internal nodes too Only leaf nodes store actual data; internal nodes store only keys for routing Range queries less efficient (data scattered across levels) Leaves are linked in a list, making range scans (BETWEEN, ORDER BY) very fast Used less often in modern DB indexes The standard choice for most relational DB indexes [ 30 | 60 ] <- internal node: routing keys only / | \ [10|20] [40|50] [70|80] <- internal nodes / | \ / | \ / | \ leaf leaf leaf ... (all data lives ONLY in leaf nodes) |----|----|----|----|----|----|----| <- leaves linked left-to-right (linked list of leaves enables fast range scans: 25 to 65 = walk the leaf chain) B+Tree: all data in leaves, leaves linked, so range queries don't need to revisit the root. Hash index A hash index applies a hash function to the key to compute a bucket location directly — giving O(1) average lookup for exact-match queries (WHERE id = 5). It cannot efficiently support range queries (WHERE id BETWEEN 5 AND 50) because hashing destroys the ordering between keys — two close values can land in completely different buckets. Index type Best for Weak at B+Tree Range queries, ORDER BY, equality Slightly slower than hash for pure equality Hash Exact-match equality lookups Range queries, ORDER BY (no useful ordering) The cost of indexes — they are not free Every index speeds up reads but slows down writes: every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must also update each index on that table, and indexes consume extra disk space. This is why you should not blindly index every column — index columns that are frequently filtered, joined, or sorted on, and avoid over-indexing write-heavy tables.

“Adding more indexes always makes queries faster.” Indexes only help the reads that use them; they actively hurt write performance and use storage. A table with 20 redundant indexes can be slower to insert into than one with 3 well-chosen ones. Always frame indexing as a read/write trade-off in interviews. Composite indexes & column order A composite index spans multiple columns, e.g. INDEX(lastName, firstName). It can serve a query filtering on lastName alone, or lastName AND firstName together — but it generally cannot efficiently serve a query filtering on firstName alone, because the index is sorted by lastName first. This is the leftmost-prefix rule.

qas.q: What is the difference between a clustered and non-clustered index?
qas.a: A clustered index determines the physical storage order of the table's rows — there can be only one. A non-clustered index is a separate structure of pointers to rows, leaving physical order untouched; a table can have many.

qas.q: Why do databases use B+Trees instead of plain B-Trees for indexes?
qas.a: B+Trees store all data only in leaf nodes (internal nodes hold routing keys), and leaves are linked together, making range scans and sorted traversal fast — a major win for queries with ORDER BY or BETWEEN, which B-Trees handle less efficiently.

qas.q: When would a hash index be a bad choice?
qas.a: For any range query (BETWEEN, <, >, ORDER BY) since hashing destroys key ordering — similar values can hash to unrelated buckets. Use hash indexes only for exact-match lookups.

qas.q: Why shouldn't you index every column in a table?
qas.a: Each index speeds up reads on that column but must be updated on every write, slowing INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, and consumes extra disk space. Index selectively based on actual query patterns (WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY columns).

qas.q: What is the leftmost-prefix rule for composite indexes?
qas.a: A composite index on (A, B) can be used for queries filtering on A alone or on A and B together, but generally not for queries filtering on B alone, since the index is physically ordered by A first.

blocks.text: Indexing questions often start conceptual then pivot to “how would you speed up this slow query?” Have a mental checklist: is there a usable index on the filtered/joined column? Is it the right type (B+Tree vs hash) for the query pattern (range vs equality)? Is a composite index ordered correctly for the leftmost-prefix rule? Always acknowledge the write-cost trade-off — it signals you think about indexing as an engineering decision, not a free speed boost.

A transaction is a sequence of one or more database operations treated as a single logical unit of work — either all of it happens, or none of it does. The classic example: transferring money between two bank accounts must debit one and credit the other together; doing only one half would lose or duplicate money.

Think of a transaction as swapping seats with a friend on a train. Either both of you move seats successfully, or neither does. If you stood up and moved but your friend couldn't (their new seat was occupied), you would sit back down rather than leave one seat empty and one double-occupied — the swap is all-or-nothing. ACID — the four guarantees Property Guarantee Atomicity All operations in the transaction succeed, or none do — no partial transaction is ever visible Consistency A transaction takes the database from one valid state to another, respecting all constraints Isolation Concurrently running transactions do not interfere with each other's intermediate state Durability Once committed, changes survive even a crash immediately afterward

ACID spells itself: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. One-line story: a bank transfer either fully happens or not at all (Atomicity), never breaks account-balance rules (Consistency), doesn't show half-done state to others (Isolation), and survives a crash the instant after confirmation (Durability).

Transfer Rs.500 from Account A to Account B: BEGIN; UPDATE Account SET balance = balance - 500 WHERE id='A'; UPDATE Account SET balance = balance + 500 WHERE id='B'; COMMIT; If the system crashes after the first UPDATE but before COMMIT, atomicity guarantees the whole transaction is rolled back on restart — A's debit is undone, money is not lost in limbo. Schedules and serializability When multiple transactions run concurrently, their operations can interleave in many possible orders — each ordering is called a schedule. A schedule is serializable if its effect is equivalent to running the transactions one at a time in some order (even though they actually interleaved). Serializability is the gold-standard correctness criterion for concurrent execution. Schedule type Meaning Serial schedule Transactions run completely one after another, no interleaving — always correct, but slow Serializable schedule Interleaved, but the result matches some serial order — correct AND concurrent Non-serializable schedule Interleaved in a way that matches NO serial order — can produce wrong/inconsistent results

“Serial and serializable mean the same thing.” Serial means literally one transaction completes before the next starts (no overlap at all). Serializable means the transactions can overlap in time, but the final outcome is still equivalent to some serial order. Serializable schedules are what databases actually aim to allow, because pure serial execution has zero concurrency. Transaction states [Active] --> [Partially Committed] --> [Committed] | v [Failed] --> [Aborted] -----------------> (transaction restarted or given up) A transaction moves toward Committed (durable success) or Aborted (fully rolled back).

qas.q: Explain ACID with a real example.
qas.a: Take a fund transfer: Atomicity ensures both the debit and credit happen together or not at all. Consistency ensures total balance across accounts stays correct (no money created/destroyed). Isolation ensures a concurrent balance check never sees a half-completed transfer. Durability ensures that once the transfer is confirmed, it survives even an immediate crash.

qas.q: What is a serializable schedule?
qas.a: A schedule of interleaved transactions whose final result is equivalent to some serial (non-interleaved) execution order of the same transactions — concurrency without sacrificing correctness.

qas.q: What is the difference between a serial schedule and a serializable schedule?
qas.a: A serial schedule has zero overlap between transactions. A serializable schedule can have overlapping/interleaved operations but still produces a result equivalent to some serial order. Serializable is the practical goal; pure serial has no concurrency benefit.

qas.q: What happens if a transaction fails midway?
qas.a: It is aborted, and the database rolls back any partial changes it made (using log records), restoring the state to before the transaction began — preserving atomicity.

blocks.text: ACID is asked almost universally, but the strong differentiator is explaining it with a concrete example (the bank transfer) rather than reciting four words. Be ready to go one level deeper: “how does the DB guarantee durability?” leads straight into write-ahead logging, covered in the Recovery section later in this book.

Concurrency control is the set of techniques a DBMS uses to let many transactions run at once while still producing a serializable (correct) result. The two dominant families are locking (pessimistic — block conflicting access) and timestamp ordering / MVCC (optimistic — let things proceed and check/version later). Lock-based protocols Lock type Allows
Shared (S) lock
Multiple transactions can hold it simultaneously — for reading
Exclusive (X) lock
Only one transaction can hold it — for writing; blocks all other locks
Two-Phase Locking (2PL)

2PL is the standard protocol guaranteeing serializability. Each transaction has exactly two phases: a Growing phase where it can only acquire locks (never release), and a Shrinking phase where it can only release locks (never acquire). Once the first lock is released, no new lock may be taken. number of locks held ^ | /\ | / \ | / \ | / \ | / \

+-----+----------+-----> time
       GROWING       SHRINKING
       (acquire only) (release only)
              ^ peak = the LOCK POINT

2PL: lock count rises then falls — never both directions at once. Plain 2PL guarantees serializability but can still allow cascading rollbacks (one transaction's abort forces others that read its uncommitted data to abort too). Strict 2PL fixes this by holding all exclusive locks until the transaction actually commits or aborts — the variant used by virtually all real databases.

2PL in one phrase: “Grow then shrink, never both.” Once you let go of one lock, you can never grab a new one — like a one-way valve. Deadlocks in databases Just like in OS, two transactions can each hold a lock the other needs, waiting forever. Databases handle this the same way OS textbooks describe: detection via a wait-for graph (abort one transaction to break the cycle), or prevention via timeout (abort a transaction that has waited too long) or ordering schemes (wait-die / wound-wait, based on transaction timestamps). Scheme Idea Wait-Die An older transaction may wait for a younger one; a younger one requesting a lock held by an older one is aborted (dies) Wound-Wait An older transaction preempts (wounds/aborts) a younger one holding the lock it needs; a younger one simply waits for an older one Timestamp ordering & MVCC Instead of locks, timestamp ordering assigns each transaction a unique timestamp at start and orders conflicting operations by timestamp, aborting and restarting a transaction that would violate that order. MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) — used by PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle — takes a related but more practical approach: instead of blocking readers, it keeps multiple versions of a row, so a reader sees a consistent snapshot from the start of its transaction while writers create new versions, without readers and writers blocking each other.

MVCC is like a library that never wants 'no checking out' signs. Instead of locking a book while someone edits it, the librarian photocopies the page: anyone who started reading before the edit keeps seeing their photocopy (the old version), while the editor works on a new version. When the edit is done, future readers get the new page. Readers and writers never block each other.

Full serializability is expensive — sometimes we accept weaker isolation for better performance. SQL defines four standard isolation levels, each permitting (or preventing) specific anomalies. Understanding isolation levels means understanding exactly which anomaly each level lets through. The three anomalies, defined precisely Anomaly What happens
Dirty Read
Transaction A reads data that Transaction B has written but not yet committed — if B rolls back, A read data that never officially existed
Non-Repeatable Read
Transaction A reads a row twice and gets different values, because Transaction B updated and committed that row in between
Phantom Read
Transaction A re-runs the same range query twice and gets a different SET of rows, because Transaction B inserted/deleted a matching row in between

Dirty read in action: B starts UPDATE Account SET balance = balance - 500 WHERE id='A' (not yet committed). A runs SELECT balance FROM Account WHERE id='A' and sees the reduced balance. B then ROLLS BACK. A has now acted on a balance that, officially, never existed. The four isolation levels Level Dirty Read Non-Repeatable Read Phantom Read Read Uncommitted Possible Possible Possible Read Committed Prevented Possible Possible Repeatable Read Prevented Prevented Possible (standard); InnoDB also prevents this Serializable Prevented Prevented Prevented

As you go Read Uncommitted → Read Committed → Repeatable Read → Serializable, you prevent one more anomaly at each step, in this exact order: dirty read, then non-repeatable read, then phantom read. Higher isolation = fewer anomalies but more blocking/lower concurrency — it is always a trade-off, never a free upgrade.

“Higher isolation is always better, so just use Serializable everywhere.” Serializable gives the strongest correctness but the worst concurrency — more blocking, more aborts/retries, lower throughput. Most production systems default to Read Committed (Postgres, Oracle default) or Repeatable Read (MySQL InnoDB default) and only escalate when correctness actually demands it.

qas.q: What is a dirty read and which isolation level prevents it?
qas.a: A transaction reading uncommitted (possibly soon-to-be-rolled-back) data written by another transaction. Read Committed and all stricter levels prevent it; only Read Uncommitted allows it.

qas.q: Difference between non-repeatable read and phantom read?
qas.a: Non-repeatable read is about the same row changing value between two reads (an UPDATE by another transaction). Phantom read is about the same range query returning a different SET of rows (an INSERT/DELETE by another transaction) — one is about a row's value, the other about a query's row count.

qas.q: What is the default isolation level in PostgreSQL vs MySQL InnoDB?
qas.a: PostgreSQL defaults to Read Committed. MySQL's InnoDB defaults to Repeatable Read, and its implementation (via MVCC) also happens to prevent phantom reads in most cases, stronger than the SQL standard strictly requires for that level.

qas.q: Why not just always use the Serializable isolation level?
qas.a: It maximises correctness but minimises concurrency — more locking/blocking and more transaction aborts under contention, hurting throughput and latency. Choose the weakest level that still meets the application's correctness needs.

qas.q: What is MVCC and why do many modern databases prefer it over pure locking?
qas.a: Multi-Version Concurrency Control keeps multiple row versions so readers see a consistent snapshot without blocking writers (and vice versa). It gives much better read concurrency than lock-based approaches, which is why Postgres/MySQL/Oracle all use some form of it.

blocks.text: The isolation-level table above is one of the most direct 'memorise and recite' tables in all of DBMS interviews — but the strongest candidates explain anomalies with a concrete two-transaction timeline like the dirty-read example, not just naming them. Be ready for “what isolation level would you pick for X system and why” — always answer with the trade-off (correctness vs concurrency), not just a level name.

Durability (the 'D' in ACID) means a committed transaction's effects must survive a crash — but writing every change straight to disk the instant it happens would be far too slow. The standard solution is Write-Ahead Logging (WAL): before modifying the actual data on disk, the DBMS first writes a record of the intended change to an append-only log, and only that log write must be flushed to disk before the transaction can be considered committed.

WAL is like keeping a diary of every move before you actually move furniture. Before sliding the sofa, you jot "moving sofa from wall A to wall B" in a notebook. If the power goes out mid-move, anyone can read the notebook afterward and know exactly what was intended, and either finish the move or undo it — without having to inspect every piece of furniture in the house to guess what happened. Transaction wants to update a row | v 1. Write a LOG RECORD describing the change (old value, new value) --> flush to disk | v 2. Apply the change to the actual data page (can stay in memory for now) | v 3. On COMMIT: ensure the log record is durably on disk -> transaction is now durable | v (the data page itself can be written to disk later, lazily) WAL rule: the log record always reaches disk BEFORE (or with) the commit, never after. Using the log to recover after a crash After a crash, the DBMS replays the log to restore consistency, typically in three passes (as in the classic ARIES algorithm): Phase What it does Analysis Scan the log to determine which transactions were committed, which were in-progress at crash time Redo Re-apply all logged changes (even from committed transactions) to make sure the data reflects everything that was logged Undo Roll back any changes made by transactions that never committed before the crash Checkpoints Replaying the entire log since the database was created would be impossibly slow. A checkpoint is a periodic marker where the DBMS confirms all changes up to that point are safely written to disk, so recovery only needs to replay the log from the most recent checkpoint onward, not from the beginning of time.

WAL's golden rule in five words: “Log it before you change it.” Everything about recovery follows from that one sentence — if the log always lands first, replaying it after a crash can always reconstruct (or undo) any in-flight change.

“The database writes data to disk immediately on every change, so WAL is redundant.” The opposite is true: WAL exists specifically so the DBMS does not need to flush every data page immediately (slow, random I/O) — it only needs the much smaller, sequential log write to be durable, and can write the actual data pages lazily and in batches later.

qas.q: What is Write-Ahead Logging and why is it used?
qas.a: A protocol where the log record of a change is written to disk before the change itself is durably applied to the data. It lets the DBMS guarantee durability and crash recovery while deferring slower data-page writes, since only the log write needs to be synchronous and it is sequential (fast) rather than random I/O.

qas.q: What is a checkpoint and why is it needed?
qas.a: A periodic marker confirming all prior changes are flushed to disk, so that crash recovery only needs to replay the log from the most recent checkpoint rather than from the very beginning, dramatically reducing recovery time.

qas.q: How does Redo differ from Undo during recovery?
qas.a: Redo re-applies logged changes to ensure the database reflects everything that was logged, including committed transactions whose data pages weren't yet flushed. Undo rolls back changes made by transactions that never committed before the crash.

blocks.text: Recovery questions are less universal than ACID/normalization but show up when interviewers probe durability deeply: “how does a database guarantee durability without being painfully slow?” is your cue to explain WAL. Mentioning checkpoints unprompted signals you understand recovery is an engineering trade-off (recovery speed vs runtime overhead), not just a textbook fact.

Rows are physically grouped into fixed-size blocks/pages (commonly 4–16 KB) — the unit the DBMS reads/writes from disk. How rows are arranged within and across these blocks is the file organization. Organization Idea Trade-off
Heap (unordered)
Rows stored in no particular order, just appended
Fast inserts; slow searches without an index Sequential (sorted)
Rows stored in sorted order by some key
Fast range scans; slow inserts (must keep
sorted)
Hash
Rows placed into buckets via a hash function on the
key
Fast equality lookups; poor range queries

This is exactly why indexes matter (Section 6): a heap-organized table with no index needs a full scan for any lookup, while adding a B+Tree index gives fast lookups without forcing the whole table to be physically re-sorted.
CHAPTER 2B
Interview Question Bank

Curated, high-yield DBMS questions most likely to be asked in SDE rounds Each section above already embedded its own questions; below are additional rapid-fire and cross-cutting ones that recur across product and service companies. Drill them by covering the answer and saying it aloud. Schema Design & Keys

qas.q: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
qas.a: DELETE removes rows (optionally filtered), is logged, and is rollback-able. TRUNCATE removes all rows at once, is minimally logged, and resets identity counters — generally not filterable or easily rollback-able. DROP removes the entire table structure itself, including its schema.

qas.q: What is a surrogate key vs a natural key?
qas.a: A natural key is derived from real-world data already unique to the entity (e.g. an email or SSN). A surrogate key is an artificial identifier with no business meaning (e.g. an auto-incrementing integer or UUID), often preferred because natural keys can change or have edge cases (duplicate SSNs, email changes).

qas.q: Can a foreign key be NULL?
qas.a: Yes, unless explicitly constrained with NOT NULL — a NULL foreign key simply means 'no relationship for this row,' which is valid in optional relationships (e.g. an employee not yet assigned a manager).

qas.q: What is a self-referencing foreign key, with an example?
qas.a: A foreign key in a table that references the same table's primary key — e.g. Employee(empId, managerId) where managerId references Employee.empId, modeling a reporting hierarchy. SQL & Query Behaviour

qas.q: What is the difference between a view and a materialized view?
qas.a: A view is a stored query — it has no data of its own and re-runs the underlying query every time it's accessed. A materialized view physically stores the query's result and must be refreshed periodically, trading staleness for much faster reads.

qas.q: What does a NULL mean in SQL, and why is NULL = NULL false?
qas.a: NULL represents 'unknown/missing,' not a value. Comparing two unknowns with = does not logically yield true — it yields NULL (neither true nor false) under three-valued logic, which is why you must use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL instead of = NULL.

qas.q: What is a stored procedure and why use one?
qas.a: A precompiled, named block of SQL/procedural logic stored in the database, callable by name. Benefits: reduced network round-trips, reusable logic, and sometimes better performance due to query plan caching — at the cost of coupling logic to the database.

qas.q: What is a trigger?
qas.a: A piece of code automatically executed by the DBMS in response to an event (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on a table — used for auditing, enforcing complex constraints, or maintaining derived data, though overused triggers can make systems hard to debug. Transactions & Concurrency

qas.q: What is a lost update, and how is it different from a dirty read?
qas.a: A lost update happens when two transactions read the same value, both compute a new value based on it, and the second write silently overwrites the first — losing one update entirely. A dirty read is about reading another transaction's uncommitted/possibly-rolled-back data. Lost update is a write-write conflict; dirty read is a read of bad data.

qas.q: What is optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency control?
qas.a: Pessimistic control (locking) assumes conflicts are likely and blocks access upfront. Optimistic control assumes conflicts are rare, lets transactions proceed freely, and only checks for conflicts at commit time, aborting/retrying if one occurred.

qas.q: What is a deadlock in a database context, briefly?
qas.a: Two or more transactions each hold a lock the other needs, so neither can proceed. Databases detect this via a wait-for graph or prevent it via timestamp-based schemes (wait-die/wound-wait) or timeouts. General / Cross-cutting

qas.q: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases, at a high level?
qas.a: SQL (relational) databases enforce a fixed schema and strong consistency/ACID guarantees, organising data into related tables. NoSQL databases (document, key-value, column-family, graph) typically offer flexible/schema-less structures and often trade strict consistency for horizontal scalability, suited to specific access patterns.

qas.q: What is database sharding?
qas.a: Splitting a large database horizontally across multiple machines, each holding a subset of the rows (e.g. by user ID range), to scale beyond what a single machine can handle. It adds complexity for cross-shard queries and joins.

qas.q: What is replication and why use it?
qas.a: Maintaining copies of the same data on multiple servers, for fault tolerance (a replica takes over if the primary fails) and to scale read throughput (reads served from replicas while writes go to the primary).

qas.q: What is the CAP theorem, briefly?
qas.a: In a distributed system, you can only guarantee two of three properties at once during a network partition: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. Since partitions are unavoidable in practice, real systems choose to favour either consistency (CP) or availability (AP) when one occurs. CHAPTER 2C The night-before-the-interview chapter: tables, formulas, and tricks Most-asked comparison tables Primary Key vs Unique Key Primary Key Unique Key Exactly one per table Multiple allowed per table Cannot be NULL Can usually contain one NULL Defines the row's main identity Enforces uniqueness on an alternate attribute Clustered vs Non-Clustered Index Clustered Non-Clustered Data physically sorted by this key Separate structure pointing to rows At most one per table Many allowed No extra hop to fetch row One extra hop via pointer DELETE vs TRUNCATE vs DROP DELETE TRUNCATE DROP Removes rows, filterable Removes all rows, not filterable Removes the table itself Fully logged, rollback-able Minimally logged Removes structure + data WHERE vs HAVING WHERE HAVING Filters rows before grouping Filters groups after aggregation Cannot use aggregate functions Can use COUNT/SUM/AVG etc. 2NF vs 3NF vs BCNF Form Removes Condition 2NF Partial dependency Non-key attrs depend on the WHOLE key 3NF Transitive dependency Non-key attrs depend ONLY on the key BCNF Overlapping-key anomalies Every determinant is a super key Isolation Levels (anomalies prevented) Level Dirty Read Non-Repeatable Phantom Read Uncommitted No No No Read Committed Yes No No Repeatable Read Yes Yes No (standard) Serializable Yes Yes Yes ("Yes" = that anomaly is prevented at this level.) table Foreign key an attribute referencing another table's primary/unique key Functional dependency X → Y: X's value uniquely determines Y's value Partial dependency a non-key attribute depends on
