Why use a DBMS over flat files, in under 60 seconds? 2. Super key → candidate key → primary key, and how do they differ? 3. Normalize a messy table live, naming the violated dependency at each step? 4. Write an INNER JOIN, a LEFT JOIN, and a query using GROUP BY + HAVING? 5. Clustered vs non-clustered index, and why B+Tree over hash for ranges? 6. ACID with the bank-transfer example? 7. Dirty read vs non-repeatable read vs phantom read, with a timeline? 8. Which isolation level prevents which anomaly? 9. What 2PL's growing/shrinking phases mean? 10. Why WAL exists and what a checkpoint is for? End of Book 2 — DBMS. Next in the series: Computer Networks, OOP, and Linux + Software Engineering. Same method as before: read a section, shut it, explain it aloud, then drill the orange and teal boxes. Recall, not recognition, is what interviews test.

blocks.text: Sending data from your laptop to a server on the other side of the world involves dozens of separate problems: how do bits travel over a wire or radio signal, how does data find the right physical machine on a local network, how does it get routed across the internet to a different network entirely, how do we guarantee it arrives complete and in order, and how does an application know what the bytes actually mean? Solving all of this as one giant tangled system would be unmanageable. Instead, networking is split into layers, each responsible for one job and relying on the layer below it to have already solved its own job.

Sending a networked message is like mailing a letter through a postal system. You write a letter (application data) and put it in an envelope with a destination address (this is like the application/transport layers). The post office doesn't read your letter — it only cares about the address on the envelope to route it city to city (network layer). The local delivery van just cares about getting the envelope from the depot to your street (data link layer). Each layer only worries about its own job and trusts the others to do theirs. The OSI 7-layer model The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model is a conceptual 7-layer reference model. Real networks (the internet) do not implement OSI exactly, but it remains the standard vocabulary interviewers use to talk about 'which layer' a concept belongs to. 7. Application <- HTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP "what the data MEANS" 6. Presentation <- encryption, compression, formatting 5. Session <- managing a connection/session between two apps 4. Transport <- TCP, UDP "reliable delivery, ports" 3. Network <- IP, routers "which machine, across networks" 2. Data Link <- Ethernet, switches, MAC "which machine, on this LAN" 1. Physical <- cables, radio waves, voltages "raw bits on the wire" Data flows down through the sender's layers, across the wire, then up through the receiver's.

Top-to-bottom OSI mnemonic: “All People Seem To Need Data Processing” — Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical. The TCP/IP 4-layer model (what the internet actually uses) The internet is built on the simpler, practical TCP/IP model, which compresses OSI's 7 layers into 4. This is the model that actually matters for how real systems (and most interview answers) are structured: TCP/IP Layer Roughly corresponds to OSI Examples Application Application + Presentation + Session HTTP, DNS, TLS Transport Transport TCP, UDP Internet Network IP, routing Link Data Link + Physical Ethernet, Wi-Fi Encapsulation: how data actually travels As data moves down the stack on the sending side, each layer wraps the data from the layer above with its own header (sometimes a trailer too) — this is called encapsulation. The receiving side reverses the process, peeling off headers as data moves up its own stack — decapsulation. SENDER (going down): [ HTTP data ] [TCP hdr][ HTTP data ] <- Transport layer adds TCP header (segment) [IP hdr][TCP hdr][ HTTP data ] <- Network layer adds IP header (packet) [Eth hdr][IP hdr][TCP hdr][ HTTP data ][Eth trl] <- Link layer adds Ethernet header/trailer (frame) --------------- sent as raw bits over the wire --------------- RECEIVER (going up): each layer strips its own header before passing data upward. Each layer's header is metadata for that layer's peer on the other end, ignored by the rest.

Names for data at each layer, top to bottom: Data → Segment (Transport) → Packet (Network) → Frame (Link) → Bits (Physical). Interviewers notice when you use the right word for the right layer (a 'packet' is specifically Network-layer terminology, not a generic word for 'data over the network').

“The internet literally implements all 7 OSI layers as separate distinct protocols.” The OSI model is a teaching/reference framework; the actual internet runs on TCP/IP's 4 (or 5, depending on how you count) layers. Several OSI layers (Presentation, Session) don't correspond to distinct protocols in practice — their jobs are absorbed into application-layer protocols like HTTP/TLS.

qas.q: Why do we need a layered networking model at all?
qas.a: It breaks an enormous, interdependent problem (getting bytes from one app to another across the world) into independent, swappable layers — each solving one job and trusting the layer below. This means, for example, you can change Wi-Fi to Ethernet (Link layer) without touching any application code, because the layers above don't depend on Link-layer details.

qas.q: What is the difference between the OSI model and the TCP/IP model?
qas.a: OSI is a 7-layer conceptual reference model used mainly for teaching/discussion. TCP/IP is the practical 4-layer model the actual internet runs on, which combines OSI's Application, Presentation, and Session layers into one Application layer.

qas.q: What is encapsulation?
qas.a: The process of each layer wrapping the data from the layer above it with its own header (and sometimes trailer) as data moves down the stack on the sender's side. The receiver reverses this (decapsulation), stripping headers as data moves up its stack.

qas.q: What is the difference between a packet and a frame?
qas.a: A packet is the unit of data at the Network layer (IP header + payload). A frame is the unit of data at the Link layer (Ethernet/Wi-Fi header + the packet + trailer). A frame contains a packet, which contains a segment, which contains application data.

blocks.text: “Explain the OSI model” is often a warm-up, not the real question — the real test is whether you can map a concrete technology (HTTP, TCP, IP, Ethernet) to its layer without hesitating, and explain why the layering helps (independent evolution of each layer). This sets up almost every later question in networking interviews, especially the 'what happens when you visit a URL' walkthrough.

To deliver data to the right machine, networks need addresses at two different levels: a MAC address identifies a specific physical network interface on a local network, and an IP address identifies a machine globally across networks. Confusing these two is one of the most common beginner mistakes — they solve different problems. MAC Address IP Address Layer Data Link (Layer 2) Network (Layer 3)
Scope
Unique to the physical network interface card Assigned per network, can change (DHCP) Format 48-bit, e.g. 3C:5A:B4:11:9E:02 32-bit (IPv4) e.g. 192.168.1.10, or 128-bit (IPv6) Changes?
Burned into hardware, essentially permanent
Can change when you join a different network
Used for
Delivery within a single LAN/local segment
Delivery across different networks (routing)

A MAC address is like a person's fingerprint — permanently tied to that specific device, unique, and used for very local identification (recognizing who's physically in front of you). An IP address is like a postal address — it can change when you move, and it's what's actually used to route something to you across a city, country, or the world. IPv4 vs IPv6 IPv4 IPv6 Address size 32-bit (~4.3 billion addresses) 128-bit (an effectively inexhaustible number) Format Dotted decimal: 192.168.1.1 Colon-separated hex: 2001:0db8::1 Why it exists Original internet addressing scheme Created because IPv4 addresses ran out Header complexity Simpler, smaller header fields Larger but more streamlined/extensible header

“We need IPv6 only because the world ran out of room to write bigger numbers in IPv4.” The real cause is that 32 bits caps IPv4 at about 4.3 billion unique addresses — nowhere near enough for the number of internet-connected devices today (phones, IoT, etc). IPv6's 128 bits give a practically unlimited address space, removing the scarcity problem altogether (and reducing reliance on workarounds like NAT). Public vs private IP addresses & NAT Certain IP ranges (like 192.168.0.0/16, 10.0.0.0/8) are reserved as private — not routable on the public internet, reused by every home/office network. Your router uses NAT (Network Address Translation) to let many devices on your private network share one public IP address when talking to the internet, translating addresses on the way out and back. Subnetting (the essentials) An IP address is split into a network portion and a host portion. A subnet mask (or CIDR notation like /24) defines where that split happens — it tells you which addresses are 'on the same local network' versus needing to be routed elsewhere.

192.168.1.0/24 means the first 24 bits are the network portion, leaving 8 bits (256 addresses, 254 usable) for hosts — addresses 192.168.1.1 through 192.168.1.254 are all on the same local subnet. A device wanting to talk to another IP first checks: is the destination's network portion the same as mine? If yes, deliver directly on the LAN. If no, send it to the default gateway (router) to be routed elsewhere.

/24 = 256 addresses total (2^8, since 32−24=8 host bits), minus 2 reserved (network address + broadcast address) = 254 usable hosts. This single fact (/24 ≈ 254 usable) covers most subnetting questions asked in interviews. ARP — bridging IP and MAC When a device knows the destination's IP address but needs to actually send a frame on the local network, it needs the destination's MAC address. ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) broadcasts "who has this IP?" on the LAN; the owning device replies with its MAC address, and the result is cached for future use. Host A wants to reach 192.168.1.5 but only knows the IP, not the MAC. Host A --[ARP broadcast: "who has 192.168.1.5?"]--> entire LAN Host B (owns that IP) --[ARP reply: "192.168.1.5 is at MAC AA:BB:CC..."]--> Host A Host A caches the mapping and sends the actual Ethernet frame to that MAC. ARP resolves an IP address to a MAC address within a local network. Switches vs routers Switch Router Operates at Data Link layer (uses MAC addresses) Network layer (uses IP addresses) Connects Devices within the SAME local network DIFFERENT networks together Forwarding decision Based on a MAC address table Based on a routing table Typical use Connecting computers in one office/LAN Connecting your LAN to the internet

qas.q: What is the difference between a MAC address and an IP address?
qas.a: A MAC address is a hardware-level, Layer 2 identifier permanently tied to a network interface, used for delivery within a local network. An IP address is a Layer 3, logical address that can change and is used to route data across different networks globally.

qas.q: Why does IPv6 exist?
qas.a: IPv4's 32-bit address space (~4.3 billion addresses) is insufficient for the number of internet-connected devices today. IPv6 uses 128 bits, giving an effectively unlimited address space and removing the need for some IPv4 workarounds like widespread NAT.

qas.q: What does a /24 subnet mean and how many usable hosts does it have?
qas.a: It means the first 24 bits of the IP address identify the network, leaving 8 bits (256 addresses) for hosts. Subtracting the reserved network and broadcast addresses leaves 254 usable host addresses.

qas.q: What does ARP do?
qas.a: It resolves a known IP address to the corresponding MAC address on a local network, by broadcasting a request and caching the reply, so a device can actually address an Ethernet frame to the right hardware interface.

qas.q: Difference between a switch and a router?
qas.a: A switch operates at Layer 2, connecting devices within the same local network using MAC addresses. A router operates at Layer 3, connecting different networks together using IP addresses and a routing table — it's what lets your LAN reach the internet.

qas.q: What is NAT and why is it used?
qas.a: Network Address Translation lets multiple devices on a private network share a single public IP address when communicating with the internet, translating addresses on the way out and mapping replies back to the correct internal device. It conserves public IPv4 addresses and adds a layer of address-hiding.

blocks.text: Addressing questions test precision: know exactly which OSI/TCP-IP layer each concept belongs to (MAC=Link, IP=Network) and be ready to compute a basic subnet (usable hosts in a /24 or /28). The switch-vs-router distinction is asked very frequently as a quick sanity check on fundamentals before moving to deeper transport-layer questions.

The Network layer (IP) only promises 'best effort' delivery of packets between machines — packets can be lost, duplicated, or arrive out of order, and IP does not care. The Transport layer sits on top and decides what guarantees (if any) to add on top of that unreliable foundation. The two dominant choices are TCP (reliable, ordered, connection-based) and UDP (unreliable, unordered, connectionless) — and choosing between them is one of the most consequential decisions in system design.

TCP is like a registered courier service with tracking and signature confirmation: every package is numbered, the sender knows if it didn't arrive and resends it, and packages are handed over in the original order even if a delivery van takes a different route. UDP is like dropping postcards in a mailbox: fast and cheap, but no confirmation of delivery, no resending if one is lost, and no guarantee they arrive in the order you wrote them. TCP UDP Connection Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) Connectionless — just send Reliability Guaranteed delivery via ACKs + retransmission No guarantee; packets can be lost silently Ordering Guaranteed in-order delivery No ordering guarantee Speed/overhead Slower, more overhead (headers, handshakes, ACKs) Faster, minimal overhead Flow/congestion control Yes, built in None (application must handle it if needed) Typical uses Web (HTTP), email, file transfer Video/voice calls, DNS, gaming, live streaming

“TCP is Trustworthy and Careful; UDP is Unreliable but Done quickly.” If an application would rather drop a frame than wait for a late one (live video call, real-time game) — UDP. If correctness matters more than speed (file download, web page, bank transaction) — TCP. The TCP 3-way handshake Before any data flows, TCP establishes a connection so both sides agree they're ready and synchronize initial sequence numbers, using three messages: Client Server |------------- SYN (seq=x) --------------->| "I want to connect, my seq starts at x" |<--------- SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1) -------| "OK, I acknowledge x; my seq starts at y" |------------- ACK (ack=y+1) -------------->| "Acknowledged; connection established" | | |<========= data now flows both ways =====>| Three messages: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK — hence '3-way' handshake. Closing a TCP connection is more involved — a 4-way handshake (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK), since either side can finish sending independently while still being willing to receive. How TCP guarantees reliability  Sequence numbers: every byte sent is numbered, so the receiver can detect missing or out-of-order data and reassemble it correctly.  Acknowledgements (ACKs): the receiver tells the sender which bytes it has received successfully.  Retransmission: if an ACK doesn't arrive within a timeout (or duplicate ACKs signal a gap), the sender resends the unacknowledged data.  Checksums: detect corrupted data so it can be discarded and retransmitted. Flow control vs congestion control — a critical distinction Flow Control Congestion Control Problem it solves Don't overwhelm the RECEIVER Don't overwhelm the NETWORK Who signals the limit The receiver (its buffer capacity) The network itself (packet loss, delay) Mechanism Receive window (advertised by receiver) Congestion window (sender's own estimate)
A listener saying 'slow down, I can't keep up' Realising the highway itself is jammed

“Flow control and congestion control are the same thing.” They solve different problems and use different signals. Flow control protects a slow receiver from being overwhelmed (using its advertised receive window). Congestion control protects the shared network from being overwhelmed (using the sender's own congestion window, adjusted based on observed packet loss/delay). TCP runs both simultaneously and sends at the minimum of the two windows. TCP congestion control phases (high level) TCP starts cautiously and adapts: Slow Start grows the congestion window exponentially at first (probing for available bandwidth), switching to Congestion Avoidance (linear growth) once a threshold is hit, and reacting sharply (halving the window, or resetting to slow start) when packet loss signals congestion — this is roughly the 'sawtooth' pattern visible in TCP throughput graphs.

qas.q: When would you choose UDP over TCP?
qas.a: When low latency matters more than guaranteed delivery and the application can tolerate or handle loss itself — live video/voice calls, multiplayer games, and DNS, where a late retransmitted packet is often worse than a dropped one.

qas.q: Walk through the TCP 3-way handshake.
qas.a: Client sends SYN with an initial sequence number. Server responds with SYN-ACK, acknowledging the client's sequence number and providing its own. Client responds with ACK, acknowledging the server's sequence number — at this point the connection is established and data can flow both ways.

qas.q: What is the difference between flow control and congestion control?
qas.a: Flow control prevents the sender from overwhelming the receiver's buffer, governed by the receiver's advertised window. Congestion control prevents the sender from overwhelming the shared network, governed by the sender's own estimate (congestion window) based on observed loss/delay. They are independent mechanisms working together.

qas.q: How does TCP know data was lost?
qas.a: Either a retransmission timeout expires without receiving an ACK for sent data, or the sender observes duplicate ACKs (the receiver repeatedly acknowledging the same point, signalling a gap) — both trigger retransmission.

qas.q: Is UDP completely unreliable, with zero guarantees?
qas.a: It guarantees nothing about delivery, ordering, or duplication — it only adds minimal structure (ports, a simple checksum) on top of raw IP. Any reliability an application needs over UDP (e.g. in WebRTC or QUIC) must be built at the application layer itself.

blocks.text: TCP vs UDP is asked in every networking interview, often pivoting straight into system design: “design a video calling app — TCP or UDP, and why?” Always justify with the latency-vs-reliability trade-off, not just naming the protocol. Be ready to draw the 3-way handshake from memory and clearly separate flow control from congestion control — interviewers specifically probe this distinction because it's commonly confused.

Computers route data using IP addresses, but humans want to type google.com, not 142.250.193.46. DNS is the internet's distributed directory service that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.

DNS is exactly like a contacts app translating a name into a phone number. You tap 'Mom' (the domain name); your phone looks up the actual number (the IP address) behind the scenes and dials that — you never had to memorise the digits yourself. How a DNS lookup actually works Browser asks: where is "www.example.com"? 1. Check browser cache -> OS cache -> (often) found, done. If not found: 2. Ask a RESOLVER (usually your ISP's DNS server) 3. Resolver asks a ROOT server: "who handles .com?" -> Root replies with the address of a .com TLD server 4. Resolver asks the TLD server: "who handles example.com?" -> TLD replies with the address of example.com's AUTHORITATIVE server 5. Resolver asks the authoritative server: "what is the IP for www.example.com?" -> Authoritative server replies with the actual IP address 6. Resolver returns the IP to the browser (and caches it for next time) A DNS lookup is a chain of queries narrowing from root -> TLD -> authoritative server. Server type Role Resolver (recursive) Does the legwork on your behalf, querying others and caching results Root server Knows which server handles each top-level domain (.com, .org, .in) TLD server Knows which authoritative server handles a specific domain within that TLD Authoritative server Holds the actual DNS records for that specific domain Common DNS record types Record Purpose A Maps a domain name to an IPv4 address AAAA Maps a domain name to an IPv6 address CNAME Aliases one domain name to another domain name MX Specifies the mail server responsible for the domain NS Specifies the authoritative name servers for the domain TTL How long a record may be cached before re-querying

DNS is fundamentally a caching system layered on a hierarchy. Every level (browser, OS, resolver) caches results using the record's TTL, which is exactly why DNS changes can take time to 'propagate' — old cached answers don't expire until their TTL runs out.

“DNS uses TCP because it needs to be reliable.” DNS primarily uses UDP (fast, low-overhead, and queries/responses are typically small enough to fit in one packet) and only falls back to TCP for larger responses (like DNSSEC-signed answers or zone transfers). It is a good example of UDP being chosen deliberately for speed where occasional retries are cheap.

HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the application-layer protocol browsers and servers use to exchange web content. It is a simple request-response protocol: the client sends a request, the server sends back a response. HTTP methods Method Purpose Idempotent? GET Retrieve data, no side effects expected Yes POST Create a new resource / submit data No PUT Replace a resource entirely Yes PATCH Partially update a resource No (typically) DELETE Remove a resource
Yes

Idempotent means calling it once or many times with the same input has the same effect as calling it once. GET, PUT, and DELETE are designed to be idempotent; POST is not (submitting the same form twice typically creates two records). Common HTTP status codes Code range Meaning Common examples 2xx Success 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content 3xx
Redirection
301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found, 304 Not Modified 4xx
Client error
400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found 5xx
Server error
500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable

“401 and 403 mean the same thing.” 401 Unauthorized actually means 'unauthenticated' — you haven't proven who you are (no/invalid credentials). 403 Forbidden means the server knows who you are but you don't have permission for this resource regardless. The naming is famously misleading — know the real distinction (authentication vs authorization). HTTP vs HTTPS HTTPS is HTTP layered on top of TLS (Transport Layer Security), which encrypts the connection so a third party intercepting traffic cannot read or tamper with it, and lets the client verify the server's identity via a certificate. Stateless protocol + cookies HTTP is stateless — each request is independent; the server doesn't inherently remember anything between requests. Cookies (small pieces of data the server asks the browser to store and resend on every subsequent request) are the standard way to layer statefulness (like login sessions) on top of a stateless protocol. HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 (brief) HTTP/1.1 HTTP/2 One request per connection at a time (without pipelining issues) Multiplexing: many requests/responses interleaved over ONE connection Text-based headers, repeated every request Binary framing, header compression Workarounds needed: multiple connections, sprite sheets Designed to remove the need for those workarounds

chapters.title: What Happens When You Type a URL and Hit Enter

blocks.text: This is the single most common networking interview question because it forces you to connect every layer covered so far into one coherent story. Here is the full sequence: 1. Browser checks its cache for the URL's IP (DNS cache, then OS, then resolver) 2. DNS resolution: domain name -> IP address (root -> TLD -> authoritative chain) 3. Browser opens a TCP connection to that IP on port 443 (HTTPS) via 3-way handshake 4. TLS handshake: client & server agree on encryption, server proves identity via cert 5. Browser sends an HTTP GET request for the page over the encrypted connection 6. Server processes the request (app logic, DB queries) and sends back an HTTP response 7. Browser receives HTML, parses it, discovers more resources (CSS, JS, images) 8. Steps 3-6 repeat (often reusing the connection) for each additional resource 9. Browser renders the page, executing JS, applying CSS, painting pixels to the screen Each step above maps to a section already covered in this book.

Practice this narration until it's automatic — interviewers use it to gauge whether you actually understand the stack or just memorised isolated facts. The strongest answers name the specific protocol and port at each step (DNS=UDP/53, HTTPS=TCP/443) and naturally mention caching at multiple levels (DNS cache, browser cache, CDN) as an optimization a real system would add.

qas.q: What port does HTTPS use by default?
qas.a: HTTP? HTTPS uses port 443; plain HTTP uses port 80. These are well-known/reserved ports (below 1024) standardized for these services.

qas.q: Why is a TLS handshake needed in addition to the TCP handshake?
qas.a: TCP's handshake only establishes a reliable, ordered connection — it provides no encryption or identity verification. TLS layers on top of that established TCP connection to negotiate encryption keys and let the client verify the server's certificate, securing the data that flows afterward.

qas.q: What is a CDN and how does it relate to the URL journey?
qas.a: A Content Delivery Network caches static content (images, JS, CSS, sometimes whole pages) on servers geographically closer to users, so the DNS resolution can return a nearby CDN server's IP instead of the origin server's, reducing latency for that step of the journey.

qas.q: What is the difference between authentication and authorization (401 vs 403)?
qas.a: Authentication is verifying who you are (login/credentials) — failing this gives 401. Authorization is checking what you, as an identified user, are allowed to do — failing this gives 403, even though the server knows exactly who you are.

blocks.text: Once data leaves your local network, it must hop across many intermediate routers to reach its destination — the internet has no single map known to everyone; instead, each router only needs to know which neighbour to forward toward for a given destination, and routing protocols build this knowledge cooperatively.
Routing table — the core idea

Each router keeps a routing table: a list of destination networks and which outgoing interface/next-hop router to send matching packets toward. When a packet arrives, the router checks the table for the best (usually most specific) match and forwards it on — it does not need to know the whole path, just the next hop. Packet destined for 203.0.113.50 arrives at Router R1 R1's routing table (simplified): Destination Next hop 10.0.0.0/8 -> directly connected 203.0.113.0/24 -> via Router R2 0.0.0.0/0 -> default route, via Router R3 (the internet at large) 203.0.113.50 matches the 203.0.113.0/24 entry -> forward to R2. R2 repeats the same process with ITS OWN routing table. And so on, hop by hop. Routing is a series of local 'best next hop' decisions, not a single global plan.
Traceroute

The traceroute (or tracert) tool reveals the hop-by-hop path to a destination by cleverly exploiting a packet's TTL (Time To Live) field: it sends packets with TTL=1, then 2, then 3, and so on. Each router decrements TTL by 1 and drops the packet (sending back an error) when it hits 0 — so each successive probe reveals one more hop along the route.

TTL's real job is to prevent infinite routing loops — if a misconfigured network loops a packet forever, TTL guarantees it eventually gets dropped instead of consuming bandwidth forever. Traceroute is simply a clever side-use of that safety mechanism.

qas.q: How does a router decide where to forward a packet?
qas.a: It checks the packet's destination IP against its routing table for the most specific matching entry, then forwards to the corresponding next-hop router or interface. It does not need to know the full end-to-end path — just the next hop, repeated at every router.

qas.q: What is the purpose of the TTL field in an IP packet?
qas.a: Primarily to prevent packets from looping forever on misconfigured or changing networks — each router decrements it, and the packet is dropped when TTL reaches zero. Traceroute exploits this mechanism to discover the path hop by hop.

blocks.text: Symmetric vs asymmetric encryption
Symmetric encryption Asymmetric encryption
Keys
One shared secret key for both encrypt and
decrypt A public key (encrypt) + private key (decrypt) pair Speed Fast Much slower (computationally heavier)
Key distribution
problem
How do both sides get the same secret key
safely?
Public key can be shared openly; private key
never leaves its owner
Typical use
Bulk data encryption (the actual traffic)
Securely exchanging the symmetric key in the first
place

Symmetric encryption is a single physical key that both locks and unlocks the same box — fast and simple, but both people need a copy of the exact same key, and getting that copy to the other person safely is the hard part. Asymmetric encryption is a mailbox with a slot: anyone can drop a letter in (encrypt with the public key), but only the person with the actual key to the box (the private key) can open it and read what's inside. Real systems use both together: asymmetric encryption to safely exchange a session key, then fast symmetric encryption for the actual bulk data — this is exactly what the TLS handshake does. The TLS handshake (simplified) Client Server |---- ClientHello (supported ciphers, random) ----->| |<--- ServerHello + CERTIFICATE + server random ----| | client verifies certificate against trusted CAs | |---- encrypted pre-master secret (using server's --->| | PUBLIC key from the certificate) | | both sides derive the same SYMMETRIC session key | |<==== rest of the connection encrypted with that =====>| | fast symmetric key (e.g. AES) ===============| Asymmetric crypto bootstraps trust + key exchange; symmetric crypto handles the actual traffic. The certificate is issued and digitally signed by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA). The browser already trusts a built-in list of CAs, so it can verify the server's certificate is authentic without ever having talked to that server before — this is what stops an attacker from simply presenting their own key and pretending to be the real server.

“HTTPS encrypts everything with one slow asymmetric key for the whole session.” Asymmetric crypto is used only briefly, during the handshake, to safely agree on a symmetric session key. All the actual back-and-forth page data is encrypted with that much faster symmetric key — this hybrid approach is exactly why HTTPS is fast enough for everyday browsing. Firewalls A firewall filters network traffic based on rules (allowed/blocked IPs, ports, protocols), sitting at the boundary between trusted and untrusted networks (e.g. between your company's internal network and the public internet). It can operate at different layers: simple packet-filtering firewalls inspect IP/port headers only; more advanced ones inspect traffic at the application layer for deeper rules. A few other security terms worth knowing Term Meaning DDoS attack Overwhelming a target with traffic from many sources so legitimate users can't be served Man-in-the-middle (MITM) An attacker secretly intercepts/alters communication between two parties who believe they're talking directly VPN Encrypts and tunnels your traffic through a trusted server, hiding your real IP/traffic from your local network/ISP Port scanning Probing a host's ports to discover which services are running/open

qas.q: Why does TLS use both symmetric and asymmetric encryption instead of just one?
qas.a: Asymmetric encryption solves the key-distribution problem (safely agreeing on a secret without having shared one beforehand) but is too slow for bulk data. Symmetric encryption is fast but needs a securely shared key first. TLS uses asymmetric crypto briefly to exchange a symmetric session key, then switches to fast symmetric encryption for the actual traffic — getting both security and speed.

qas.q: What does a Certificate Authority do, and why do we trust it?
qas.a: It verifies a server's identity and digitally signs a certificate vouching for it. Browsers/OSes ship with a built-in list of trusted CAs, so when a server presents a certificate signed by one of them, the client can verify authenticity without prior direct contact with that server.

qas.q: What is a man-in-the-middle attack and how does HTTPS help prevent it?
qas.a: An attacker intercepts communication between two parties, potentially reading or altering it while both sides believe they're talking directly to each other. HTTPS's certificate verification step is designed specifically to prevent this — an attacker without the real server's private key cannot successfully impersonate it.

qas.q: What is the basic job of a firewall?
qas.a: To filter traffic crossing a network boundary based on rules (IP, port, protocol, sometimes application-layer content), blocking unwanted or unauthorized traffic while allowing legitimate traffic through.

blocks.text: Security questions in SDE interviews usually stay at this conceptual level rather than deep cryptography — be ready to explain why HTTPS is both secure and fast (the symmetric/asymmetric hybrid) and to define MITM, DDoS, and CA cleanly. This often comes as a natural follow-up after the 'URL journey' question, since TLS is one of its steps.

Everything covered so far (TCP, UDP, IP, ports) is what happens underneath the code you write. A socket is the programming abstraction — an endpoint for sending and receiving data — that lets your application code actually use these protocols without manually building packets by hand. A socket is identified by a combination often called a 4-tuple: (source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port). This is exactly how the OS distinguishes thousands of simultaneous connections on the same machine — e.g. many browser tabs all talking to different (or the same) servers on port 443 are distinguished by their unique combination of source port and destination.
Ports — why they exist

An IP address identifies a machine, but a machine runs many services (a web server, a mail server, an SSH server) at once. Ports (0–65535) let the Transport layer direct incoming data to the correct application/process on that machine. Port Common service 20/21 FTP 22 SSH 25 SMTP (email sending) 53 DNS 80 HTTP 443 HTTPS 3306 MySQL (common default) 5432
PostgreSQL (common default)
The basic socket workflow (server side, TCP)

socket() -> create a new socket (like getting a phone line) bind() -> attach it to a specific IP + port (like getting a phone number) listen() -> mark it ready to accept incoming connections (start answering calls) accept() -> block until a client connects; returns a NEW socket for that client read/write()-> exchange data over that connection-specific socket close() -> end the connection Note: accept() returns a fresh socket per client — the original listening socket just keeps listening.

“One socket handles all clients connecting to a server.” The original socket created by bind()/listen() is only the listening socket. Every time accept() succeeds, the OS hands back a brand-new socket specific to that one client connection (uniquely identified by its 4-tuple), while the original listening socket keeps accepting more new connections. This is how one server handles thousands of clients on the same port.

qas.q: What is a socket?
qas.a: A programming abstraction representing one endpoint of a network connection, identified by a (source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port) tuple, that lets application code send/receive data without manually handling packets.

qas.q: Why do we need ports if we already have IP addresses?
qas.a: An IP address identifies a machine, but a machine can run many network services simultaneously. Ports let the Transport layer route incoming data to the correct service/process on that machine — without ports, a machine could only run one network service at a time.

qas.q: What does accept() actually return, and why does that matter for handling many clients?
qas.a: It returns a brand-new socket specific to that one client connection, while the original listening socket continues accepting further new connections. This is how a single server process can serve many simultaneous clients on one port.

qas.q: Can two different connections use the same destination port?
qas.a: Yes — many client connections to a server's port 443 are distinguished from each other by their different source IP/port combinations, even though the destination port is identical for all of them.

blocks.text: Socket questions test whether you understand the gap between 'the network protocol' and 'the code that uses it.' Be ready to explain the basic socket() / bind() / listen() / accept() flow and why a busy server doesn't need one socket per port — it needs one listening socket plus one new socket per active connection. CHAPTER 3B Interview Question Bank Curated, high-yield Computer Networks questions for SDE rounds Each section above already embedded its own questions; below are additional rapid-fire and cross-cutting ones. Drill them by covering the answer and saying it aloud. Models & Addressing

qas.q: What layer does a switch operate at, and what about a router?
qas.a: A switch operates at the Data Link layer (Layer 2), forwarding based on MAC addresses within a LAN. A router operates at the Network layer (Layer 3), forwarding based on IP addresses between different networks.

qas.q: What is the difference between a hub and a switch?
qas.a: A hub is a dumb device that broadcasts incoming data to every connected port regardless of destination (wasting bandwidth, causing collisions). A switch learns which MAC address is on which port and forwards data only to the relevant port, much more efficiently.

qas.q: What is broadcast vs unicast vs multicast?
qas.a: Unicast sends data to one specific recipient. Broadcast sends to every device on the local network. Multicast sends to a specific group of interested recipients, not everyone and not just one — e.g. streaming to a subscribed group. Transport & Reliability

qas.q: What is a port number range, and what's special about ports below 1024?
qas.a: Ports range 0–65535. Ports 0–1023 are 'well-known' ports reserved for standard services (HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, SSH=22) and typically require elevated privileges to bind to on most operating systems.

qas.q: What is head-of-line blocking?
qas.a: When one lost or delayed packet/request blocks all the data behind it from being processed, even if that later data already arrived — common in HTTP/1.1's per-connection ordering and a key problem HTTP/2 multiplexing and HTTP/3 (built on UDP/QUIC) address.

qas.q: What is the purpose of a checksum in networking?
qas.a: A small value computed from the data, sent alongside it, allowing the receiver to detect if the data was corrupted in transit (recomputing and comparing) — but a checksum detects corruption, it does not by itself fix it; that's what retransmission is for.

qas.q: What is Nagle's algorithm, briefly?
qas.a: A TCP optimization that delays sending small packets briefly to combine them into fewer, larger packets, reducing overhead — though it can add latency for latency-sensitive applications, which is why such apps often disable it (TCP_NODELAY). Application Layer & Real Systems

qas.q: What is a proxy server?
qas.a: An intermediary that forwards client requests to servers (or vice versa) on the client's/server's behalf. A forward proxy sits in front of clients (e.g. for caching or filtering outbound requests); a reverse proxy sits in front of servers (e.g. load balancing, hiding backend topology, TLS termination).

qas.q: What is a load balancer and why is it needed?
qas.a: A system that distributes incoming requests across multiple backend servers, preventing any single server from being overwhelmed and improving availability — if one backend fails, traffic is routed to the healthy ones.

qas.q: What is the difference between long polling, WebSockets, and Server-Sent Events?
qas.a: Long polling has the client repeatedly ask 'anything new?', with the server holding the request open until there's data. WebSockets establish one persistent, full-duplex connection where either side can push data anytime. Server-Sent Events provide a one-way, persistent stream from server to client over plain HTTP.

qas.q: What is REST, briefly?
qas.a: An architectural style for designing networked APIs around stateless requests, standard HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) mapped to resource operations, and resources identified by URLs — not a protocol itself, but a set of conventions on top of HTTP.

qas.q: Why is HTTP described as 'stateless,' and how do real apps maintain login sessions?
qas.a: Each HTTP request is independent with no inherent memory of previous requests. Login sessions are layered on top using cookies (a session ID the browser resends every request) or tokens (e.g. JWTs), letting the server reconstruct 'who is this and are they logged in' despite the underlying statelessness. blocks.text: Procedural programming organises a program around functions acting on data that is passed between them — data and behaviour are separate, and nothing stops any function from reaching in and corrupting any data structure. As programs grow to thousands of functions and structs, this becomes unmanageable: a bug anywhere could be caused by any function that touched that data, anywhere in the codebase. Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) bundles data and the behaviour that operates on it together into a single unit — an object — built from a class (the blueprint). The class decides exactly which functions are allowed to touch its internal data, dramatically narrowing where a bug affecting that data could originate.

Procedural code is like a shared office desk where anyone walking by can shuffle your papers, hide your stapler, or scribble on your notes — nothing stops them. OOP is like giving everyone a locked personal desk drawer (the object's private data) with a clearly labelled request slot on the front (its public methods) — others can ask you to do something with your contents, but they can't reach in and rearrange things themselves. Class vs object Class Object A blueprint / template defining structure and behaviour A concrete instance created from that blueprint Exists once in the code Many can exist simultaneously, each with its own data No memory allocated just by defining it Occupies actual memory when created

class Car { string model; int speed; void accelerate(); }; is the class — a blueprint, no actual car exists yet. Car myCar; Car yourCar; creates two objects — each with its own independent model/speed, both built from the same blueprint. The four pillars — the master list Pillar One-line idea Encapsulation Bundle data with the methods that operate on it; hide internal details Abstraction Expose only what's necessary; hide implementation complexity Inheritance A class can reuse and extend another class's structure and behaviour Polymorphism The same interface/call can behave differently depending on the actual object

Four pillars, one mnemonic: “EAIP — Every Acronym Is Painful” (just remember Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism in that order — it's also roughly the order interviewers tend to drill into them).

Encapsulation means bundling an object's data with the methods that operate on it, and restricting direct access to that data from outside — typically by making fields private and exposing controlled access via public methods (getters/setters, or more meaningful named operations).

Without encapsulation: class Account { public: double balance; }; — any code anywhere can do acc.balance = -500;, an invalid state with no validation possible. With encapsulation: class Account { private: double balance; public: void deposit(double amt) { if (amt > 0) balance += amt; // validation enforced HERE, in one place } bool withdraw(double amt) { if (amt > 0 && amt <= balance) { balance -= amt; return true; } return false; // can never go negative } double getBalance() const { return balance; } }; Now balance can only change through deposit()/withdraw(), and both enforce validation — an invalid balance becomes structurally impossible, not just discouraged by convention. Access specifiers in C++ Specifier Accessible from private Only within the class itself protected The class itself AND derived (child) classes public Anywhere — the class's external interface

Encapsulation in one sentence: “Make fields private, expose behaviour, not raw data.” If you find yourself writing a plain getter+setter pair with zero validation for every field, ask whether that field should be public to begin with — encapsulation is about meaningful control, not ceremony.

“Encapsulation just means making fields private and writing getters/setters for all of them.” Mechanically true but misses the point. Encapsulation's actual value is enforcing invariants (rules that must always hold, like balance ≥ 0) through controlled methods. A class with private fields but a setter for every single one with zero validation has the syntax of encapsulation without any of its benefit.

qas.q: What is encapsulation and why does it matter?
qas.a: Bundling data with the methods that operate on it, and hiding internal representation behind a controlled public interface. It matters because it lets a class enforce invariants (e.g. balance can never go negative) by funnelling all changes through validated methods, rather than allowing arbitrary external code to corrupt internal state.

qas.q: What is the difference between private and protected?
qas.a: private members are accessible only within the defining class itself. protected members are additionally accessible from derived classes, letting subclasses build on internal state that's still hidden from the outside world.

qas.q: Does having private fields automatically mean a class is well-encapsulated?
qas.a: No — if every private field has an unvalidated public getter and setter, external code can still set it to any value, defeating the purpose. True encapsulation means exposing behaviour that enforces rules, not just relocating direct field access behind a thin wrapper.

blocks.text: Encapsulation questions are rarely standalone — interviewers use them as a setup for “design a class for X” live-coding questions, where they specifically watch whether you make fields private and add validation in your methods without being told to. Demonstrating this unprompted is a strong signal.

Abstraction means exposing only the essential, relevant interface to the user of a class, while hiding the complexity of how it actually works internally. It is closely related to encapsulation but answers a different question: encapsulation asks 'how do we protect the data?'; abstraction asks 'how much do we even need to show the outside world?'

Driving a car is pure abstraction in action: you turn a key/press a button and press a pedal — you don't need to know about fuel injection timing, the combustion cycle, or transmission gear ratios. That complexity is hidden behind a simple interface (steering wheel, pedals). You're given exactly the controls you need and nothing more. Abstraction in C++: abstract classes & pure virtual functions An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly — it exists purely to define a common interface that derived classes must implement. It contains at least one pure virtual function (declared with = 0), which has no implementation in the base class and must be overridden by any concrete derived class.

class Shape { // abstract class public: virtual double area() const = 0; // pure virtual -- no body, forces override virtual ~Shape() {} // virtual destructor (see Section 4) }; class Circle : public Shape { double radius; public: Circle(double r) : radius(r) {} double area() const override { return 3.14159 * radius * radius; } }; // Shape s; <- ERROR: cannot instantiate an abstract class Shape* s = new Circle(5); // OK: pointer/reference to base, actual object is Circle double a = s->area(); // calls Circle::area() -- this IS polymorphism (Section 5) Any class inheriting from Shape must implement area() or it remains abstract itself. This guarantees every concrete shape in the codebase has a working area() method, enforced by the compiler. Abstract class vs interface C++ has no separate interface keyword (unlike Java/C#) — the convention is that a class with only pure virtual functions and no data members acts as a pure interface, while an abstract class can mix pure virtual functions with some implemented methods and data. Abstract class Pure interface (convention) Can have both implemented and pure virtual methods All methods are pure virtual Can have data members / constructors Typically no data members Models an 'is-a' relationship with shared implementation Models a 'can-do' capability contract

Encapsulation vs abstraction in five words each: Encapsulation = “hide the data, protect it.” Abstraction = “hide the complexity, simplify it.” They usually work together but answer different questions.

“Encapsulation and abstraction are the same thing.” They are related but distinct. Encapsulation is an implementation technique (private fields + public methods). Abstraction is a design-level decision about what to expose at all — you can have abstraction without much encapsulation (a simple interface over a struct) and vice versa, though good design usually uses both together.

Inheritance lets a class (the derived/child class) reuse and extend the fields and methods of another class (the base/parent class), modeling an 'is-a' relationship — a Dog is an Animal. Animal (name, eat(), sleep()) / \ Dog Cat (bark(), inherits (meow(), inherits name/eat/sleep) name/eat/sleep) Dog and Cat each inherit Animal's shared members and add their own specific behaviour. Types of inheritance Type Structure Single One base, one derived class
Multiple
One derived class inherits from more than one base class
Multilevel
A chain: A -> B -> C, where C inherits B which inherits A
Hierarchical
Multiple derived classes share one common base class
Hybrid
A combination of more than one of the above types
The diamond problem

Multiple inheritance creates a specific hazard called the diamond problem: if class D inherits from both B and C, and both B and C separately inherit from A, then D ends up with two separate copies of A's members — which one does D.someAFunction() actually call? It's ambiguous. A / \ B C \ / D D has TWO copies of A's members (one via B, one via C) -- ambiguous! The classic diamond inheritance hazard.

Fix using virtual inheritance in C++: class B : virtual public A {}; class C : virtual public A {}; class D : public B, public C {}; virtual inheritance tells the compiler to share a single copy of A across B and C, so D ends up with exactly one A, removing the ambiguity.

Diamond problem in one phrase: “Two paths, one ancestor, double trouble.” The fix is always the same word: virtual inheritance — share, don't duplicate, the common ancestor. Composition vs Inheritance Inheritance models 'is-a'; composition models 'has-a' — a class contains an instance of another class as a member, instead of inheriting from it. A Car has an Engine, rather than being an Engine. Inheritance ('is-a') Composition ('has-a') Tighter coupling — base class changes can ripple to all derived classes Looser coupling — components can be swapped independently Reuses behaviour by extending a base class Reuses behaviour by delegating to a contained object Fixed relationship decided at compile time Can be changed at runtime (swap which object is held)

“Always reach for inheritance when you want to reuse code.” The widely-cited design heuristic is “favor composition over inheritance.” Inheritance creates tight coupling between base and derived classes (changing the base can silently break every subclass) and forces an 'is-a' relationship that may not actually hold semantically. Composition is usually more flexible — prefer it unless there is a genuine, stable 'is-a' relationship.

qas.q: What is the diamond problem and how does C++ solve it?
qas.a: When a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common base, it ends up with two separate copies of that base's members, causing ambiguity. C++ resolves it with virtual inheritance, which ensures only one shared copy of the common base exists.

qas.q: What is the difference between inheritance and composition?
qas.a: Inheritance models an 'is-a' relationship by extending a base class, creating tighter coupling. Composition models a 'has-a' relationship by containing an instance of another class as a member, offering looser coupling and more runtime flexibility (e.g., swapping the contained object).

qas.q: Why is 'favor composition over inheritance' a common design guideline?
qas.a: Because inheritance tightly couples derived classes to their base's implementation details — a change to the base can silently break subclasses (the 'fragile base class' problem), and forces a rigid 'is-a' relationship decided at compile time. Composition is more flexible and decouples components.

qas.q: Can a constructor be inherited in C++?
qas.a: Not automatically by default, though derived classes can explicitly bring in base constructors with a using declaration (C++11 onward). Derived classes always need their own constructor logic for their own added members, even if it just delegates to the base constructor in its initializer list.

blocks.text: Inheritance questions often pivot straight into “design X using a class hierarchy” live problems (e.g. a shape hierarchy, a vehicle hierarchy, a payment system). Always pause and ask yourself whether the relationship is genuinely 'is-a' before reaching for inheritance — voicing 'composition over inheritance' as a deliberate consideration (even if you choose inheritance) signals design maturity.

Polymorphism ('many forms') means the same interface or function call can produce different behaviour depending on the actual type involved. It comes in two flavours: compile-time (decided while compiling — overloading, templates) and run-time (decided while the program is actually running — virtual functions).

Think of the word 'open' applied to different things: 'open the door,' 'open a book,' 'open a bank account' — same word, different actions depending on what you're opening. That's polymorphism: one interface ('open'), many context-dependent behaviours. Compile-time (static) polymorphism Function overloading: multiple functions with the same name but different parameter lists. The compiler decides which one to call based on the arguments, at compile time — no runtime decision needed.

void print(int x) { cout << "int: " << x; } void print(double x) { cout << "double: " << x; } void print(string x) { cout << "string: " << x; } print(5); // compiler picks print(int) at COMPILE time print(3.14); // compiler picks print(double) at COMPILE time Operator overloading is the same idea applied to operators — defining what + means for your own class (covered in Section 7). Templates are another compile-time mechanism: one generic function/class definition the compiler specialises for each type it's used with. Run-time (dynamic) polymorphism Achieved via virtual functions and inheritance: a base-class pointer or reference can point to any derived-class object, and calling a virtual function through that pointer invokes the derived class's version — decided while the program runs, based on the object's actual type, not the pointer's declared type.

class Animal { public: virtual void speak() const { cout << "..."; } }; class Dog : public Animal { public: void speak() const override { cout << "Woof!"; } }; class Cat : public Animal { public: void speak() const override { cout << "Meow!"; } }; void makeItSpeak(const Animal& a) { a.speak(); } // doesn't know the real type at compile time Dog d; Cat c; makeItSpeak(d); // prints "Woof!" -- decided at RUNTIME based on actual object makeItSpeak(c); // prints "Meow!" -- same call site, different behaviour Both calls go through the exact same function (makeItSpeak), but produce different output — this is the defining signature of run-time polymorphism: one call site, behaviour determined by the actual object at runtime. Compile-time polymorphism Run-time polymorphism Overloading, templates, operator overloading Virtual functions + inheritance (overriding) Resolved by the compiler before the program runs Resolved while the program is actually executing No runtime overhead Small overhead — a vtable lookup per call Also called 'static binding' Also called 'dynamic binding' / 'late binding' How virtual functions actually work: the vtable Each class with virtual functions gets a hidden vtable (virtual table) — an array of function pointers, one per virtual function, pointing to the correct implementation for that class. Every object of such a class secretly carries a hidden vptr (vtable pointer) to its class's vtable. Calling a virtual function actually means: follow the object's vptr to its vtable, then call whichever function pointer is stored there — which is why the actual object's class determines the behaviour, not the static type of the pointer/reference used to call it. Dog object in memory: Dog's vtable: +------------------+ +------------------------+ | vptr ------------>| | speak -> Dog::speak() | | (Dog's own data) | +------------------------+ +------------------+ a.speak() where 'a' is an Animal& bound to this Dog object: -> follow a's vptr (it's a Dog's vptr, regardless of the reference's declared type) -> look up "speak" in that vtable -> call Dog::speak() The vptr (set at object construction, based on the REAL class) drives the dispatch.

Polymorphism dispatch in one phrase: “The object knows what it really is; the pointer doesn't have to.” A Animal* pointing at a Dog still calls Dog's virtual functions, because the vptr lives with the object, not with the pointer/reference type.

“Without the virtual keyword, a base-class pointer calling an overridden function still gets the derived behaviour.” False — without virtual, C++ uses static binding: the function called is determined by the pointer/reference's declared type, not the object's actual type. This silently calls the base class's version even when the object is really a derived type — a classic C++ bug. Always mark functions meant to be overridden as virtual.

“A base class destructor doesn't need to be virtual if I never delete through a base pointer.” True in that narrow case, but it's a fragile assumption. If a base class has ANY virtual function (suggesting polymorphic use is intended) and someone later does delete basePtr; where basePtr actually points to a derived object, a non-virtual destructor means only the base part is destroyed — the derived class's destructor never runs, leaking any resources it owned. Rule of thumb: if a class has any virtual function, give it a virtual destructor too.

qas.q: What is the difference between compile-time and run-time polymorphism?
qas.a: Compile-time polymorphism (overloading, templates) is resolved entirely by the compiler before the program runs, with zero runtime overhead. Run-time polymorphism (virtual functions) is resolved during execution based on the object's actual type, via a vtable lookup, enabling one call site to behave differently for different derived types.

qas.q: How does C++ implement virtual function dispatch internally?
qas.a: Each polymorphic class has a vtable — an array of function pointers to the correct implementations. Every object of such a class carries a hidden vptr to its class's vtable. A virtual call follows the object's vptr to find and invoke the right function, regardless of the static (declared) type of the pointer/reference used.

qas.q: What happens if you forget the virtual keyword on a function meant to be overridden?
qas.a: C++ falls back to static binding: which function runs is decided by the pointer/reference's declared type, not the object's real type — so calling it through a base pointer silently invokes the base version even when the object is actually a derived type, which is rarely the intended behaviour.

qas.q: Why should a base class have a virtual destructor if it has any other virtual functions?
qas.a: Because polymorphic use is likely (objects accessed via base pointers/references), and deleting a derived object through a non-virtual base destructor only runs the base class's destructor, leaking any resources the derived class's destructor was responsible for cleaning up.

qas.q: What is the difference between overloading and overriding?
qas.a: Overloading defines multiple functions with the same name but different parameters in the SAME scope, resolved at compile time. Overriding redefines a base class's virtual function in a DERIVED class with the SAME signature, resolved at run time based on the actual object type.

blocks.text: The vtable explanation is a strong differentiator — many candidates can define polymorphism but few can explain how it's implemented. Practice drawing the vptr/vtable diagram from memory. The virtual destructor question is extremely common as a 'gotcha' follow-up after any polymorphism discussion — always mention it proactively when discussing base classes with virtual functions.

chapters.title: Constructors, Destructors & the Rule of Three/Five

blocks.text: A constructor initialises an object when it's created; a destructor cleans up when it's destroyed (frees memory, closes files, releases locks). Getting these right — especially when a class manages a resource like dynamically allocated memory — is one of the most interview-tested areas of C++ specifically. Types of constructors Type Purpose
Default constructor
Takes no arguments; sets up a default-initialised object
Parameterized constructor
Takes arguments to initialise members with specific values
Copy constructor
Creates a new object as a copy of an existing object of the same class
Move constructor
Creates a new object by 'stealing' resources from a temporary object
Why the default copy constructor can be dangerous

If you don't define your own copy constructor, C++ generates one automatically that does a shallow copy — it copies each member's value as-is. For a raw pointer member, this copies the pointer, not the data it points to — leaving two objects pointing at the same memory.

class Buffer { int* data; public: Buffer(int size) { data = new int[size]; } ~Buffer() { delete[] data; } // no custom copy constructor defined! }; Buffer a(10); Buffer b = a; // default (shallow) copy: b.data == a.data (SAME pointer!) // when a and b are both destroyed: // a's destructor: delete[] data; -- frees the memory // b's destructor: delete[] data; -- DOUBLE FREE! Same pointer, already freed. CRASH. This is the canonical C++ bug born from shallow copying: a double-free crash, or worse, silent memory corruption. The Rule of Three (classic C++) If a class manages a resource manually (like a raw pointer) and needs to define any one of: a destructor, a copy constructor, or a copy assignment operator — it almost certainly needs to define all three, to ensure copies are deep and cleanup is correct.

class Buffer { int* data; int size; public: Buffer(int s) : size(s) { data = new int[size]; } ~Buffer() { delete[] data; } // 1. Destructor Buffer(const Buffer& other) : size(other.size) { // 2. Copy constructor data = new int[size]; copy(other.data, other.data + size, data); // DEEP copy } Buffer& operator=(const Buffer& other) { // 3. Copy assignment if (this == &other) return *this; delete[] data; size = other.size; data = new int[size]; copy(other.data, other.data + size, data); return *this; } }; Now every copy allocates its own memory — no shared pointers, no double-free. The Rule of Five (modern C++11+) C++11 added move semantics, extending the rule to five special member functions: the original three, plus a move constructor and move assignment operator. A move 'steals' the resource from a temporary (rvalue) object instead of copying it — much faster, since no new allocation or copying is needed, just a pointer hand-off. Copy semantics Move semantics Duplicates the resource — both objects independently own their own copy Transfers ownership of the resource — the source is left empty/null Used for: Buffer b = a; (a still valid afterward) Used for: Buffer b = std::move(a); (a is now empty) Cost: a real allocation + copy Cost: just a few pointer assignments — very cheap

Rule of Three/Five in one sentence: “If you write one of destructor/copy/move, you almost certainly need all five.” The compiler's auto-generated shallow versions are fine for simple classes with no raw resource ownership — the rule applies specifically when a class itself manages a resource (raw pointer, file handle, lock) directly.

“If I just don't define a destructor, the compiler-generated one is always safe.” Only true if the class owns no resources that need explicit cleanup. If a class holds a raw pointer obtained via new, the compiler's default destructor does nothing special — it does NOT call delete for you, causing a memory leak. (Modern C++ guidance: prefer smart pointers like unique_ptr/shared_ptr specifically so you don't have to hand-write the Rule of Five at all.)

qas.q: What is a shallow copy and why is it dangerous?
qas.a: A shallow copy duplicates a member's value as-is — for a pointer member, this copies the pointer itself, not the data it points to, leaving two objects pointing at the same memory. When both are destroyed, the resource is freed twice (a double-free), typically crashing the program or corrupting memory.

qas.q: What is the Rule of Three?
qas.a: If a class needs a custom destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment operator, it almost certainly needs all three, to ensure resources are deep-copied correctly and cleaned up exactly once per object.

qas.q: What is the difference between a copy constructor and a move constructor?
qas.a: A copy constructor duplicates a resource, leaving both the source and the new object independently valid. A move constructor transfers ownership of a resource from a (typically temporary) source object to the new object, leaving the source empty — far cheaper since no actual data copying happens.

qas.q: Why does modern C++ encourage smart pointers over raw pointers for resource ownership?
qas.a: Smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr) automatically manage the Rule of Five's concerns internally — proper copy/move semantics and automatic cleanup in the destructor — so the class using them doesn't need to hand-write any of it, eliminating a major source of memory bugs.

blocks.text: The shallow-copy double-free bug is one of the most common C++-specific interview questions, often phrased as “what's wrong with this code?” with a class holding a raw pointer and no custom copy constructor. Be ready to spot it instantly and explain the fix (Rule of Three/Five, or just recommending smart pointers).
C++ lets you redefine what an operator means for your own class, so objects can be used as naturally as built-in types — e.g. adding two Vector2D objects with + instead of calling v1.add(v2).

class Vector2D { double x, y; public: Vector2D(double x, double y) : x(x), y(y) {} Vector2D operator+(const Vector2D& other) const { return Vector2D(x + other.x, y + other.y); } }; Vector2D a(1, 2), b(3, 4); Vector2D c = a + b; // calls a.operator+(b) -- reads naturally

“You can overload any operator to mean anything you want.” Technically true syntactically, but a strong design guideline is to preserve the operator's intuitive meaning — overloading + to perform subtraction would compile fine but violate the principle of least surprise. Also: a few operators (like ::, ., sizeof) cannot be overloaded at all in C++.

qas.q: Why would you overload an operator instead of writing a named method?
qas.a: To let objects of your class be used with natural, expected syntax (a + b, a == b), improving readability for types that have an intuitive mathematical or comparison meaning — but only when the overloaded meaning matches the operator's expected intent.

qas.q: Can all C++ operators be overloaded?
qas.a: No — a small set, including the scope resolution operator (::), the member access operator (.), and sizeof, cannot be overloaded.

blocks.text: SOLID is five design guidelines for keeping object-oriented code flexible and maintainable as it grows. Each principle exists to prevent a specific kind of fragility — learn them as trade-off stories with a concrete before/after, not as abstract rules.
S — Single Responsibility Principle
A class should have only one reason to change — i.e., one responsibility. Mixing unrelated responsibilities in one class means a change to one responsibility risks breaking the other.

Before: class Report { void generate(); void saveToFile(); void printToConsole(); }; — mixes report generation, file I/O, and printing in one class. A change to file-saving logic risks breaking report generation by accident (they're tangled in the same class). After: split into ReportGenerator, ReportFileSaver, and ReportPrinter — each has exactly one reason to change. O — Open/Closed Principle A class should be open for extension but closed for modification — you should be able to add new behaviour without changing existing, already-tested code.

Before: double calcArea(Shape s) { if (s.type==CIRCLE) ...; else if (s.type==SQUARE) ...; } — adding a new shape means editing this function and risking every existing case. After: give each shape its own virtual area() method (polymorphism). Adding a new shape means writing a new class — the existing calcArea-style code is never touched again. L — Liskov Substitution Principle Objects of a derived class must be substitutable for objects of the base class without breaking correctness — if code works with a Base, it must keep working correctly when given any Derived instead.

Classic violation: class Rectangle { virtual void setWidth(int); virtual void setHeight(int); }; class Square : public Rectangle { void setWidth(int w) override { width=height=w; } ... }; — Square overrides setWidth to also change height, breaking any code that assumed setting a Rectangle's width leaves its height alone. Square LOOKS like a valid Rectangle subtype but violates callers' expectations. This shows LSP is about behavioural correctness, not just having the right method signatures — Square technically 'is-a' Rectangle mathematically, but not substitutably so in code. I — Interface Segregation Principle Don't force a class to implement methods it doesn't need. Prefer several small, specific interfaces over one large, general-purpose one.

Before: class Worker { virtual void work(); virtual void eat(); }; implemented by both Human and Robot — Robot is forced to implement eat(), which makes no sense for it. After: split into separate Workable and Eatable interfaces; Human implements both, Robot implements only Workable. Neither class is forced to implement irrelevant behaviour. D — Dependency Inversion Principle High-level code should depend on abstractions (interfaces), not on concrete, low-level implementations directly. This is what makes components swappable and testable.

Before: class NotificationService { MySQLLogger logger; }; — NotificationService is hard-wired to one specific logging implementation; swapping it (or mocking it for a test) means editing NotificationService's code. After: class NotificationService { ILogger* logger; }; where ILogger is an abstract interface — NotificationService now depends only on the abstraction. Any logger implementing ILogger (MySQLLogger, a mock for tests, a FileLogger) can be passed in without touching NotificationService at all. Letter Principle Prevents S Single Responsibility One class doing too many unrelated jobs O Open/Closed Editing tested code every time you add a feature L Liskov Substitution Subclasses that subtly break callers' expectations I Interface Segregation Forcing classes to implement irrelevant methods D Dependency Inversion High-level code hard-wired to low-level details

SOLID, one phrase each: Single job per class. Open to extend, closed to edit. Looks like the parent, BEHAVES like the parent. Interfaces should be small and specific. Depend on abstractions, not concrete details.

“SOLID principles should be applied maximally everywhere, all the time.” SOLID is a set of heuristics for managing complexity in growing systems, not an absolute law. Over-applying them to a tiny script (e.g. splitting a 10-line utility into five interfaces 'for flexibility') adds indirection and complexity with no real payoff. Senior engineers apply SOLID where change and growth are actually expected.

qas.q: Explain the Open/Closed Principle with an example.
qas.a: A class should allow new behaviour to be added without modifying its existing, tested code. Classic example: instead of a big if/else chain checking shape type to compute area, give each shape class its own overridden area() method — adding a new shape means writing a new class, not editing existing logic.

qas.q: What does the Liskov Substitution Principle actually require, beyond matching method signatures?
qas.a: It requires that substituting a derived class object for a base class object preserves the program's correctness — the subclass must honor the base class's behavioural contract, not just its method signatures. The Square-extends-Rectangle example is the classic case where signatures match but behaviour breaks callers' assumptions.

qas.q: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle and why does it help with testing?
qas.a: High-level code should depend on abstractions (interfaces) rather than concrete implementations. This makes it easy to substitute a mock/fake implementation during testing without modifying the class under test, since it only ever depended on the interface, not a specific concrete class.

qas.q: Give a real example of violating the Single Responsibility Principle.
qas.a: A class that both calculates business logic and handles its own file I/O / database persistence / UI rendering — changes to the storage format now risk breaking the business logic, and vice versa, even though they are conceptually unrelated changes.

blocks.text: SOLID is asked constantly, but reciting the five letters scores poorly — interviewers want a concrete before/after example for at least two or three of them, ideally ones you can sketch in code on a whiteboard. The Liskov Substitution Rectangle/Square example is extremely commonly asked because it's surprising — practice explaining exactly why it violates LSP despite seeming like valid inheritance.

A design pattern is a reusable, named solution to a recurring design problem — a shared vocabulary so engineers can say "just use a Factory here" instead of re-explaining the whole idea each time. Below are the four patterns most frequently asked about in SDE interviews. 1. Singleton Ensures a class has exactly one instance for the entire program, with a single global access point to it. Used for things that should genuinely be unique — a configuration manager, a logging service, a connection pool.

class Logger { private: static Logger* instance; Logger() {} // private constructor -- can't 'new' it externally public: static Logger& getInstance() { static Logger instance; // created once, on first call (thread-safe in C++11+) return instance; } void log(string msg) { /* ... */ } }; Logger::getInstance().log("started"); // always the SAME Logger object, everywhere

“Singleton is just a fancy global variable, so it's basically harmless.” Singletons are controversial precisely because they behave like global state: they create hidden dependencies between unrelated parts of code, make unit testing harder (you can't easily swap in a mock), and can cause subtle bugs if used for anything that shouldn't truly be global. Use sparingly, and be ready to discuss the downsides unprompted — interviewers often ask 'what's wrong with Singleton?' specifically to see if you know. 2. Factory Provides an interface for creating objects without exposing the exact class being instantiated to the calling code — the caller asks for 'a shape' or 'a payment method' and the factory decides which concrete class to actually create.

class Shape { public: virtual void draw() = 0; }; class Circle : public Shape { public: void draw() override { /*...*/ } }; class Square : public Shape { public: void draw() override { /*...*/ } }; class ShapeFactory { public: static Shape* create(string type) { if (type == "circle") return new Circle(); if (type == "square") return new Square(); return nullptr; } }; Shape* s = ShapeFactory::create("circle"); // caller doesn't write 'new Circle()' directly The calling code never says new Circle() directly — it depends only on the Shape interface and the factory, which is exactly the Dependency Inversion Principle from Section 8 in action. 3. Observer Lets one object (the subject) notify a list of dependent objects (observers) automatically whenever its state changes, without the subject needing to know any details about those observers beyond a common interface. This is the foundation of event systems, pub/sub, and UI reactivity. Subject (e.g. a WeatherStation) | notify() loops through all registered observers v +--------+ +--------+ +--------+ | Obs A | | Obs B | | Obs C | <- each implements update() +--------+ +--------+ +--------+ (PhoneDisplay) (WebDashboard) (Logger) Subject doesn't know what the observers DO with the update -- only that they implement update().

Real-world parallel: subscribing to a YouTube channel. The channel (subject) doesn't know or care what each subscriber (observer) does when notified of a new video — one might get a push notification, another an email, a third might just update an unread counter. The subject only knows 'notify everyone who's subscribed.' 4. Strategy Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms, encapsulates each one behind a common interface, and lets the algorithm be selected/swapped at runtime without changing the code that uses it.
