class SortStrategy { public: virtual void sort(vector<int>&) = 0; }; class QuickSort : public SortStrategy { public: void sort(vector<int>& v) override {...} }; class MergeSort : public SortStrategy { public: void sort(vector<int>& v) override {...} }; class Sorter { SortStrategy* strategy; public: Sorter(SortStrategy* s) : strategy(s) {} void setStrategy(SortStrategy* s) { strategy = s; } // swap algorithm at RUNTIME void sortData(vector<int>& v) { strategy->sort(v); } }; Sorter doesn't care which sorting algorithm it's using — it can be swapped at runtime (e.g. switch to a different strategy based on input size), and this is the same underlying idea as the Strategy used in real libraries (e.g. passing a custom comparator). Pattern Solves Real-world example Singleton Ensure exactly one instance, globally accessible Logger, config manager, connection pool Factory Create objects without exposing concrete classes to callers UI toolkit creating OS-specific buttons Observer Notify many dependents automatically on state change Event listeners, pub/sub systems Strategy Swap interchangeable algorithms at runtime Sorting comparators, payment method selection

One line each: Singleton = “only one, ever.” Factory = “don't say 'new' yourself, ask for one.” Observer = “tell everyone subscribed, don't ask what they'll do.” Strategy = “swap the algorithm, keep the caller the same.”

qas.q: What problem does the Singleton pattern solve, and what's the main criticism of it?
qas.a: It guarantees exactly one instance of a class exists with one global access point — useful for things like a single shared logger or config. The main criticism is that it behaves like global state: it creates hidden dependencies, makes unit testing harder (can't easily mock/swap it), and is often overused for things that didn't truly need to be globally unique.

qas.q: How does the Factory pattern relate to the Dependency Inversion Principle?
qas.a: The Factory hides the concrete class being instantiated behind an abstraction — calling code depends only on the returned interface/abstract type and the factory itself, never on a specific concrete class, exactly matching DIP's goal of depending on abstractions, not concrete details.

qas.q: What's the key difference between the Observer and Strategy patterns?
qas.a: Observer is about ONE-TO-MANY notification — a subject pushing updates to many registered dependents automatically. Strategy is about swapping ONE interchangeable algorithm/behaviour at a time, chosen by the calling code — there's no 'notify many subscribers' aspect to it at all.

qas.q: Give a real-world example of the Observer pattern outside of GUIs.
qas.a: A pub/sub messaging system, a stock price feed notifying multiple subscribed trading algorithms of a price change, or reactive state management in frontend frameworks — any case where many independent parties need to react to one source's state changes.

blocks.text: Design pattern questions often appear as “how would you design X” — listen for the actual underlying need (exactly one instance? object creation hidden from callers? many things reacting to one change? swappable algorithms?) and name the matching pattern explicitly, then sketch a minimal class diagram. Knowing the Singleton's downsides unprompted is a frequently used filter question. CHAPTER 4B Interview Question Bank Curated, high-yield OOP questions for SDE rounds Each section above already embedded its own questions; below are additional rapid-fire and cross-cutting ones that recur across product and service companies. Pillars & Fundamentals

qas.q: Can a class exist without any objects of it ever being created?
qas.a: Yes — a class is just a definition/blueprint in the code; it occupies no object memory until something actually instantiates it. An abstract class can never have objects of itself directly created at all, only of its concrete derived classes.

qas.q: What is 'this' pointer in C++?
qas.a: An implicit pointer available inside non-static member functions, pointing to the object the method was called on. It's how a method accesses its own object's members and is used explicitly to resolve naming conflicts or to return *this for method chaining.

qas.q: What is a friend function/class in C++?
qas.a: A function or class granted access to another class's private/protected members, despite not being a member of that class itself — used sparingly, for tightly coupled helper functions/classes that need deep access (e.g. operator<< for printing).

qas.q: What is the difference between a struct and a class in C++?
qas.a: Functionally almost identical in C++ — the only real difference is the default access specifier: struct members are public by default, class members are private by default. By convention, struct is used for simple plain-data aggregates; class for types with behaviour and invariants. Inheritance & Polymorphism

qas.q: What is function hiding (name hiding) in C++ inheritance?
qas.a: If a derived class defines a function with the same name as a base class function (but a different signature, not an override), it hides ALL overloads of that name from the base class for that derived type, even ones with different parameters — a frequent source of confusing bugs.

qas.q: Can a constructor be virtual?
qas.a: Can a destructor? A constructor cannot be virtual — at the moment a constructor runs, the object's type isn't fully established yet, so dynamic dispatch wouldn't make sense. A destructor CAN and often SHOULD be virtual, specifically to ensure correct cleanup when deleting through a base class pointer.

qas.q: What is an abstract class with no pure virtual functions used for?
qas.a: A class can be made abstract simply by giving it a pure virtual destructor or a protected constructor, intentionally preventing direct instantiation even without any other pure virtual methods — used when you want a common base purely for shared implementation, not as an instantiable type.

qas.q: What is slicing in C++?
qas.a: When a derived class object is assigned/passed BY VALUE to a base class variable, only the base class portion is copied — the derived-specific data is 'sliced off' and lost. This is a key reason polymorphic objects should be passed/stored via pointers or references, not by value. Design & Patterns

qas.q: What is the difference between aggregation and composition?
qas.a: Both are forms of 'has-a' relationships (composition in the general OOP sense), but aggregation is a weaker relationship where the contained object can exist independently of the container (a University has Students, but Students exist without the University). True composition implies the contained object's lifetime is tied to the container (a House has Rooms; the Rooms don't outlive the House).

qas.q: What is method overriding's relationship to the Open/Closed Principle?
qas.a: Overriding lets you add new behaviour for a new subclass without modifying the base class or any other existing subclass's code — it's one of the core mechanisms that makes the Open/Closed Principle achievable in practice.

qas.q: Why might over-using inheritance hurt the Open/Closed Principle?
qas.a: Deep inheritance hierarchies can make adding new behaviour require understanding (and sometimes touching) several levels of base classes, and a change to a shared base can ripple unpredictably through every subclass — the opposite of being safely 'closed for modification.' Favoring composition often keeps the system more genuinely extensible.

qas.q: In your own words, why are design patterns useful beyond just solving the immediate problem?
qas.a: They give engineers a shared vocabulary ('use a Factory here,' 'that's basically an Observer') that communicates an entire design intent in a few words, making code reviews, design discussions, and onboarding faster — the value is as much about communication as about the code itself. CHAPTER 4C The night-before-the-interview chapter: tables, formulas, and tricks Most-asked comparison tables Encapsulation vs Abstraction Encapsulation Abstraction Hides DATA, protects internal state Hides COMPLEXITY, simplifies what's exposed Implementation technique (private + public methods) Design-level decision (what to expose at all) Inheritance vs Composition Inheritance ('is-a') Composition ('has-a') Tighter coupling to base class Looser coupling, swappable at runtime Fixed relationship at compile time Can change which object is held at runtime Compile-time vs Run-time Polymorphism Compile-time Run-time Overloading, templates Virtual functions + overriding Resolved by compiler, zero overhead Resolved at runtime via vtable lookup Overloading vs Overriding Overloading Overriding Same name, different parameters, same scope Same name AND signature, base vs derived class Resolved at compile time Resolved at run time Abstract Class vs Pure Interface (C++ convention) Abstract class Pure interface Mix of implemented + pure virtual methods All methods pure virtual Can have data members Typically no data members Copy vs Move Semantics Copy Move Duplicates the resource; source stays valid Transfers ownership; source becomes empty Cost: allocation + copy Cost: a few pointer assignments — cheap Key facts to have ready Fact Detail The four pillars Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism Rule of Three If you write a destructor/copy-ctor/copy-assign, you need all three Rule of Five Rule of Three + move constructor + move assignment (C++11+) Diamond problem fix virtual inheritance — shares one copy of the common ancestor Virtual destructor rule If a class has any virtual function, give it a virtual destructor SOLID Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion Vtable mechanism vptr (per object) -> vtable (per class) -> correct function pointer Operators that can't be overloaded :: . sizeof (and a couple of others) One-line definitions (rapid fire) Term One-liner Class a blueprint defining structure and behaviour; no memory until instantiated Object a concrete instance of a class, occupying actual memory Encapsulation bundling data with methods, hiding internals to enforce invariants Abstraction exposing only the essential interface, hiding implementation complexity Inheritance a class reusing/extending another class's members; models 'is-a' Polymorphism one interface, behaviour determined by the actual underlying type Pure virtual function a virtual function with no implementation; forces derived override Abstract class a class with at least one pure virtual function; cannot be instantiated Vtable per-class array of function pointers used for dynamic dispatch Vptr per-object hidden pointer to its class's vtable Diamond problem ambiguity from inheriting the same base twice via two parent paths Shallow copy copies a pointer's value, not the data it points to (shared ownership bug) Deep copy duplicates the actual resource, giving each object independent ownership Move semantics transferring resource ownership instead of copying it (C++11+) Slicing losing derived-class data when assigning a derived object to a base by value Singleton a pattern ensuring exactly one instance with one global access point Factory a pattern that creates objects without exposing the concrete class to callers Observer a pattern where a subject auto-notifies many registered dependents Strategy a pattern that makes an algorithm swappable at runtime behind one interface All memory tricks in one place Topic Trick Four pillars “EAIP” — Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism Encapsulation rule “Make fields private, expose behaviour, not raw data” Encapsulation vs Abstraction Encapsulation hides DATA; Abstraction hides COMPLEXITY Diamond problem “Two paths, one ancestor, double trouble” — fixed by virtual inheritance Favor composition Inheritance = tight coupling; composition = swap parts at runtime Polymorphism dispatch “The object knows what it really is; the pointer doesn't have to” Virtual destructor rule Any virtual function in a class -> give it a virtual destructor too Rule of Three/Five Write one of destructor/copy/move -> you almost certainly need all five SOLID one-liners One job (S), extend don't edit (O), behave like the parent (L), small interfaces (I), depend on abstractions (D) Pattern one-liners Singleton=only one ever; Factory=don't say 'new' yourself; Observer=notify many; Strategy=swap the algorithm Final 60-second confidence check IF YOU CAN ANSWER THESE OUT LOUD, YOU ARE READY 1. The four pillars, each with a concrete bug it prevents? 2. Why is a shallow-copy bug dangerous, and what's the Rule of Three fix? 3. Draw the vptr/vtable diagram and explain dynamic dispatch? 4. Why does a class with any virtual function need a virtual destructor? 5. The diamond problem and its fix (virtual inheritance)? 6. 'Favor composition over inheritance' — explain why, with an example? 7. All five SOLID principles, each with a before/after example? 8. Why does the Rectangle/Square example violate Liskov Substitution? 9. Singleton, Factory, Observer, Strategy — one line each, plus Singleton's downside? 10. Compile-time vs run-time polymorphism — mechanism and cost of each? End of Book 4 — OOP. Last book in the series: Linux + Software Engineering Fundamentals. Same method as before: read a section, shut it, explain it aloud, then drill the orange and teal boxes. Recall, not recognition, is what interviews test.

blocks.text: In Linux, everything is a file — documents, directories, devices, even running processes are represented in the filesystem. Everything hangs off a single root, written as / (unlike Windows' separate C:, D: drives). Understanding the standard directory layout helps you navigate any Linux system. Path Purpose / The root of the entire filesystem — everything lives under here /home Users' personal directories (e.g. /home/syed) /bin, /usr/bin Essential command binaries (ls, cp, etc.) /etc System-wide configuration files /var Variable data: logs (/var/log), caches, mail /tmp Temporary files, typically cleared on reboot /proc A virtual filesystem exposing kernel and process info /dev Device files (disks, terminals) /opt Optional / third-party software

The Linux filesystem is like a company's single org chart with one CEO at the top (/). Every department (/home, /etc, /var) branches off that single root, and every employee, document, and even meeting room has exactly one place in the hierarchy — there's never any question of 'which drive is it on,' because there's only one tree. Navigation commands Command What it does pwd Print working directory (where am I?) ls List directory contents (ls -la = long format, including hidden files) cd path Change directory (cd .. = up one level, cd ~ = home, cd - = previous) mkdir / rmdir Make / remove a directory find path -name '*.txt' Search for files by name, type, size, or modification time tree Show directory structure as a visual tree Absolute vs relative paths An absolute path starts from root and is unambiguous everywhere: /home/syed/project/main.cpp. A relative path is interpreted from your current directory: if you're in /home/syed, then project/main.cpp means the same file. . means 'current directory' and .. means 'parent directory.'
Command
What it does
cat file
Print a whole file (cat a b > c concatenates a and b into c)
less file View a file one screen at a time (q to quit) — good for large files head -n 20 file
First 20 lines; tail -n 20 = last 20 lines
tail -f log Follow a file live as new lines are appended — essential for watching logs cp src dst
Copy (cp -r for directories, recursively)
mv src dst
Move or rename a file
rm file Remove (rm -r directory, rm -f to force — be careful, no recycle bin!) touch file
Create an empty file, or update its timestamp
wc -l file Count lines (also -w words, -c characters)

“rm just moves files to a trash/recycle bin like on a desktop.” No — rm deletes immediately and permanently, with no recovery. rm -rf / (or a careless variable like rm -rf $DIR/ where DIR is empty) is the infamous command that can wipe an entire system. Treat rm -rf with extreme caution, especially with wildcards.

Every file has an owner, a group, and a set of permissions for three categories: the user (owner), the group, and others (everyone else). Each category can have read (r), write (w), and execute (x) permission. Output of `ls -l`: - rwx r-x r-- 1 syed devs 4096 main.cpp

|   |    |    |          |     |
   |   |    |    |        owner  group
   |   |    |    +--- others:  read only
   |   |    +-------- group:   read + execute
   |   +------------- user:    read + write + execute
   +----------------- file type (- = file, d = directory, l = symlink)
The 9 permission characters are three groups of rwx: user, group, others.
Numeric (octal) permissions
Permissions are often written as a 3-digit octal number where read=4, write=2, execute=1, summed per category:

Octal Symbolic Meaning 7 rwx read + write + execute (4+2+1) 6 rw- read + write (4+2) 5 r-x read + execute (4+1) 4 r-- read only 755 rwxr-xr-x owner: all; group & others: read + execute (common for scripts/dirs) 644 rw-r--r-- owner: read+write; others: read only (common for normal files) Command
What it does
chmod 755 file
Set permissions numerically
chmod +x file
Add execute permission (symbolic form)
chown user:group file
Change ownership
sudo command Run a command as the superuser (root) — for privileged operations

Permissions: r=4, w=2, x=1, added per category. The two you'll use constantly: 755 = 'owner can do everything, others can read and run' (scripts, directories) and 644 = 'owner read/write, others read only' (normal files). Memorise these two and you'll rarely need to think harder.

qas.q: What does chmod 755 mean?
qas.a: Owner gets read+write+execute (7=4+2+1); group and others get read+execute (5=4+1). It's the typical permission for executable scripts and for directories (where execute means 'can enter/traverse').

qas.q: What is the difference between a file's owner, group, and others?
qas.a: Owner is the user who owns the file. Group is a set of users sharing access. Others is everyone else. Each of the three gets its own read/write/execute permission set, letting you grant different access to each category.

qas.q: Why does a directory need execute permission?
qas.a: For a directory, execute permission means the ability to enter/traverse it (cd into it and access files within by name). Without it, you can't access a directory's contents even if you have read permission to list the names.

qas.q: What does sudo do, and why is it preferred over logging in as root?
qas.a: sudo runs a single command with elevated (superuser) privileges. It's preferred over logging in as root because it limits privileged actions to specific commands, logs who ran what, and reduces the risk of accidentally damaging the system during routine work.

blocks.text: Linux questions for SDE roles are practical, not trivia: navigating to a log, fixing a permission, understanding what chmod 755 means. Be ready to explain the permission model and the numeric/symbolic forms fluently — it's a common quick fundamentals check before deeper command-composition questions in the next sections.

The real power of the Linux command line is composing small tools — each does one thing well, and you chain them together with pipes to solve complex tasks. This 'do one thing well, then combine' idea is the Unix philosophy, and demonstrating it is exactly what command-line interview questions test. Pipes and redirection Symbol Meaning | Pipe: send one command's output as the next command's input > Redirect output to a file (overwrite)
Redirect output to a file (append) < Take input from a file 2> Redirect error output (stderr) specifically 2>&1 Redirect stderr into the same place as stdout

Pipes are like an assembly line. Each worker (command) does one specific task and passes the result to the next worker down the line. No single worker builds the whole product — the power comes from chaining specialists together. cat | grep | sort | uniq is four specialists on a line. The big three text tools Tool Specialty
grep Search lines matching a pattern grep 'ERROR' app.log sed Stream-edit text (find/replace, transform) sed 's/foo/bar/g' file awk Field/column-based processing awk '{print $1}' file

Real task: from a web server log, find the top 5 IP addresses making the most requests. awk '{print $1}' access.log # extract the 1st field (the IP) from each line | sort # sort so identical IPs are adjacent | uniq -c # collapse duplicates, prefixing each with its count | sort -nr # sort by count, numeric, descending | head -5 # keep the top 5 No single tool does this — five specialists chained together do. Being able to build a pipeline like this live is one of the strongest signals you can give in a Linux interview.

More quick one-liners: grep -r 'TODO' . — recursively find 'TODO' in all files under the current directory. grep -c 'ERROR' app.log — count matching lines (-i = case-insensitive, -v = invert/exclude). cat file | sort | uniq -d — show only duplicate lines.

grep finds, sed edits, awk extracts columns. If a question involves 'search logs' it's grep; 'replace text' it's sed; 'pull out the 3rd column / sum a field' it's awk. Pair any of them with sort | uniq -c | sort -nr and you can answer most 'analyse this file' questions.

A running program is a process (see Book 1 for the OS-level theory). From the command line, you observe and control processes with a standard set of tools, and communicate with them using signals. Command
What it does
ps aux
Snapshot of all running processes with details
top / htop
Live, continuously-updating view of processes and resource usage
kill PID Send a signal to a process (default: SIGTERM, polite termination) kill -9 PID
Send SIGKILL — force kill, cannot be caught or ignored
jobs / fg / bg
Manage jobs in the current shell session
command &
Run a command in the background
nohup command &
Run in background, surviving terminal logout
ps aux | grep name Find a specific process by name Common signals Signal Meaning Can be caught?
SIGINT (Ctrl+C)
Interrupt — politely ask the program to stop Yes
SIGTERM (kill)
Terminate — request graceful shutdown (allows cleanup) Yes
SIGKILL (kill -9)
Force kill immediately, no cleanup possible No SIGSTOP / SIGCONT Pause / resume a process No / Yes SIGHUP Terminal hung up; often used to tell daemons to reload config Yes

“Always use kill -9 to stop a process — it's the most reliable.” Reach for kill (SIGTERM) first. SIGTERM lets the program shut down gracefully — flush buffers, close files, release locks. SIGKILL (-9) is instantaneous and uncatchable, so the program gets no chance to clean up, which can corrupt data or leave stale lock files. Use -9 only when a process is genuinely stuck and ignoring SIGTERM.

qas.q: What is the difference between kill and kill -9?
qas.a: Plain kill sends SIGTERM, a catchable request for graceful termination that lets the program clean up. kill -9 sends SIGKILL, which the kernel enforces immediately and cannot be caught or ignored — no cleanup happens. Prefer SIGTERM first; use SIGKILL only as a last resort.

qas.q: How would you find which process is listening on a specific port?
qas.a: Use ss -ltnp (or the older netstat -tulpn), or lsof -i :PORT. These show listening sockets along with the owning process ID and name.

qas.q: How do you run a long task so it keeps running after you log out?
qas.a: Run it with nohup and in the background (nohup ./task &), or use a terminal multiplexer like tmux/screen, or run it as a systemd service. nohup makes it ignore the SIGHUP sent when the terminal closes.

qas.q: How would you find the top memory-consuming processes?
qas.a: Use top/htop interactively (sort by memory), or ps aux --sort=-%mem | head to list the biggest memory consumers non-interactively.

qas.q: What's the difference between a foreground and a background process in a shell?
qas.a: A foreground process occupies the terminal until it finishes (you can't run other commands). A background process (started with &, or pushed back with bg) runs while freeing the terminal for other commands; jobs lists them and fg brings one back to the foreground.

blocks.text: Process and pipe questions are where interviewers check you can actually operate a Linux machine: tailing a live log, finding a runaway process, killing it gracefully, and composing a pipeline to analyse a file. Practice the 'top 5 IPs from a log' style pipeline until it's automatic — it's a favourite because it combines extraction, sorting, counting, and limiting in one fluent chain. CHAPTER 5B Part B — Software Engineering Fundamentals Git, process, testing, complexity, and how real software is built

Version control tracks changes to code over time, lets multiple people work on the same codebase without overwriting each other, and lets you undo mistakes or see who changed what and why. Git is the dominant version control system — being comfortable with it is assumed for every SDE role.

Git is like a video game with unlimited save points. Every commit is a save you can return to. Branches let you explore a risky side-quest (a new feature) on a separate save file without endangering your main progress — and if the experiment works, you merge it back into the main storyline; if it fails, you just discard that branch and your main save is untouched. The three areas (Git's mental model) Working Directory --git add--> Staging Area --git commit--> Repository (.git) (your edited files) (changes marked (permanent snapshots, ready to commit) the project history) git checkout / git restore <----------- pull changes back the other way Git's key distinction: staging lets you commit SOME changes, not necessarily all of them. This staging step is what trips up beginners coming from simpler tools: editing a file does not put it in the next commit. You must git add it to the staging area first. This lets you craft focused commits — staging only the changes that belong together, even if you edited several files. Essential commands Command What it does git init / git clone url Start a new repo / copy an existing one git status Show what's changed, staged, and untracked git add file (or .) Stage changes for the next commit git commit -m 'msg' Record staged changes as a snapshot with a message git push / git pull Upload commits to / download+merge from a remote git branch / git checkout -b name List branches / create and switch to a new branch git merge branch Combine another branch's history into the current one git log --oneline View commit history compactly git diff Show line-by-line changes not yet staged git stash Temporarily shelve uncommitted changes to switch context Branching, and merge vs rebase A branch is an independent line of development. You build a feature on its own branch, then integrate it back. There are two ways to integrate — and the difference is a very common interview question: git merge git rebase Combines branches with a new 'merge commit', preserving the actual branched history Replays your commits on top of the target branch, creating a linear history History shows the true, branched shape (with merge commits) History looks clean and linear, as if you'd worked sequentially Safe for shared/public branches Avoid on shared branches — it rewrites commit history MERGE (preserves branch shape): REBASE (linear history): A---B---C---M (main) A---B---C (main) \ / \ D---E (feature) D'--E' (feature replayed on top of C) M is a new merge commit. D' and E' are NEW commits with the same changes. Merge keeps the branching truth; rebase rewrites it into a straight line.

Merge preserves history; rebase rewrites it into a straight line. The golden rule: never rebase commits that you've already pushed to a shared branch — rewriting shared history forces everyone else's history out of sync. Rebase your own local work; merge shared work. Merge conflicts A merge conflict happens when two branches changed the same lines of the same file differently, so Git can't automatically decide which version wins. Git marks the conflicting region in the file with <<<<<<<, =======, and >>>>>>> markers; you edit the file to the desired final state, remove the markers, then git add and commit to resolve it. Conflicts are normal, not errors — they just need a human decision.

“Git and GitHub are the same thing.” Git is the version control tool that runs on your machine and works fully offline. GitHub (and GitLab, Bitbucket) is a hosting service that stores Git repositories online and adds collaboration features (pull requests, issues, CI). You can use Git with no GitHub account at all.

qas.q: What is the difference between git merge and git rebase?
qas.a: Merge combines two branches by creating a merge commit, preserving the true branched history. Rebase replays your branch's commits on top of another branch, producing a clean linear history but rewriting commit hashes. Never rebase commits already pushed to a shared branch, since it rewrites shared history.

qas.q: What is the difference between git fetch and git pull?
qas.a: git fetch downloads new commits from the remote but does NOT merge them into your working branch — it just updates your knowledge of the remote. git pull is essentially fetch followed by an automatic merge (or rebase) into your current branch.

qas.q: What is the staging area and why does Git have it?
qas.a: An intermediate area between your working directory and the repository where you mark which changes go into the next commit. It lets you craft focused commits by staging only related changes, rather than being forced to commit every modified file at once.

qas.q: How do you resolve a merge conflict?
qas.a: Open the conflicted file(s) — Git marks the competing versions with conflict markers. Edit the file to the desired final content, remove the markers, then git add the resolved file and commit. The conflict is a request for a human decision Git couldn't make.

qas.q: What's the difference between git reset and git revert?
qas.a: git revert creates a NEW commit that undoes a previous commit's changes — safe for shared history. git reset moves the branch pointer backward, potentially discarding commits — it rewrites history, so it's risky on shared branches but useful for cleaning up local work.

blocks.text: Git is assumed knowledge — gaps here stand out badly. The merge-vs-rebase and fetch-vs-pull distinctions are asked constantly. Beyond commands, interviewers value a sensible workflow answer: work on a feature branch, make small focused commits, open a pull request for review, and keep the main branch always deployable. Mentioning this unprompted signals real team experience.

The Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) is the overall process teams follow to build software — from understanding what's needed to shipping and maintaining it. The classic phases are: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance. Different methodologies arrange and repeat these phases differently. Waterfall vs Agile Waterfall
Agile
Sequential — finish each phase fully before the next
Iterative — build in small increments, repeating the cycle Requirements fixed upfront Requirements evolve based on feedback
Working software only near the end
Working software delivered every iteration (sprint) Hard to change course late
Designed to adapt to change continuously
Good when requirements are truly fixed/known Good for most modern product development

Waterfall is like building a house from a fully finalised blueprint — you can't easily move a wall once the foundation is poured, so everything must be decided upfront. Agile is like sculpting clay — you shape a rough form, step back, get feedback, and refine repeatedly, able to change direction at any stage as you learn what actually works. Agile in practice: Scrum Scrum is the most common Agile framework. Work is organised into fixed-length sprints (usually 1–4 weeks), each producing a potentially shippable increment. Scrum concept Meaning Sprint A fixed time-box (e.g. 2 weeks) of focused work Product Backlog The prioritised master list of everything the product might need Sprint Backlog The subset of work committed to for the current sprint Daily Standup A short daily sync: what I did, what I'll do, any blockers Sprint Review Demo the increment to stakeholders at sprint's end Retrospective The team reflects on how to improve their process next sprint Scrum Master Facilitates the process and removes blockers (not a 'boss') Product Owner Owns and prioritises the backlog, represents the customer

Waterfall = one big sequential pass; Agile = many small iterative loops. For Scrum, remember the rhythm: plan a sprint → daily standups → review the demo → retrospective → repeat. The whole point of Agile is delivering working software early and adapting to feedback rather than guessing everything upfront.

qas.q: What is the difference between Waterfall and Agile?
qas.a: Waterfall is sequential — each phase (requirements, design, build, test) completes before the next, with working software only near the end. Agile is iterative — software is built in small increments with frequent feedback, so requirements can evolve and value is delivered continuously.

qas.q: What happens in a daily standup?
qas.a: A short (≈15-min) daily team sync where each member briefly shares what they did yesterday, what they plan to do today, and any blockers. Its purpose is coordination and surfacing obstacles early, not detailed problem-solving (which happens separately).

qas.q: What is the difference between a Scrum Master and a Product Owner?
qas.a: The Product Owner owns and prioritises the backlog, representing the customer's interests (the 'what' and 'why'). The Scrum Master facilitates the Scrum process and removes impediments for the team (the 'how' of the process) — they're a facilitator, not a traditional manager.

qas.q: When might Waterfall actually be the better choice?
qas.a: When requirements are genuinely fixed, well-understood, and unlikely to change — e.g. certain regulated, safety-critical, or contractually-fixed projects where upfront specification and documentation are mandatory and mid-project change is undesirable.

blocks.text: Testing verifies that software behaves correctly, and crucially, protects against future changes breaking existing behaviour (regressions). Good tests let teams change code confidently — without them, every change is a gamble. Levels of testing Level Tests

Unit A single function/class in isolation Does calculateTax(100) return 18? Integration Multiple components working together Does the API correctly save to the DB? End-to-End (E2E) The whole system from the user's perspective Can a user sign up, log in, and checkout? The testing pyramid /\ /E2E\ few -- slow, expensive, brittle, but realistic /------\ / Integ. \ some /----------\ / Unit \ many -- fast, cheap, precise; the foundation /--------------\ Favour many fast unit tests at the base, fewer slow E2E tests at the top. The testing pyramid is the guideline that you should have many fast, cheap unit tests, fewer integration tests, and only a handful of slow, expensive end-to-end tests. The common anti-pattern (the 'ice cream cone') is the inverse — relying mostly on slow, brittle E2E tests — which makes the test suite slow and painful to maintain. Test-Driven Development (TDD) TDD flips the usual order: you write a failing test first, then write just enough code to make it pass, then refactor — the Red → Green → Refactor cycle. It forces you to clarify the requirement before coding and guarantees every piece of code has a test. Term Meaning Regression test A test ensuring a previously-fixed bug or feature doesn't break again Mock / stub A fake stand-in for a real dependency (e.g. a database) during a test Code coverage The % of code exercised by tests — useful but not a guarantee of quality CI (Continuous Integration) Automatically running tests on every commit/push

“100% code coverage means the code is fully tested and bug-free.” Coverage only measures which lines ran during tests — not whether the tests actually assert correct behaviour or cover the right edge cases. You can have 100% coverage with weak tests that catch nothing. Coverage is a useful signal of what's untested, not a proof of correctness.

qas.q: What is the difference between unit, integration, and end-to-end tests?
qas.a: Unit tests check a single component in isolation (fast, precise). Integration tests check that multiple components work together (e.g. code + database). E2E tests verify the whole system from the user's perspective (slow, realistic). The testing pyramid recommends many unit, fewer integration, and few E2E tests.

qas.q: What is Test-Driven Development?
qas.a: A practice where you write a failing test first, then write minimal code to pass it, then refactor — the Red-Green-Refactor cycle. It clarifies requirements before coding and ensures all code is covered by tests.

qas.q: Why is high code coverage not the same as good testing?
qas.a: Coverage only measures which lines executed during tests, not whether the tests meaningfully assert correct behaviour or cover important edge cases. Tests can run a line without verifying it's correct, so 100% coverage can still miss real bugs.

qas.q: What is a mock, and why use one?
qas.a: A fake stand-in for a real dependency (like a database, network call, or external API) used during testing. It lets you test a unit in isolation, control the dependency's behaviour, and avoid slow or unpredictable real systems — directly enabled by depending on abstractions (Dependency Inversion).

qas.q: What is continuous integration (CI)?
qas.a: The practice of automatically building and running the test suite on every commit/push to catch integration problems and regressions early, keeping the main branch in a consistently working, deployable state.

blocks.text: Testing questions probe whether you build software responsibly. Know the pyramid and be able to classify a given test. A very common behavioural-adjacent question is “how do you make sure your code works / doesn't break things?” — answer with unit tests, the pyramid, and CI. Mentioning that you'd add a regression test when fixing a bug is a strong, mature signal.

Big-O notation describes how an algorithm's running time (or memory) grows as the input size n grows — ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms, focusing on the dominant trend for large inputs. It's the single most important analytical tool in coding interviews: you'll be asked the complexity of almost every solution you write.

Big-O answers 'how does the effort scale as the problem gets bigger?' — not the exact time. Finding a name in a phone book by flipping page by page scales linearly (twice the pages, twice the work → O(n)). Finding it by repeatedly splitting the book in half scales logarithmically (double the pages, just one extra split → O(log n)). The strategy's growth rate is what Big-O captures, regardless of how fast your hands move. Common complexities, best to worst Big-O Name

n=1000 roughly O(1) Constant Array index access, hash lookup 1 O(log n) Logarithmic Binary search, balanced-tree ops ~10 O(n) Linear Single loop over the input 1,000 O(n log n) Linearithmic Efficient sorts (merge, heap, quicksort avg) ~10,000 O(n^2) Quadratic Nested loop over the input (bubble sort) 1,000,000 O(2^n) Exponential Naive recursive subsets / Fibonacci astronomically huge O(n!) Factorial Generating all permutations beyond astronomical operations ^ O(2^n) O(n^2) | / / | / / | / / | / / O(n log n) | / / / | // / O(n) | // / ____/ | /// / ____/ | _____///_____/____/______________ O(log n) | ________________/______________________________ O(1) +-------------------------------------------------> input size n Growth rates diverge dramatically as n grows -- the whole point of Big-O analysis. Deriving complexity (the rules)  Drop constants: O(2n) becomes O(n); O(n/2) becomes O(n). Big-O cares about growth rate, not exact counts.  Drop lower-order terms: O(n² + n) becomes O(n²) — for large n, the n² term dominates utterly.  Sequential code adds: a loop of O(n) followed by another O(n) loop is O(n) + O(n) = O(n).  Nested code multiplies: a loop inside a loop, each O(n), is O(n) × O(n) = O(n²).  Different inputs use different variables: two separate inputs give O(a + b), not O(n) — don't conflate them.

Why is a nested loop O(n²)? The inner loop runs n times for each of the outer loop's n iterations — so n × n = n² total operations. Doubling n roughly quadruples the work (2n × 2n = 4n²), which is the signature of quadratic growth. Time complexity of common data-structure operations Know these cold — interviewers expect instant recall: Structure Access Search Insert Delete Array O(1) O(n) O(n) O(n) Dynamic Array (end) O(1) O(n) O(1) amortized O(1) at end Linked List O(n) O(n) O(1) at head O(1) at head Hash Table — O(1) avg O(1) avg O(1) avg Balanced BST O(log n) O(log n) O(log n) O(log n) Heap (min/max) O(1) peek O(n) O(log n) O(log n) Stack / Queue — — O(1) O(1)

Hash table = O(1) average for search/insert/delete (the reason hashing is everywhere), but O(n) worst case if many keys collide into one bucket. Balanced BST = O(log n) guaranteed for all and keeps keys sorted (which hashing doesn't). That trade-off — average-case speed vs sorted/guaranteed bounds — is a frequent interview discussion. Big-O vs Big-Theta vs Big-Omega (brief) Notation Bounds Plain meaning Big-O (O) Upper bound Grows AT MOST this fast (worst case ceiling) Big-Omega (Ω) Lower bound Grows AT LEAST this fast (best case floor) Big-Theta (Θ) Tight bound Grows EXACTLY this fast (both bounds match)

“Big-O describes the average or actual running time.” Big-O is strictly an upper bound on growth rate (typically worst-case). In casual interview usage people say 'O(n log n)' to mean the tight/typical complexity, but technically a tight bound is Big-Theta (Θ). Also remember Big-O ignores constants — an O(n) algorithm isn't always faster than an O(n²) one for small n; it's about scaling behaviour as n grows large.

qas.q: What is Big-O notation, in your own words?
qas.a: A way to describe how an algorithm's resource usage (time or space) grows as the input size grows, ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms, so we can compare algorithms by their scaling behaviour for large inputs.

qas.q: What's the time complexity of searching in a hash table, and the catch?
qas.a: O(1) on average, because hashing jumps near-directly to the bucket. The catch is the worst case is O(n) if many keys collide into the same bucket (e.g. a bad hash function or adversarial input), degrading it to a linear scan.

qas.q: Why is the complexity of appending to a dynamic array O(1) amortized rather than O(1)?
qas.a: Most appends are O(1), but occasionally the array is full and must be resized — allocating a bigger array and copying all n elements (an O(n) operation). Averaged (amortized) over many appends, since resizing happens rarely and doubles capacity, the per-append cost works out to O(1).

qas.q: If an algorithm is O(n) and another is O(n^2), is the O(n) one always faster?
qas.a: Not for small inputs — Big-O ignores constant factors, so an O(n) algorithm with a large constant can be slower than an O(n^2) one for small n. Big-O describes scaling for large n; for small inputs, constants and real-world factors can dominate.

qas.q: What is the difference between time and space complexity?
qas.a: Time complexity measures how the number of operations grows with input size; space complexity measures how the additional memory used grows with input size. An algorithm can trade one for the other — e.g. caching/memoization uses more space to save time.

blocks.text: Big-O is non-negotiable for coding rounds — you'll state the complexity of essentially every solution. Practice deriving it out loud ("this loop is n, nested in another n, so n²") rather than guessing, and have the data-structure operations table memorised cold. A common follow-up is “can you do better?” — recognising when an O(n²) solution can become O(n log n) or O(n) (often via sorting or a hash map) is exactly the optimization instinct interviewers are testing.

System design asks how to build software that works reliably at scale — for millions of users, not just one. Even for entry-level roles, a basic grasp of these concepts is increasingly expected, and a dedicated handbook (your earlier System Design work) goes deeper. This section gives you the vocabulary and the core ideas. Vertical vs horizontal scaling Vertical scaling (scale up) Horizontal scaling (scale out)
Add more power (CPU/RAM) to ONE machine
Add MORE machines and distribute the load
Simple, but has a hard ceiling
Near-unlimited, but adds complexity (coordination)
Single point of failure
Fault-tolerant — others survive if one dies

Vertical scaling is hiring one super-strong worker and giving them a bigger desk and faster tools — easy, but there's a physical limit to how much one person can do, and if they're sick, all work stops. Horizontal scaling is hiring more workers — you can keep adding them almost indefinitely and the team survives if one is out, but now you need a manager to coordinate them (the added complexity). Load balancing A load balancer sits in front of multiple server instances and distributes incoming requests among them, so no single server is overwhelmed and the system stays available if one server fails. Common strategies: round-robin (rotate through servers in turn), least-connections (send to the least-busy server), and IP-hash (consistently route a given client to the same server). +----------> Server 1 Clients --> Load +----------> Server 2 Balancer +----------> Server 3 (distributes requests; reroutes around any failed server) One entry point, many backend servers -- the foundation of horizontal scaling. Caching A cache stores the results of expensive operations (a slow DB query, a computed page) in fast storage (usually memory) so repeat requests are served instantly without redoing the work. Caching is one of the highest-impact performance techniques — but it introduces the hard problem of cache invalidation: keeping cached data from going stale when the underlying data changes.

There's a famous saying: 'There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things.' Caching dramatically speeds up reads, but the moment the underlying data changes, your cached copy may be wrong — deciding when to invalidate or refresh it is the genuinely hard part. Caching layer

Browser cache Static assets cached on the user's device CDN Content cached on servers geographically near users Application cache In-memory stores like Redis or Memcached for query results/sessions Database cache The DB's own buffer of frequently-accessed data SQL vs NoSQL (system-design lens) SQL (relational) NoSQL Fixed schema, tables with relations Flexible/schema-less (document, key-value, graph, wide-column) Strong consistency, ACID transactions Often favours availability/scale over strict consistency Scales vertically more naturally Designed to scale horizontally across many nodes Great for complex queries/relationships Great for huge scale + simple access patterns The CAP theorem In a distributed system, the CAP theorem says you can guarantee at most two of these three during a network partition: Consistency (every read sees the latest write), Availability (every request gets a response), and Partition tolerance (the system keeps working despite network splits between nodes). Since network partitions are unavoidable in practice, real distributed systems must choose between favouring consistency (CP) or availability (AP) when one occurs. Consistency /\ / \ CP system / \ AP system (e.g. many / \ (e.g. many SQL/HBase) \ NoSQL: Cassandra) / \ Availability ---------- Partition Tolerance (you must keep P, so you pick C or A during a partition) During a network partition you must sacrifice either Consistency or Availability.

“CAP means you permanently pick two of the three and lose the third forever.” The trade-off only forces a choice during a network partition. When the network is healthy, a system can provide both consistency and availability. CAP is specifically about what gives way when nodes can't communicate — and since partitions will happen, you must design for either CP or AP behaviour during those moments.

qas.q: What is the difference between vertical and horizontal scaling?
qas.a: Vertical scaling adds more resources (CPU/RAM) to a single machine — simple but capped and a single point of failure. Horizontal scaling adds more machines and distributes load across them — nearly unlimited and fault-tolerant, but introduces coordination complexity. Modern large systems rely mostly on horizontal scaling.

qas.q: What does a load balancer do?
qas.a: It distributes incoming requests across multiple backend servers so none is overwhelmed, improving throughput and availability — if one server fails, traffic is rerouted to healthy ones. It's the entry point that makes horizontal scaling work.

qas.q: Why is caching powerful, and what's its main difficulty?
qas.a: Caching serves repeat requests from fast storage instead of redoing expensive work, hugely improving performance and reducing load. The main difficulty is cache invalidation — ensuring cached data doesn't serve stale results after the underlying data changes.

qas.q: Explain the CAP theorem.
qas.a: In a distributed system during a network partition, you can guarantee at most two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. Since partitions are unavoidable, you effectively choose between staying consistent (rejecting some requests) or staying available (risking stale reads) when nodes can't communicate.

qas.q: When would you choose NoSQL over SQL?
qas.a: When you need to scale horizontally to very large data/throughput, have flexible or evolving schemas, and your access patterns are relatively simple — and when you can tolerate eventual (rather than strict) consistency. SQL remains better for complex relationships, multi-row transactions, and strong consistency needs.

blocks.text: For entry-level roles, system design questions stay conceptual — you won't design Twitter from scratch, but you should confidently explain scaling, load balancing, caching, and SQL-vs-NoSQL trade-offs, and name the CAP theorem. The meta-skill is reasoning about trade-offs out loud rather than giving one 'right' answer — interviewers want to hear you weigh consistency against availability, or speed against complexity, like an engineer who's thought about real systems. CHAPTER 5C Interview Question Bank Curated, high-yield Linux & Software Engineering questions for SDE rounds Each section above already embedded its own questions; below are additional rapid-fire and cross-cutting ones across both Linux and software engineering. Linux & Command Line

qas.q: What's the difference between a hard link and a symbolic (soft) link?
qas.a: A hard link is another directory entry pointing to the same underlying inode (same data); the file persists until all hard links are removed. A symbolic link is a small file containing a path to the target; it breaks if the target is deleted/moved and can cross filesystems, which hard links cannot.

qas.q: How would you find all files larger than 100MB under /var?
qas.a: find /var -type f -size +100M — the find command filters by type and size. You can pipe to ls or sort for more detail, e.g. find /var -type f -size +100M -exec ls -lh {} +.

qas.q: What does the command 'chmod +x script.sh' do?
qas.a: Adds execute permission to script.sh for the relevant categories, making it runnable as a program (e.g. ./script.sh). Without execute permission, the shell refuses to run it directly.

qas.q: What's the difference between > and >> in the shell?
qas.a: > redirects output to a file, OVERWRITING its existing contents. >> redirects output but APPENDS to the end of the file, preserving what's already there.

qas.q: What is an environment variable?
qas.a: Give an example. A named value available to processes in the shell environment, used for configuration. Examples: PATH (where to find executables), HOME (the user's home directory), or a custom one like DATABASE_URL. Set with export NAME=value. Git & Workflow

qas.q: What is a pull request (merge request) and why is it used?
qas.a: A proposal to merge one branch into another, providing a place for code review, discussion, and automated checks (CI) before the changes are integrated. It's the core of collaborative, reviewed development on platforms like GitHub/GitLab.

qas.q: What does git stash do?
qas.a: Temporarily shelves your uncommitted changes (returning your working directory to a clean state) so you can switch context — e.g. to fix an urgent bug on another branch — then reapply them later with git stash pop.

qas.q: What is a .gitignore file?
qas.a: A file listing patterns for files/directories Git should NOT track — build artifacts, dependencies (node_modules), secrets, IDE config. It keeps the repository clean and avoids committing things that shouldn't be versioned.

qas.q: What's the difference between git reset --soft and --hard?
qas.a: --soft moves the branch pointer back but KEEPS your changes staged (safe). --hard moves the pointer back AND discards all changes in the working directory and staging area (destructive — work is lost). Use --hard with great caution. Engineering Practice & Design

qas.q: What is technical debt?
qas.a: The implied future cost of choosing a quick/easy solution now over a better but slower one — like taking a loan. Some is acceptable to ship fast, but unmanaged technical debt compounds, slowing all future development until it's 'repaid' through refactoring.

qas.q: What is the DRY principle?
qas.a: Don't Repeat Yourself — every piece of knowledge/logic should have a single, authoritative representation. Duplicated logic means a change must be made in multiple places, risking inconsistency. (Its counterpart caution: don't over-abstract prematurely just to avoid any repetition.)

qas.q: What is the difference between an interpreted and a compiled language?
qas.a: A compiled language (C++, Rust) is translated to machine code ahead of time by a compiler, then run directly — fast execution. An interpreted language (Python, JavaScript) is executed line-by-line by an interpreter at runtime — more portable and flexible but generally slower. Many languages blur this (e.g. JIT compilation).

qas.q: What is an API?
qas.a: An Application Programming Interface — a defined contract by which one piece of software can request services or data from another, hiding the internal implementation. A web API (often REST/HTTP) lets a client request data from a server through well-defined endpoints without knowing how the server works internally.

qas.q: What is idempotency and why does it matter in API design?
qas.a: An operation is idempotent if performing it multiple times has the same effect as performing it once. It matters because networks are unreliable — if a client retries a request after a timeout, an idempotent operation (like GET, PUT, DELETE) won't cause duplicate side effects, whereas a non-idempotent one (POST) might create duplicates. A FINAL WORD ON HOW TO USE THIS SERIES These five books give you the content. But interviews don't test what you've read — they test what you can recall and explain under pressure. The single highest-value way to use this series is the same for every book: read a section, close it, and explain the idea out loud as if teaching someone. Then drill the interview-question and placement-perspective boxes as flashcards. Pair this theory with consistent hands-on practice — timed problem-solving and mock interviews — and you'll walk in genuinely prepared, not just well-read. Good luck.
